She crouched at the edge of the blood with her arm at full stretch, face turned away like she was feeding something at the zoo, and held it there for thirty seconds. "It's misting," she whispered. "Oh my god, it's misting—" It was misting because all three of us were leaning over it breathing. We established this, after a very bad fifteen seconds, by Pickle holding the mirror under her own armpit, where it also misted. The science portion of the evening was not going well. "Pulse, then," said Pickle. "Wrist or neck. Neck's better but it's —" she looked at Keith's neck, which even face-down had a quality of being Keith's neck — "wrist is fine, wrist is completely accepted." Nobody moved. "I'm logging that I suggested it," said Pickle. So I did it, in the end, because Saff couldn't — and I mean *couldn't*, I watched her try, watched her get one knee down and then stand straight back up like the floor was hot — and because somebody had to, and because being the one who does the thing nobody else can do is, it turns out, a role I'll accept even when the thing is holding Keith Doyle's enormous wrist in a damp marigold. His arm was heavy in a way I wasn't ready for. Not stiff — heavy, like the rest of him had stopped vouching for it. I pressed where you press. I felt a pulse straight away, a strong one, going like anything, and said so, and Pickle said, gently, like a midwife with bad news, that statistically that would be my own thumb. You can't use your thumb. Everyone knows you can't use your thumb, the same way I knew it the second she said it. I tried again with two fingers. And here is where I have to be honest about what we found, which is: I don't know. There was something, or there was nothing, or there was my own heart going in my fingertips, which by that point was going hard enough to count as a regional event. Thirty seconds is a very long time to kneel in your best friend's hallway holding a man's wrist while a telly sells sofas. I said I couldn't feel anything. I said it in a small voice, with a question mark on it, and the question mark got lost somewhere between me and the others, and by the time the sentence reached them it had set. We had a meeting about it at the foot of the stairs. Pickle got her phone out and typed *how to tell if someone is dea* and then deleted it and went to incognito mode first, and the fact that she went to incognito mode told you what she'd already decided, what we'd all already decided. The advice, when it came, was to look for breathing, which took us back to the mirror problem, or to do CPR, which took us to YouTube. The CPR video had an advert on it. I want this written down somewhere official: a man died or didn't die in that hallway while three girls stood over him watching an advert for car insurance that could not be skipped for five seconds, and then could be skipped, and Pickle's thumb went at the screen like a woodpecker. The video itself featured a calm man kneeling over a dummy on a lawn, in sunshine, saying you should push hard and fast to the beat of "Stayin' Alive," and all three of us looked from the screen to Keith, to the blood, to the angle of his arm, and Pickle turned it off without anyone telling her to. "He's gone," said Saff, to the wall. "Hasn't he. We're not — he's gone." And we voted on it. Not with hands. With silence — which is how the worst votes are taken, I think now; nobody says aye, everybody just fails to say no, and afterwards everyone remembers being privately against it. I remember being privately against it. I'd bet the other two remember the same. You can't vote on whether a man's dead. We did, though. It was unanimous. That's when his phone went off. It was in the lounge, on the arm of his chair, and it buzzed and lit up and all three of us went off the floor like the same animal. Saff got to it first. She stood in the lounge doorway with it, in the light of his telly, reading, and her face did nothing at all, which is the face I've learned to fear. "Gaz," she said. "From — work, it says. *Mate where were you Thursday, D's saying don't bother coming back, sort it out.*" She scrolled. The phone buzzed again in her hand and she flinched and kept reading. "There's loads. There's — Thursday, and last Tuesday, and." She stopped. A little grey notification slid down over the top of the screen, a banking one, the kind that tells you what's left, and I was too far away to read the number but not too far to see her read it. "Saff?" "He's been getting up at half seven," she said, in a voice from somewhere else. "Every morning. Lanyard and everything. He does his sandwiches." She looked at the lanyard on the hook, KEITH DOYLE and the barcode and the face. "Five weeks. There's a text from Mum on here from today, from teatime, and I'm not—" she put the phone face-down on the arm of the chair, exactly where it had been, lining it up with the seam of the fabric, taking real care over it. "I'm not reading you that one." The fridge hummed. In my head, without permission, the gas card moved across the kitchen, the letter under the lemon magnet rotated slightly, teatime rearranged itself — *something* had happened at teatime, the text said so, Saff's face said so — and none of it, I want to be clear, made the man on the floor deserve the floor. It just made him smaller. He'd been pretending to go to work. He did his sandwiches. The biggest thing in the house, the weather itself, sitting in a Wetherspoons by the retail park every day going through her mum's account, and now he was face-down in the hall and even his phone was only full of small, bad, embarrassing things. "He can't be found here," said Saff. It didn't follow from anything. I've gone back over it and it doesn't follow — there were about six sentences missing between that phone and that conclusion, and she didn't say any of them, and Pickle clicked her biro twice, and neither of us asked.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of darkly comic YA fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me work through the key elements. VOICE AND NARRATION: Wren's retrospective, slightly defensive voice is consistently maintained throughout. The narration has the quality of someone reconstructing events with a mixture of self-awareness and self-justification that the plan called for. Lines like "the question mark got lost somewhere between me and the others, and by the time the sentence reached them it had set" demonstrate a narrator who understands exactly what she did and is choosing how to frame it. The "we voted" passage is the chapter's moral center and it lands precisely: "You can't vote on whether a man's dead. We did, though. It was unanimous." This is the kind of writing that earns its darkness. COMEDY: The comedy is genuinely situational rather than epigrammatic, as planned. The mirror misting from their own breath, the thumb-pulse problem, the unskippable car insurance ad — these are funny because they're logically inevitable, not because someone made a quip. The "Stayin' Alive" CPR detail is perfectly deployed: it's real, it's absurd, and the three girls looking from the phone to Keith to the blood and Pickle turning it off without being told is a small masterpiece of comic-to-dark transition. The jokes don't announce themselves. CHARACTER EXECUTION: All three characters are consistent with their profiles. Pickle's procedure, her incognito mode instinct (which Wren correctly reads as pre-decided conclusion), her gentle correction about the thumb — all of this is exactly Pickle. Saff's face "doing nothing at all, which is the face I've learned to fear" is economical and true to the profile. The detail of Saff lining up the phone with the seam of the fabric after reading the banking notification is excellent — it's the kind of precise, controlled gesture that reveals someone managing themselves under enormous pressure. THE PHONE REVELATION: The plan called for Keith's phone to reveal he'd lost his job and been draining the account, and for this to "justify nothing; it just makes him smaller." The chapter executes this beautifully. "The biggest thing in the house, the weather itself, sitting in a Wetherspoons by the retail park every day going through her mum's account" — this is the chapter's best sentence, and it earns its slight lyricism because it's doing real work: collapsing Keith's menace into something pathetic without excusing anything. The "he did his sandwiches" detail is devastating in its smallness. THE ENDING: "He can't be found here" followed by the observation that it doesn't follow from anything, that six sentences are missing, and that nobody asks — this is exactly the kind of flat, unresolved ending the plan called for. It refuses to explain the leap, which is both true to Saff's character (she manages people, doles out truth in instalments) and true to the story's refusal to provide clean causation. MINOR CRITICISMS: The "regional event" metaphor for Wren's heartbeat is slightly strained — it's reaching for wit and lands a bit awkwardly. The phrase "like a midwife with bad news" for Pickle's correction about the thumb is a touch overwritten for this voice, though it's borderline. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes, and the "telly sells sofas" detail, while good, is the kind of ambient specificity that could be trimmed. These are small complaints against a chapter that is otherwise executing at a high level. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The mirror scene, the incognito mode, the CPR video with the unskippable ad, the thumb-pulse problem, the "voting" on death, Keith's phone revealing the job loss and account draining, the banking notification Saff won't share, the chapter ending with "he can't be found here" and nobody asking her to explain — all present and executed. The plan's instruction that "it justifies nothing; it just makes him smaller" is honored in the prose. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. Saff's reading of the texts, her "I'm not reading you that one," her final "He can't be found here" — these are all in character and do real work. Pickle's "I'm logging that I suggested it" is a small, perfect character beat. No dialogue feels expository or false. OVERALL: This is a chapter that knows what it's doing and does it. The comedy and darkness are genuinely integrated rather than alternating. The moral complexity is present without being announced. The voice is sustained and distinctive. It's not quite a masterpiece — there are small moments of overreach — but it's very strong work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five — Crisps We didn't move him straight away, because at quarter to two in the morning Pickle said, in a wobbly voice, that she felt weird, and we did an audit and established that none of us had eaten since tea. There's no good way to say what happened next, so: we sat on the stairs, the three of us, two feet from Keith, and ate Denny's variety-pack crisps. They were on top of the fridge, the multipack, raided down to the dregs — two cheese and onion, a prawn cocktail, a ready salted nobody fought me for. We sat in a row on the bottom steps like the world's worst school photo and ate them, and you could hear every crunch, because the telly had finally gone to one of those quiet bits between adverts, and the crunching was enormous, cathedral-sized, and nobody stopped. And we talked about mocks. That's the bit I'd leave out if I were allowed to leave bits out. Pickle was worried about the maths paper, the second one, the calculator one, and Saff said the calculator one's the easy one, and Pickle explained why that was a myth, with statistics, and I said I'd revised nothing, which was a lie, because I'd revised loads, because that's another thing I do. We talked about whether Pickle's mum would notice the marigolds were gone, and decided she would, because Pickle's mum notices everything, and Pickle would have to say she'd taken them to school for a thing, and we workshopped the thing. We did not talk about Tyler. I had three points and a conclusion about Tyler and they were still in my coat, with the phone, going off about once a paragraph in my head. Then Saff did an impression of the calm CPR man — *push hard and fast to the beat of Stayin' Alive* — except she did it in PCSO-voice, official and sad, and it wasn't funny. I need to be clear that it wasn't funny. And I laughed. It came out of me sideways, through my nose, the way it does when it's been queueing, and then Pickle went, and then Saff went, and then we were all three of us on the stairs absolutely gone, hysterical, eyes streaming, doing that silent shaking laughter that hurts your ribs, two feet from a dead man, at his crisps, in his hallway, with his blood going dry on the floor — and I caught us in the dark of the telly screen, this little reflected huddle of shaking girls, and the laugh curdled in me all at once like milk. Saff stopped a second after I did. Pickle took longest, wound down in little aftershocks, going *sorry, sorry,* and then it was very, very quiet, and the ready salted sat in my stomach like a verdict. "I'll check the alley," said Pickle, standing up too fast. "For the — to see if it's clear. For later." And she went out the back way with her head-torch on, glad of the job, and the kitchen door clicked, and it was just me and Saff and Keith. Saff went to the worktop. There's a spot where the laminate's lifting at the edge, by the kettle, has been for years, and she stood there and got her thumbnail under it. Pick, pick. "He grabbed Denny," she said. I didn't say anything. I'd learned that much by then. "At tea. He spilled his squash — Denny, I mean — and it went near the remote and Keith just." Pick. "He had him by the arm before I even. There's a mark. I checked when he was in the bath, there's a proper mark, like finger — like fingers." Pick, pick. "And Mum was at work and I didn't text her because what's she going to do on a corridor in the home at six o'clock, worry? So I did the thing, I did normal voice, I did *let's get you to bed, mate*, and then him and me were upstairs after, on the landing, and I told him if he ever touches Denny again, and he did the laugh. He does this laugh." She stopped picking. "And I pushed him." The kettle was right there. I remember wanting, insanely, to put it on. Make it a kitchen again. "I pushed him and he went down like — he went down. And I watched him land, Wren, I stood at the top and watched the whole, all of it, and you want to know the thing?" She turned around and her face was the switched-off one, which is how I knew we were at the true bit. "I felt nothing. Not nothing like shock. Nothing like — done. Like when you finally send a text you've been writing for ages. That's what I'm." She pressed her sleeve against her eyes once, hard, and came back from wherever she'd been, and her voice changed key entirely. "He slipped. Anyway. He was drunk and it's stairs and I wasn't even near him, I was in the bathroom. That's — anyway. It doesn't matter, does it." "Saff—" "It doesn't *matter*, Wren, because either way he's at the bottom, so what is there to —" and then, vicious, out of nowhere, the sarcasm that means it's the worst: "Sorry, did you want it notarised? Should we wait for Pickle, get it in the log?" I hadn't asked her anything. I want that on the record. I hadn't said a word, and she was furious at me for the question I hadn't asked, and the question sat there in the kitchen anyway with its coat on, not leaving. And what I said back — the sum total of what I found, in the moment my best friend handed me the heaviest thing she owned — was: "Okay." Okay. That's what I had. Three points and a conclusion for Tyler, and *okay* for this. The back door went and Pickle leaned in, torch beam swinging across the ceiling. "Alley's clear. There's a fox by the garages but he's not — he's minding his own business." Pause. Click of the biro. "Clear for what, though? What's the actual—" "The bin," said Saff. She said it the way she says the gas card things, flat and operational, already moving towards the back door. "Green one. Recycling's not till Thursday, it's basically empty. We take him to his flat — it's three streets, it's Maitland Road, it's nothing — and he's had a fall on his own stairs, on his own, like he lives. Like he deserves." I could have said no then. I've gone over it. That was the gap in the fence, right there, and what I did instead was hold the back door open while Saff went round the side of the house in the dark, and between us we walked the green wheelie bin up the path, and it was lighter than I expected and the lid banged once, and the left wheel had a squeak in it. Every third turn. Squeak. Squeak. I counted without deciding to. That's the detail my brain chose to keep from the worst night of my life: every third rotation, regular as anything, like something asking the same small question over and over and not getting an answer.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of darkly comic YA fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through it carefully. The opening dead-air scene is handled with real confidence. The decision to have the girls eat crisps two feet from a body and talk about mocks is exactly the kind of situational comedy the plan called for, and it lands because it's earned through specificity: the calculator paper debate, workshopping Pickle's excuse for the marigolds, the ready salted "nobody fought me for." The comedy doesn't announce itself; it emerges from the texture of three teenagers being teenagers in an impossible situation. The laughter scene is the chapter's centrepiece and it works. The escalation from Saff's not-funny impression to the hysterical group laughter to the curdling is handled with precision. "I caught us in the dark of the telly screen, this little reflected huddle of shaking girls" is a strong image that earns its place — it's not decorative, it's the moment Wren's perspective shifts. The milk simile ("curdled in me all at once like milk") is slightly on-the-nose but functional. "The ready salted sat in my stomach like a verdict" is the chapter's one moment of slightly forced poetry — it's trying a bit hard — but it's not egregious. Saff's confession is the chapter's most important passage and it's handled with real skill. The fragmented, self-contradicting delivery is exactly right: "He slipped. Anyway. He was drunk and it's stairs and I wasn't even near him, I was in the bathroom." The plan called for the confession to be given in pieces and never resolved, and the chapter delivers this. The detail about feeling "done" — "like when you finally send a text you've been writing for ages" — is the chapter's best line, genuinely surprising and emotionally precise. It tells us more about Saff's interior life than any amount of backstory. Wren's response of "Okay" is perfect. The self-aware narration around it ("Three points and a conclusion for Tyler, and okay for this") is exactly the voice established in the character profiles — retrospective, slightly defensive, catching itself. This is the chapter's emotional core and it doesn't overplay it. The pick-pick-pick laminate detail for Saff is consistent with her character profile and used well here. Denny's absence from this chapter is notable — he appeared in the plan's chapter 3 and will appear in chapter 6, so his absence here is appropriate. The ending with the wheelie bin and the squeaking wheel is strong. "Every third rotation, regular as anything, like something asking the same small question over and over and not getting an answer" — this is the chapter's most extended metaphor and it mostly works, though "like something asking the same small question" is slightly vague. The plan called for ending "mid-gesture" with Wren noticing the squeak, and the chapter delivers this while adding the counting detail that characterizes Wren's stress response. Pickle's role in this chapter is appropriately reduced — she's the one who goes to check the alley, giving Saff and Wren their private moment. Her "Clear for what, though?" is a good functional line. The chapter follows its plan closely: the dead-air crisp scene, the hysterical laughter that curdles, Pickle going to check the alley, Saff's fragmented confession, the wheelie bin plan, the mid-gesture ending. The plan's note about "the gap between podcast forensics and a real hallway" is less present here than in earlier chapters, but that's appropriate given the chapter's emotional pivot. Weaknesses: The "verdict" simile is the one moment of purple prose. The narration occasionally over-explains its own effects ("I need to be clear that it wasn't funny"). Saff's vicious sarcasm about the notary and the log is slightly too articulate for the moment — it's a good line but it feels slightly written. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes, though the pacing is generally well-controlled. The dialogue is strong throughout — naturalistic, character-specific, doing emotional work without announcing it. The chapter is coherent, emotionally engaging, and consistent with the established characters. It earns its darkness through specificity rather than inflation. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six — Brian Getting Keith into the bin took forty minutes and I'm not going to be funny about it, because it wasn't. It was the worst forty minutes of my life and it was made of logistics. You can't lift fourteen stone. That's the headline. Three girls who between them cannot do a pull-up cannot lift fourteen stone of man, and every plan we made dissolved on contact with how heavy a person is. We got him onto the duvet — Saff's own duvet, off her own bed, and she didn't hesitate and I couldn't look at her not hesitating — and dragged him down the hall in stages, a foot at a time, all three of us hauling like a tug-of-war team, and the duvet made it possible and also made it worse, because it made him luggage. At the back step his shoe came off, the half-off trainer, finally, and the noise it made on the lino was small and ordinary and I had to go and look at the garden fence for a minute. The bin went on its side in the end. That was Pickle's contribution, the only piece of podcast knowledge that survived contact with reality: don't lift the man to the bin, lay the bin down to the man. We walked him in, is the truthful verb, rolled and walked and apologised — all three of us, out loud, *sorry, sorry, sorry,* to a man we were posting into a recycling bin — and then tipped the bin upright with him in it, which took all of us and a sound from the bin I will hear forever. Pickle put cling film over him, "for fibres," and none of us asked for whom the fibres mattered, and the lid wouldn't quite shut, sat proud by an inch, and we stood in the dark garden breathing. "Is Keith going in the bin because he's rubbish?" Denny. At the back door. School jumper, pyjamas, completely still, been there an unknown length of time, his whole face doing its watching. Nobody answered. Here's the thing — nobody answered, and Denny nodded slowly like that *was* the answer, like silence was a yes he'd suspected for some time, and filed it. Saff went over and crouched and did the Denny setting and the words *secret* and *helping* and *back to bed, mate*, and Denny heard her out politely and said: "I'm coming. I'll be lookout. Foxes can hear a watch ticking from forty metres." And we let him. I want that recorded with everything else. It was nearly three in the morning and we let a seven-year-old come, because putting him back to bed had a hundred per cent failure rate and because — this is the true reason — Saff wasn't leaving him in that house alone, not tonight, not with the file and the letter and the everything, and none of us said any of that. Pickle gave him her head-torch. He wore it round his neck like a medal. Three streets to Maitland Road. The first one was fine — fine meaning silent and endless, the bin rumbling on the tarmac, the left wheel doing its squeak every third turn, Denny walking point with the torch off, "saving it." It's downhill to the underpass and uphill after, and it was on the uphill, by the garages, that the hi-vis came round the corner. "Evening! Or — morning, is it, technically." PCSO Brian Tunstall stood under the streetlight with his thumbs in his stab vest like a man leaning on a fence that wasn't there, and he was *pleased*. That was the thing you clocked first and it was the worst possible thing he could have been. Pleased to see us. Four kids and a wheelie bin at 3 a.m., and Brian's face said: company. "Bit late for bins," said Brian. "Eco-project," said Pickle, instantly, beautifully. "For school. We're auditing the green stream. It's a — we weigh a sample bin and log the contamination levels." "Contamination," said Brian, nodding slowly. "See, that's the word, isn't it. I said this to Carol — green stream, black stream, food caddy. In my day you put your rubbish out and that was your lot." He settled. You could see it happen, a man lowering himself into a conversation like a hot bath. "Whereabouts is this for, then, the high school? My nephew went there. He's in Australia now. Scaffolding." I have been frightened in my life. I have never been frightened like that: motionless, polite fear, four feet of pavement between a Police Community Support Officer and a bin with a man in it, the lid sat proud by an inch, while Brian told us about scaffolding. He talked about his shift, which was quiet, "which you want, but also, you know." He talked about the caravan, which was his ex-wife's now, in Mablethorpe, "which is fine." Denny stared up at him with his whole face and Brian said kids today were glued to screens and it was nice to see one out and about, and a fox screamed from over by the garages and all four of us went off the ground an inch and Brian didn't notice because he was getting his notebook out. "I'll stick you in the log," he said, warmly, like he was buying us all chips. "Names? Just for the log. Anything happens overnight, see, you're accounted for. It's how it works." And we gave them. Real ones. There's a version of that night where one of us is quick enough to lie, and we'd just watched Pickle invent an entire municipal auditing scheme without blinking, but a name is different, a name has your mum on the end of it — and Brian wrote us down, *Priya, Saffron, Wren, and—?* "Dennis," said Denny, who'd have given a blood sample. Brian wrote, breathing audibly, licking his thumb for the page. The lid was eight inches from his elbow. "Righto," he said, and put it away, and didn't leave. He came back twice — once to recommend hi-vis for the bikes, once to add something about the caravan — and then his radio did a noise about somewhere else and he ambled off towards it, and was gone. We stood on the hill. Nobody said anything for a long time. "His flat's got a shared front door," said Saff, looking down towards Maitland Road, and her voice had stopped being operational. "Buzzer entry. There's a man on the ground floor who smokes in the doorway at all hours, Mum's mentioned it, and there's a — I didn't think about the door. I planned everything and I didn't think about his actual door." She looked at the bin. At Denny, torch round his neck, swaying on his feet now, three in the morning finally landing on him. At the hill. "Take him back," she said. "Wren. Take him back." So we did. Downhill the bin pulled at our arms, wanting to go faster than the squeak, and Denny rode on the front of it the last stretch, feet on the axle, holding the lid down with both hands like it was the best go-kart of his life, and over the rooftops the sky on one side had started doing something I refused to call getting light. Squeak. Squeak. Squeak. Nobody spoke. Saff's mum's shift ended at six, and we had achieved, in total, one logged sighting of all four of our names, a quarter-mile from home, with the bin.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of darkly comic YA fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me work through the key elements. The logistics-over-jokes approach promised in the plan is delivered faithfully. The opening declaration — "I'm not going to be funny about it, because it wasn't" — is a smart piece of voice work that immediately signals the chapter's tonal register. The description of getting Keith into the bin is genuinely harrowing in its mundanity: the duvet as luggage, the apologising out loud, the shoe making "a small and ordinary" noise. These details are earned rather than performed. Denny's appearance and his question — "Is Keith going in the bin because he's rubbish?" — lands exactly as planned. The silence-as-answer beat is beautifully handled, and the narration's refusal to editorialize ("filed it") is exactly right. The fox-watch detail is deployed naturally. His riding the bin home at the end is the chapter's best image: genuinely funny, genuinely sad, and doing real character work simultaneously. Brian is calibrated precisely as specified: not stupid, not quirky, just lonely and slow to leave. The scaffolding-nephew-Australia-caravan accumulation is pitch-perfect. The detail of the lid being "eight inches from his elbow" while he writes names is the chapter's most effective piece of sustained dread. The names being given truthfully — "a name has your mum on the end of it" — is a sharp psychological observation that feels true rather than convenient. Wren's voice is consistent throughout: the retrospective framing, the self-aware parenthetical asides, the way she notices the squeak rather than the enormity. "I have never been frightened like that: motionless, polite fear" is clean and effective without being overwrought. The ending is deliberately flat as planned — the accounting of what they've achieved ("one logged sighting of all four of our names, a quarter-mile from home, with the bin") is a perfect comic-deflating summary that also carries genuine weight. Some minor criticisms: "a sound from the bin I will hear forever" edges toward the kind of lyrical inflation the plan explicitly wanted to avoid, though it's a single instance and not egregious. The phrase "the sky on one side had started doing something I refused to call getting light" is clever but slightly self-conscious. The Saff speech about the buzzer and the shared front door is the chapter's weakest moment — it's slightly too articulate for the character as profiled, who manages rather than discloses, though it serves the plot necessity of explaining the plan's collapse. The dialogue is sparse and functional throughout, which is correct. Pickle's "Eco-project" improvisation is given its due without being over-praised. Brian's voice is consistent with his profile. Denny's lines are exactly right. The chapter follows its plan almost point for point: the logistics narration, Denny's question and the silence-answer, the head-torch medal, Brian's loneliness and notebook, the names given truthfully, the plan collapsing at the buzzer, the flat ending with Denny on the bin. The squeak detail from Chapter 5's ending is carried forward correctly. The comedy is situational rather than epigrammatic as specified. The darkness is well-earned. The chapter does not reach for resolution it hasn't earned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven — Lazarus Back in the kitchen it was ten to five and the window over the sink had gone from black to a grey I couldn't argue with. The bin stood on the patio, right up against the glass of the back door, lid proud by its inch. Denny had folded up on the sofa in the lounge mid-sentence, something about foxes, and slept like the dead — like the genuinely, properly asleep, I mean. Words go strange at 5 a.m. Pickle sat at the table with the EVIDENCE notebook shut in front of her, not clicking the biro, which was somehow worse. Saff stood at the worktop. Nobody had put a light on. We were three grey shapes in a grey kitchen and the truth was arriving with the morning at the same speed and there was no version left. That was the maths I was doing. Mum home at six. Brian's notebook. A duvet with blood on it. There was no story now, no flat on Maitland Road, no fall-on-his-own-stairs — every option had been spent, and what was left was the first option, the original one, the one I'd had my thumb on at half twelve. So I took my phone out and dialled a nine. Then the second nine. "What are you doing." Saff didn't shout it. It came out flat and I turned around with the phone in my hand, screen up, two nines on it, and we looked at each other across her kitchen. "It's five in the morning," I said. "I'm doing what I should've—" "Put it down." "Saff, there's nothing left. There's no plan. Your mum walks in in an hour either way, the only thing we get to choose now is whether we're the ones who—" "*We*," she said, and there it was, the vicious key, the one that means she's drowning. "That's nice, *we*. You know what's mad? You get to go home after this. Whatever happens — that's the bit you keep skipping — you put your bike back against your wall and your mum does you beans on toast and asks how Saff's doing, and you'll say *fine*, because you're brilliant at that, you're actually brilliant at it, I watched you do it to that policeman like it was nothing — and I get the file. Me and Denny get the file. You're not calling anyone, you're *donating* a phone call, and then you're going home." It was true. That's what I want on the record about that sentence: every word of it was true, and I stood there with two nines on my screen and what came out of me, from somewhere I keep the worst things, was: "At least my mum's boyfriend's not in the bin because I—" I stopped it. Most of it. There are about four words that never made it out and both of us heard them anyway, every one, and Saff's face went through something fast and then switched off completely, and the kitchen was so quiet I could hear the fridge and the grey light and the four words going round the room on their own. "Saff—" "Don't." And into that silence, from the patio, the bin groaned. Not the wind. Not the lid settling. A long, low, *upholstered* groan, a sound with a man in it, and then the whole bin rocked once on its wheels and the lid lifted and what came up out of the recycling, grey-faced, with blood dried black into one eyebrow and cling film stuck to his shoe, was Keith. I'd like to report what I did in that moment. I have no information. There's a gap in the record between the lid going up and the three of us pressed against the far wall by the hall door, and in the gap somebody made a noise like a kettle, and I genuinely don't know which of us it was. Keith got an arm over the rim. He looked at the garden, at the grey sky, at the kitchen window with three girls in it, and you could watch it assemble behind his eyes — slowly, with the concussion dragging at it — the bin, the cling film, the night. He came in through the back door because of course it wasn't locked, we'd been going in and out with a body all night, and he stood holding the doorframe, filling it, swaying, one shoe, and the worst thing was how quiet he was. "Okay," said Keith. "Okay. Let's all be sensible." Nobody moved. "I had a fall." He said it carefully, laying it down like flooring. "I had a fall, on the stairs, and you lot panicked. Kids panic. No harm done." He touched his eyebrow, looked at his fingers. "And when Lisa gets home, that's what happened, because think about — let's think about what the *other* version sounds like. Out loud. Three girls put a man in a bin. Who does that sound bad for, in an office, written down? With your family's — circumstances." He nodded along with himself, gaining ground, the weather system reassembling out of nothing, and I felt it working, that's the thing I have to be honest about, I felt the room rearranging itself around him the way the house had, eight months of it happening in fast-forward — "and the little lad's asleep, no need for him in any of this, and you, what's-your-name, Priya, your mum and dad have got the chemist's, haven't they, they'd want this sensible too — see, you're nodding—" "You grabbed my arm." Denny was in the hall doorway. Sleep-creased, jumper twisted, torch still round his neck, completely still. He wasn't dramatic about it. He said it the way he says the fox facts, as information the room appeared to be missing. "At tea. 'Cause of the squash. And it hurt and there's a mark, look—" he pushed the sleeve up, matter-of-fact, a little official producing a document, and there it was in the grey light, four small ovals going green at the edges, fingers, unmistakeably fingers — "and then you laughed at Saff on the landing and then you fell down the stairs. I was watching through the bars. You're not allowed to grab arms. Miss Khalil says." The kitchen held still. Keith looked at the arm. Something went across his face that on a better man might have been shame, and on Keith was just arithmetic — and Saff stepped away from the wall, between her brother and the doorframe, and her voice when it came out was steady in a way I'll never manage if I live to ninety. "Mum's shift ends at six," she said. "And your phone's been going all night, Keith. Gaz texts a lot. So does the bank." She let it sit. Pick of the thumbnail against her sleeve, once. "You want to talk about what things sound like written down. Five weeks of sandwiches." Keith stood in the doorway, swaying slightly, one shoe on, bin juice on his shoulder, and looked around the kitchen at all of us — the maths visibly not coming out, for once, for the first time in eight months — and what he said, in the end, quietly, was: "Where's me other shoe."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely impressive piece of craft that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me work through it carefully. **Narrative Voice and Execution** Wren's retrospective, slightly defensive voice is maintained throughout with real consistency. The line "Words go strange at 5 a.m." is exactly the kind of small, earned observation the plan called for — not lyrical inflation, just a character noticing something true. The narration's self-awareness ("I'd like to report what I did in that moment. I have no information.") is funny and honest without being cute. The voice never tips into self-congratulation. **The Central Confrontation** The fight between Wren and Saff is the chapter's emotional core and it lands hard. Saff's speech — "you get to go home after this" — is devastating precisely because it's true, and the narration acknowledges this explicitly: "every word of it was true." This is the plan's instruction about "both of them saying unforgivable accurate things" executed cleanly. Wren's half-sentence about the push is handled with real skill: "what came out of me, from somewhere I keep the worst things" — the four words that never fully emerge but "both of us heard them anyway" is a genuinely effective technique, showing the damage without spelling it out. **Keith's Return** The Lazarus moment is handled with the right tonal balance. The comedy of the bin groaning, the cling film on the shoe, the "noise like a kettle" — these land without undercutting the menace that follows. The plan specified that "the comedy collapses within a paragraph into real menace" and this is achieved. Keith's quiet administrative menace — laying down the story "like flooring," the weather system reassembling — is exactly right. The detail of him nodding along with himself, "gaining ground," and Wren feeling it working on her is the story's most honest and frightening moment. The plan called for this to "nearly work" and it does. **Denny** Denny's entrance and testimony is the chapter's best scene. "You're not allowed to grab arms. Miss Khalil says" is perfect — the child's moral authority coming from a completely mundane source, delivered without drama. The four green ovals "fingers, unmistakeably fingers" is shown rather than told. His framing of events ("you laughed at Saff on the landing and then you fell down the stairs") is both clarifying and deliberately ambiguous — it doesn't resolve the push question, it just adds another layer. This is exactly what the plan called for: "complicates rather than resolves." **Saff's Defeat of Keith** Saff's steady voice, the thumbnail pick, "five weeks of sandwiches" — this is economical and effective. The plan's instruction that Keith's defeat be "small" is honored: he doesn't flee in shame, he asks for his shoe. "Where's me other shoe" is the right ending line for Keith's arc in this chapter — deflating, pathetic, and somehow still slightly menacing in its ordinariness. **Potential Weaknesses** The "grey light" motif is used three times (grey shapes, grey sky, grey light) which is slightly overdone — it's doing atmospheric work but the repetition becomes noticeable. "The weather system reassembling out of nothing" is a metaphor that's been used earlier in the story (per the character profiles) and its reuse here is intentional and earned, but it's worth noting. The line "the truth was arriving with the morning at the same speed" is the chapter's one moment of slight purple prose — it's reaching for something that the surrounding plainness doesn't quite support. The gap in the record during Keith's emergence ("There's a gap in the record between the lid going up and the three of us pressed against the far wall") is a clever technique but slightly self-conscious — it draws attention to the narration's constructedness in a way that's mostly earned but slightly precious. **Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan with remarkable fidelity. The fight is present, the unforgivable accurate things are said, Denny's testimony complicates rather than resolves, Keith's quiet administrative menace nearly works, and the chapter ends with Keith looking for his shoes. The plan's instruction that "the comedy collapses within a paragraph into real menace" is executed. The flat ending is achieved. **Overall** This is skilled, controlled writing that earns its darkness and its occasional lightness. The dialogue is strong throughout — particularly Denny's and Keith's. The chapter does what it sets out to do without overreaching. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight — Thursday Keith left at twenty to six. I want to be exact about it because nothing about it was dramatic, and the lack of drama is the part I've had to live with. He went round the house collecting himself — phone off the chair arm, charger out of the wall, the sports bag from the cupboard under the stairs that none of us had known was already half packed, which told you something about Keith, that there'd always been a bag — and we stood in the kitchen and watched him do it through the doorway, the way you watch weather leave. He found his other shoe by the back step. He sat on the bottom stair, two feet from his own blood, and put it on, breathing hard through his nose, concussed and slow with the laces, and not one of us offered to help, and not one of us called an ambulance, and I've thought about both of those facts a lot since. At the door he stopped. The lanyard was on the hook by his head, KEITH DOYLE and the barcode and the face, and he looked at it for a second and left it there, which was the closest he came to a confession. Then he looked back down the hall at us. At Saff. "You want to watch that one," he said, to the house in general, and was gone. That's the ending he got. No sirens, no handcuffs, no consequences arriving in a marked car. A man with a head wound and one shoe and a sports bag, walking down towards the underpass at twenty to six in the morning, knowing exactly what we'd done. Brian's notebook had our names in it. The bin had blood in it, and the duvet, and recycling was Thursday. None of that was over and we all knew it wasn't over; it just stopped happening *at* us for a minute, and we stood in the hall like people after an earthquake, checking the shelves. We had eighteen minutes. We spent them on toast. I'm not being flippant — toast was Pickle's idea and it was the smartest thing anyone said all night: "When your mum comes in," she said, "there should be toast. A kitchen with toast in it is a normal kitchen." So we made toast nobody wanted and put the lights on and the radio low, and Pickle wiped the hallway floor while I watched her do it and didn't help, because I'd reached the end of something, and Denny ate everyone's crusts and told us a fox can run at thirty miles an hour, and at six minutes past six there were keys in the door. Saff's mum stood in the hallway in her work fleece, smelling of the home, dead on her feet, and took in: four children, 6 a.m., toast, the wrong faces. And Saff said, "Mum. Kitchen," in a voice I'd never heard before — older than the managing voice — and the kitchen door shut behind the two of them. I will not tell you what was said in there because I don't know, and I've decided not to invent it, which is, you'll have noticed, not like me. We sat in the lounge with Denny between us and the telly off and I heard the shapes of it through the wall: Saff's voice level for a long time, then her mum's, going up, then breaking, then a chair scraping, then nothing for ages — the nothing being, I'd guess, the part where they held onto each other, though I'm only guessing. At one point her mum laughed, once, horribly, the way you laugh at the size of something. The word "bin" was said. There was a silence after it with a whole future in it. Pickle, beside me on the sofa, quietly unzipped the Bag for Life. She took out the EVIDENCE notebook, and I watched her look at it — the label, her own neat handwriting from a hundred years ago, midnight — and I watched the whole night go across her face: the head-torch, the marigolds, the photographs, the fizzing, shameful, best-night-of-her-life feeling that I'd clocked at the door and said nothing about. She didn't tear it up or anything. She just put it inside her coat, against her chest, zipped the coat over it, and stared at the dead telly. That was Pickle's whole speech. It was a good one. When Saff came out she'd been crying and wasn't anymore. She leaned on the doorframe — her doorframe, in her house, which had already begun very slightly rearranging itself back — and looked at the three of us on the sofa. "Mum's phoning in about the locks," she said. "And there's going to be — phone calls. Proper ones. She's doing them herself." Pause. Thumbnail, sleeve. "She wants to know exactly nothing about the bin, she says, ever, except she also wants the bin bleached by tonight, so, work that one out." Then she came and sat on the sofa arm and buttered the last cold toast, and said, not to anyone, in the flattest voice she owns: "I'd do it again." And here's where I'm supposed to give you the speech. I had all morning to write it — three points and a conclusion: it was wrong, or it was understandable, or it was both and here's how to live with both. I'm fifteen. He grabbed a seven-year-old's arm hard enough to leave fingers, and he did the laugh, and she pushed him, and she watched him land and felt done, and she'd do it again, and she ate her toast while she said it. I didn't argue. I'm writing this down weeks later and I still haven't argued, and I still don't know if that's loyalty or agreement or just me being brilliant at *fine*, and every time I think I've decided, I un-decide by teatime. That's the honest ending. Sorry. It's the only one I've got. — The settee came on a Thursday, three weeks later. Second-hand but new to them, grey, enormous, no lock on the lounge door anymore, no chair that was anyone's. I was home revising, actually revising, when my phone went. 🩸 One. Singular. And I was up off my bed with my shoes half on and my heart doing the regional event before the second text landed: **denny did a nosebleed on the new settee. its everywhere. he's thrilled. bring snacks** I stood in my room with my shoes on, breathing, reading it about four times. Then I texted back **omw**, and went down past Mum — "Saff's," I said, and it was the whole truth, all of it, every word — and got my bike out through the gap in the gate. I took crisps.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of YA fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Voice and Narration** Wren's retrospective voice is consistently maintained and genuinely distinctive. The self-awareness ("I'm not being flippant") and the self-implication ("which is, you'll have noticed, not like me") feel earned rather than performed. The line "I've thought about both of those facts a lot since" does real work — it's the kind of thing a fifteen-year-old narrator would actually say, and it carries moral weight without announcing itself. The voice avoids the trap of being too clever or too literary; it stays in register throughout. **The Ending's Moral Refusal** The plan called for no absolution, no bow, and the chapter delivers. "I'd do it again" is genuinely disturbing precisely because Keith was only ever pathetic — the plan's key insight is fully realized. Wren's admission that she "un-decides by teatime" is the honest non-resolution the story needed. This is not a cop-out; it's a deliberate and mature refusal to tidy up what shouldn't be tidied. **Pickle's Notebook Moment** This is the chapter's best scene. "She didn't tear it up or anything. She just put it inside her coat, against her chest, zipped the coat over it, and stared at the dead telly. That was Pickle's whole speech. It was a good one." This is exactly the kind of show-don't-tell that the plan demanded — Pickle's arc resolves without a word of dialogue, and the narration's brief commentary ("It was a good one") is perfectly calibrated. It doesn't over-explain. **Keith's Exit** The lanyard detail is excellent — "he looked at it for a second and left it there, which was the closest he came to a confession." This is precise and resonant without being overwrought. His parting line ("You want to watch that one") is appropriately menacing and small simultaneously. The sports bag already half-packed is a strong character detail that lands without fanfare. **The Kitchen Scene** The decision not to narrate what Saff and her mum say is bold and correct. "I will not tell you what was said in there because I don't know, and I've decided not to invent it, which is, you'll have noticed, not like me." This is one of the chapter's finest moments — it's meta-aware without being cute, and it trusts the reader. The shapes of the conversation through the wall (the laugh "at the size of something," the word "bin" and the silence after it) are beautifully handled. **The Final Beat** The single blood-drop emoji followed by Denny's nosebleed is exactly right — it earns its lightness because the reader has been through the full weight of the night. "I said, and it was the whole truth, all of it, every word" is a lovely callback to Wren's lying that doesn't over-explain itself. "I took crisps" is the correct final line: dry, small, character-consistent, and genuinely funny without undercutting the preceding darkness. **Potential Weaknesses** The paragraph where Wren addresses the reader directly about the speech she's "supposed to give" is the chapter's most vulnerable moment. It works, but it's close to the edge of being too self-conscious — the "I'm fifteen" line in particular risks feeling like the author speaking rather than the character. It's saved by the specificity of "I un-decide by teatime," but it's worth noting. The "watching weather leave" metaphor for Keith's departure is the one moment of slight purple prose — it's not terrible, but it's the most conventionally "literary" image in a chapter that otherwise earns its effects through precision rather than metaphor. The plan called for "at least two chapters end flat, mid-gesture" — this chapter ends with a complete gesture (the crisps), which is appropriate for a final chapter, but the ending is perhaps slightly warmer than the plan's stated "no bow" intention. The nosebleed text is genuinely funny and the warmth feels earned, but a very strict reading of the plan might find it marginally too resolved. I'd argue it works because the threat (Brian's notebook, the bin, Keith knowing) is explicitly left unresolved in the body of the chapter. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. "Mum. Kitchen" is excellent. "You want to watch that one" is right. "I'd do it again" lands. The plan's instruction to ration quips is followed. **Consistency with Character Profiles** All characters behave consistently with their profiles. Saff's "flattest voice she owns" for the most disturbing line is exactly right. Pickle's silent notebook moment is her character resolving as planned. Denny's fox fact and crust-eating are perfectly in register. Wren's self-implication and the lying-as-skill thread are properly closed. **Overall** This is a high-quality piece of literary YA fiction that executes a sophisticated plan with genuine craft. The restraint is consistent, the voice is distinctive, the moral refusal is earned, and the comedy and darkness are properly calibrated. Minor quibbles about one slightly purple metaphor and one slightly self-conscious paragraph do not significantly diminish what is otherwise an excellent final chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction. The narrator's voice — Wren — is immediately distinctive and sustained with impressive consistency across all eight chapters. The first-person present-tense retrospective narration, with its self-aware asides ("I want this on the record"), creates a voice that feels genuinely teenage without being condescending or clichéd. The writer understands that the best YA fiction doesn't talk down to its audience. **Strengths:** The characterization is the piece's greatest achievement. Wren's self-awareness about her own complicity — the note against the salt, the phone going in the pocket "fluently," the washing-up — is rendered with surgical precision. She's not a hero; she's a fifteen-year-old who is "brilliant at fine," and the story never lets her off the hook for that. Saff is equally complex: the managing voice, the Denny setting, the thumbnail picking at the sleeve seam — these physical tics accumulate into a fully realized portrait of a girl who has been performing competence under duress for years. Pickle's arc — the shameful excitement of the podcast-trained amateur detective confronting real horror — is handled with particular delicacy. The moment she puts the EVIDENCE notebook inside her coat is genuinely moving. Denny is a triumph of economy. His fox facts, his administrative acceptance of horror ("Is he drunk again?"), his matter-of-fact production of the bruised arm — these are perfectly calibrated. The writer understands that children who have grown up around instability develop a particular kind of flat pragmatism that is more devastating than any dramatic reaction. The dark comedy is genuinely earned. The CPR video with the unskippable car insurance advert, the variety-pack crisps on the stairs, Brian the PCSO and his caravan in Mablethorpe — these land because they're grounded in specific, observed detail rather than generic comic beats. The laughter on the stairs is handled with real skill: the moment it curdles is precisely timed, and the "ready salted sat in my stomach like a verdict" is one of the piece's best lines. The dialogue is excellent throughout. "He's not a scene, Pickle, he's on our floor" is perfect. Keith's resurrection speech — "Let's all be sensible" — is chilling in its familiarity, the abuser's toolkit deployed with horrible fluency. "You want to watch that one" as his exit line is exactly right: petty, threatening, diminished. The ending is beautifully handled. The single blood drop emoji resolving into a nosebleed on the new settee is structurally elegant and emotionally satisfying without being saccharine. The final line — "I took crisps" — earns its simplicity. **Weaknesses:** The piece occasionally over-explains its own effects. "That's the honest ending. Sorry. It's the only one I've got" is slightly too self-conscious, breaking the fourth wall in a way that feels more like the author hedging than Wren speaking. The voice is strong enough that it doesn't need this kind of meta-commentary to justify its ambiguity. Some of the metaphors, while generally landing, occasionally tip into the slightly overwrought: "the question sat there in the kitchen anyway with its coat on, not leaving" is charming but perhaps one personification too many in a piece that already uses the technique frequently. "A silence with the shape of the flash still in it" is genuinely good; "the most important sentence in the world" is a slight lapse into telling. The pacing in Chapter 6 (the bin logistics) is slightly labored — the comedy of the wheelie bin is well-observed but the chapter runs a little long before Brian's arrival rescues it. The Brian scene itself is excellent, but the setup could be tighter. Keith's resurrection is the one moment where the piece risks tipping from dark comedy into farce, and it just about stays on the right side of the line, but it requires the reader's goodwill. The subsequent confrontation scene, however, is handled with enough restraint that it recovers. The "forced poetry" question: the piece uses a lot of figurative language, but it's almost always grounded in the specific and the observed. The weather-system metaphor for Keith is used consistently and earns its repetition. The fox screaming "like the end of the world and means, according to Denny, absolutely nothing" is thematically resonant. These are not non-sequiturs; they're doing work. Overall, this is a genuinely impressive piece — confident, consistent, emotionally intelligent, and funny in the right ways for the right reasons. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Pacing: 15 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 84.3/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 86.1 | Final: 89.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **The prompt:** "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." Classic hook — a salvage/cargo ship, a mysterious crate, and a crew about to have a very bad (or transformative) week. The Space Western genre suggests: frontier worlds, lawlessness, bounty hunters, dusty mining colonies, a ship as a horse/wagon, laconic dialogue, moral codes among outlaws, corporate empires as railroad barons, Firefly/Cowboy Bebop/Outer Wilds vibes. **What's in the crate? Options:** 1. **A child** — alien or genetically engineered, escaping a corporation. Crew must decide: turn her in for the bounty or protect her. Very Firefly ("River in a box"). Emotionally rich but well-trodden. 2. **A creature** — horror angle. Alien predator loose on the ship. Tense but turns into Alien-pastiche; less "western," more horror. 3. **A person in cryo** — an outlaw, a witness, a dying lawman with a secret. Could be the captain's old partner/lover/enemy. Personal history angle. 4. **Terraforming seed/living world-engine** — something that wants to grow, turn the ship into a garden. Weird, lyrical, less conflict-driven unless someone wants it. 5. **A sentient AI core / robot** — last of its kind, carries a map to a lost claim (gold rush echo: water rights, a vein of exotic ore). Strong western parallel: the treasure map, the claim jumpers chasing it. 6. **Alien livestock** — comedic angle: they've accidentally rustled someone's prize alien cattle, and a rancher baron sends riders after them. Lighter tone, fun, true to western tropes (cattle rustling!). 7. **The crate contains a dying alien who imprints memories/a debt on whoever opens it** — a "blood debt" story, very western honor-code. **Combination idea:** The crate holds a creature — but it's not a monster; it's a young alien beast (like a foal of some near-extinct species, e.g., the last "sandmare" of a destroyed world), worth a fortune. A cattle-baron-equivalent (corporate magnate who exterminated the species for terraforming rights) wants it dead or owned. The crew picked up the crate as anonymous freight. Now hired guns are coming. This gives: mystery → discovery → moral choice → pursuit → standoff → sacrifice → resolution. Western beats: the rustled animal, the range war, bounty hunters, a frontier town shootout, riding off into the sunset (nebula). Alternative: the child version, but make her an "indigenous" frontier kid whose people's land claim the corporation erased — she's literally the living deed (biometric key to a homestead claim). That fuses the western land-rights theme with the SF MacGuffin. The crate-child is the deed to a world. **Characters:** - Captain: weathered, debt-ridden, ex-something (ex-marshal? ex-corporate range rider who quit after a massacre). Name ideas: Captain Eadie Vance, Captain Cole Reyes, Captain Marisol "Mara" Okafor of the *Jenny Creel* / *Dustwitch* / *Prodigal Mare*. - First mate/engineer who speaks the prompt line: young deckhand, nervous — good POV character? Or grizzled mechanic. - A doc with a past; a pilot who never leaves the cockpit; maybe keep crew small (3–4) for 8k words. - Antagonist: bounty hunter with a code (could turn at the end, western trope of the honorable gunslinger), and behind him, the corporation/baron. - Maybe an old acquaintance of the captain among the pursuers — personal stakes. **Tone options:** Lean, dusty prose; laconic dialogue; melancholy but warm; ends bittersweet-hopeful. Or horror-tense. Or comic caper. The strongest for a short story is probably the melancholy-warm western with a tense middle. **8-chapter beat sketches:** Version A (creature/foal): 1. Setup: ship, crew, debt, the crate job at a backwater station; the line is spoken at chapter's end. 2. Opening the crate — the creature revealed; what it is; first signs they're carrying contraband worth killing for. 3. Bonding + backstory; discovery of who shipped it and why; captain's history with the species/the baron. 4. Pursuers arrive — hailed mid-void or at a fuel stop; refusal; chase. 5. Damage, hiding in an asteroid field / dead town on a moon; crew dissent (sell it? dump it?). 6. Betrayal or capture — a crew member tips off the hunters, or they're caught at the settlement. 7. Standoff/shootout — western climax in a frontier town street or hangar; cost paid (a death or the ship wounded). 8. Resolution — deliver the creature to the hidden herd/sanctuary/its people; the hunter lets them go or dies well; ride into the black. Version B (child/deed): similar beats but climax is at a land office / claims tribunal or the homestead world itself; ending = the girl sets foot on her world, claim registers, corporation checked (locally, at least). POV choice: first-person captain gives voice and western flavor ("I've hauled stranger cargo, but never cargo that hummed"). Or the deckhand who says the line — naive eyes. Captain POV probably stronger and more authoritative. Pacing concern: 8×1000 words — need each chapter to end on a hook. Avoid overstuffing antagonists: one named bounty hunter + faceless corporation is enough. Crew of three: captain, engineer (Trick/Juno), pilot/medic combo. # Reflection What fits the prompt best: the line demands the crate be the engine of the entire story — so whatever's inside must generate sustained conflict and emotional weight across 8 chapters, not just a one-scene reveal. A pure horror creature burns hot and fast and drifts from the western register into Alien territory; I'd rather honor the western promise: land, livestock, debts, codes, standoffs. The foal/last-of-its-species idea is evocative and visually western (a horse in a crate! rustling! a cattle baron!), and gives lovely imagery, but its emotional ceiling is limited — the creature can't speak, so the middle chapters risk going slack, leaning on crew arguments alone. The child-as-living-deed idea has more dialogue potential, sharper land-rights thematics (the heart of westerns), and a clear destination plot (get her to the claims tribunal/her world), which structures chapters 4–8 naturally. The risk is Firefly derivativeness — "mysterious girl in a box" is familiar. I can offset that by making her specific: not a weapon, not psychic — just a kid whose blood/genome is the legal key to a homestead world, the last signatory of a settlement compact, with her own prickly personality and grief. The MacGuffin is paperwork made flesh — that's a fresh, very western twist (it's all about the deed). A hybrid is tempting (girl + animal), but at 8,000 words that's clutter. Keep it lean: small crew (captain + two), one named hunter with a code, the corporation offstage as weather. Captain's first-person voice will carry tone economically — laconic, dust-dry, letting worldbuilding ride in asides rather than exposition dumps. What needs care: chapter 5–6 sag risk. The crew-dissent/betrayal beat must feel earned, so I should seed money trouble and one crew member's desperation early (chapter 1). Also the ending — westerns earn their sunsets with cost. Someone or something should be lost: the ship badly wounded, the bounty hunter dying to buy them the crossing, or the captain surrendering her own clean record/bounty. The hunter-with-a-code turning at the climax is a beloved trope; to keep it from feeling cheap, give him a stated principle in his first appearance that the corporation later violates (e.g., "I don't haul children" / they lie to him about the cargo), so his turn is logical, not sentimental. Decision-leaning: Version B structure, captain first-person POV, the prompt line closing chapter 1, melancholy-warm tone with a hard shootout climax in chapter 7 and a quiet, earned landing in chapter 8 — the girl's boots on her family's dirt, the crew poorer and better for it. That balances compelling character work, genuine western architecture, and clean 1000-word chapter beats with hooks at every break.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I'm writing a melancholy-warm Space Western told in the first-person voice of Captain Mara Vance of the freight-hauler *Prodigal Mare* — laconic, dust-dry, wry, with worldbuilding delivered in asides rather than exposition. The crate contains a child: Senna, eleven years old, the last living signatory-heir of a homestead compact on the world Cully's Rest. Her genome is the legal key to the land claim; Halloran Combine, the corporate "cattle baron" of the sector, needs her gone before the claim window closes and the world reverts to open lease. She is paperwork made flesh — the deed in a box — which keeps the story rooted in the western's true subject: land, law, and who gets to hold them. **Plot choices:** Small crew of three (Mara; Trick, the engineer, drowning in a debt I seed in chapter 1; Okoye, pilot/medic). One named antagonist with a stated code — bounty hunter Eli Crane, who declares early that he doesn't hunt children and was lied to about the cargo, so his climactic turn is logic, not sentiment. Halloran Combine stays offstage, felt like weather. Beats: discovery → reveal → bonding/backstory → first contact with pursuit → flight and dissent → Trick's desperate betrayal and capture → shootout at a dead frontier settlement where Crane turns → arrival at Cully's Rest, claim sealed, cost paid. **Things I'm aiming for:** the prompt line closing chapter 1 as the hook; a hook at every chapter break; Senna as a prickly, grieving, specific kid — never a mystic waif; an earned bittersweet ending (Trick forgiven but diminished, the ship wounded, Crane dead or ruined, Mara out of pocket and at peace); western iconography translated honestly (the deed, the rustlers, the standoff in a dirt street under a thin sky). **Things I'm avoiding:** psychic-child clichés; horror-pastiche; more than one named villain; a cost-free happy ending; expository lore dumps; sentimentality that isn't paid for. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Freight, No Questions.** Establish the *Mare*, the crew, the debt. At Bellwether Station, Mara takes a no-manifest crate for triple rates from a dying woman in a freight office — cash up front, deliver to coordinates near Cully's Rest, "don't open it, don't be late." Seed Trick's gambling debt to a station shark; seed Okoye's steadiness. Two days into the black, Trick comes to the galley pale: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." End chapter. **Chapter 2 — What the Box Held.** They crack the crate: life-pod rig, and inside, Senna — furious, dehydrated, armed with a stripped stunner, demanding to be put back in. She won't talk. Okoye doctors her; Mara finds a compact ledger sewn into the pod lining: a homestead claim, signatory genome, a deadline twelve days out. News feeds confirm the freight office on Bellwether burned the night they left. Hook: a registered pursuit bond — somebody's posted paper on "stolen Combine property." **Chapter 3 — The Deed That Breathes.** Quiet chapter; bonding and backstory. Senna thaws by degrees — her family dead in a "depressurization accident," the aunt who shipped her was the woman at the freight office. Mara's own history surfaces: she rode protection for Halloran once, quit after they cleared a settlement. Trick fields a private comm he doesn't mention. Crane's ship appears on long-scan, hailing polite as Sunday. Hook: his voice on the comm — "I'm told you're hauling stolen equipment, Captain. I don't make trouble over equipment." **Chapter 4 — Parley in the Black.** Tense comm parley; Crane states his code plainly: he hunts bonded men, not babies, and was contracted for "a biological asset, hazardous." Mara lies badly; Crane doesn't quite believe her but lets it lie — for now. They run for the Tallow Drift, a debris field, to lose his scan. Crew dissent erupts: Trick wants to hand the cargo over and clear his debt; Okoye won't. The deadline math gets ugly. Hook: Trick alone at the comm station, the pursuit bond's contact channel open on his screen. **Chapter 5 — The Drift.** Cat-and-mouse in the Tallow Drift; the *Mare* takes hull damage hiding cold. Best chapter for Mara and Senna: the kid fixes a relay better than Trick, talks about her father's orchard rows on Cully's Rest; Mara tells her about the settlement Halloran cleared and why she ran. Trick, cornered by his debt and his fear, sends a burst transmission. They limp out of the Drift needing fuel at Perdition's Ferry, a half-dead waystation town. Hook: Crane's ship is already docked there. **Chapter 6 — Perdition's Ferry.** The trap closes. Crane takes them at the fuel depot — courteous, professional — and reveals he knows about Trick's tip and despises it. But when Senna is brought out, Crane sees what the cargo is and goes very still; his Combine minders (two bonded guns sent to "supervise") move to take the girl themselves and tidy up witnesses. Trick, wrecked by what he's done, confesses to Mara in lockup. Hook: Crane unlocks their cell. "Combine lied to me. I don't haul children. You've got about four minutes." **Chapter 7 — The Street.** The shootout: dawn light, the dirt-and-grating main street of Perdition's Ferry, Combine guns between the crew and the *Mare*. Crane stands with them — the western standoff, named and paid for. Trick takes a round shielding Senna; Crane kills both minders and is gut-shot doing it. He refuses passage: a man who breaks contract stays where he broke it. They burn off the pad with the Combine's wider net waking up. Hook: nine hours to the claim window, and the *Mare* flying on a cracked drive. **Chapter 8 — Cully's Rest.** The crossing on a dying engine; Okoye keeping Trick alive; Senna in the copilot seat. They ground hard on Cully's Rest — wind, grass, the burnt frame of her family's homestead — and reach the claims beacon as Combine ships enter orbit too late: her blood on the reader, the compact sealed, the world hers under law even Halloran must publicly honor. Quiet coda: Trick will live, owing a different kind of debt; Mara declines payment she's owed and takes a standing berth contract instead — somebody has to haul freight for a one-girl world. The *Mare* lifts into a thin gold sunset, poorer and better for it.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This A human writer given "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up" would likely start from a feeling, not a structure. The line is funny-nervous; it has a voice in it already — the ellipsis, the hedging. A human might build outward from *who says that line and why they're scared to say it*, and let the story accrete around relationships and texture before plot. They'd probably write the crate-opening scene first, discover the characters in the writing, and find that the story is "actually about" something they didn't plan — a divorce, a dead sibling, the feeling of being responsible for people you can't protect. The plot would serve that discovered core. A human would also be comfortable with mess: scenes that exist mainly for flavor, a character whose backstory never gets explained, dialogue that wanders before it lands, a chapter that ends flat because the next one starts hot. They'd trust the reader. They'd let some things stay unresolved because life does. And crucially — a human writing a western pastiche would either lean all the way into genre pleasure (and have *fun*, with swagger and jokes) or deliberately subvert it. They wouldn't execute the tropes with dutiful competence. # Telltale LLM Failings, Generally 1. **Safety.** The median choice at every fork. The kid is plucky-but-vulnerable, the captain is gruff-with-a-heart-of-gold, the bounty hunter has a code. Nothing risks alienating anyone. 2. **The bow.** Everything wraps. Every gun fires, every seed sprouts, every character arcs, every debt (literal and thematic) is settled in the final chapter, often with a symmetrical callback. 3. **Symmetry and tidiness of structure.** Chapters of equal weight, a hook at every break, beats arriving on schedule like trains. Real stories breathe unevenly. 4. **Writing impressively at the expense of readability.** Stacked metaphors, "lyrical" closing lines on every paragraph, fondness for triads ("poorer, wiser, and better for it"), portentous fragments. For dust-dry western voice this is fatal — laconic means *withholding*. 5. **Stated themes.** LLMs underline meaning: a character will say the theme out loud ("It's all about who holds the land, ain't it"). Humans bury it. 6. **Emotionally legible characters.** Everyone's motivation is articulable and articulated. People confess, explain, apologize at the right narrative moment.
