Real people deflect, lie to themselves, stay silent. 7. **Sentiment without weirdness.** LLM-tender moments are generic-tender (the kid fixes a relay; the captain softens). Human-tender moments are specific and a little off (the kid hoards sugar packets; the captain can't say the dead person's name so she calls her "the woman"). 8. **Frictionless competence in prose.** No rough edges, no risky jokes, no boring-on-purpose, no sentence that takes a second read. Polish that reads as laminate. 9. **Dialogue as plot delivery.** Everyone responds to what was actually said. No crosstalk, no non-sequiturs, no people having two different conversations at once. # Deep Dive: Critiquing the Intention & Plan Going through this honestly, my plan exhibits a *lot* of the above. **1. The plan is a trope-execution machine.** "Bounty hunter with a code who turns," "betrayer crew member redeemed by taking a bullet," "child who is secretly the MacGuffin," "shootout in a dirt street at dawn," "ride into the sunset." Each is fine; all five stacked is a model reaching for the highest-probability western. I even flagged the Firefly derivativeness in brainstorming and then... kept the whole skeleton and changed the MacGuffin's paperwork. The "fresh twist" (deed-as-genome) is a *premise* tweak, not a *story* tweak — the emotional beats are still the most expected ones. **2. The bow is enormous.** Look at chapter 8: claim sealed *just in time*, villains arrive *too late*, Trick lives *and* is redeemed, Mara takes a contract that gives everyone a future, the ship lifts into "a thin gold sunset, poorer and better for it." That last phrase is the LLM tell in its purest form — the triadic, thematically self-congratulating closing line, planned in advance. Even Crane gets a tidy noble death with a stated reason ("a man who breaks contract stays where he broke it" — too quotable, too neat, a fortune-cookie code). **3. Mechanical hook discipline.** "A hook at every chapter break" is stated as an explicit goal. That produces a story that feels like a content schedule. Some chapters should end quiet, mid-thought, on an image, on nothing. **4. Everything is motivated and explained.** Trick has a gambling debt (seeded ch. 1, fires ch. 5, redeemed ch. 7 — clockwork). Mara has a guilt backstory that maps 1:1 onto the plot (she once helped Halloran clear a settlement; now she saves one). This mirror-symmetry between backstory and plot is deeply LLM — humans let backstory rhyme obliquely or not at all. Worse, the plan has Mara *tell Senna* this backstory in chapter 5, i.e., the theme delivered via campfire confession at the structural midpoint. **5. Crane is too convenient.** His code is installed in chapter 4 specifically so his chapter 6 turn "is logic, not sentiment" — I literally wrote that the setup exists to justify the payoff. That's writing backward from the trope. A human Crane might be genuinely frightening, or pathetic, or might *not* turn, or might turn for a petty reason (Combine shorted him once; he hates the minders' manners), or might just leave — refuse to participate either way, which is colder and truer to mercenaries. **6. Senna risks waif-hood despite my disclaimer.** I said "prickly, grieving, specific" but the planned beats are stock: armed when found, thaws by degrees, fixes a relay (competence = endearment, the laziest bonding device), talks about her dad's orchard (sentiment via dead parent). "Prickly" as a character note usually produces three sarcastic lines and then full compliance with the plot. **7. The deadline.** A claim window twelve days out is pure screenwriting software — an artificial ticking clock so the climax can be a race. It also forces the "arrived just in time" ending. Westerns mostly don't run on clocks; they run on inevitability — the riders *will* come, the question is what you do when they do. **8. The crew is understaffed as people.** Okoye is "steadiness" — i.e., a function, not a person. Pilot/medic combo, won't betray, keeps Trick alive. She has no want of her own. LLM ensembles often have exactly one developed crew member per plot function. **9. Prose-plan red flags.** "Worldbuilding delivered in asides," "felt like weather," "paperwork made flesh" — these are good notes but I can already feel the voice they'll produce: every paragraph mortared with one wry aphorism, uniformly. True laconic voice has long dull stretches and then one sentence that cuts. Also names: *Prodigal Mare*, Perdition's Ferry, Tallow Drift, Cully's Rest — each individually fine, together it's a thesaurus of frontier-flavored naming, suspiciously even in its evocativeness. A human's universe has some flat names (Station 9, the Dietrich field) among the poetic ones. **10. Trick's arc is a morality play.** Sin (debt) → temptation → betrayal → confession → redemptive bullet → survives chastened. Every station of the cross. A human might let the betrayal be smaller and uglier (he didn't sell them out — he just *answered a question* on a comm because he was scared, and that was enough), and let forgiveness be incomplete: Mara keeps him on because she needs an engineer, and they don't talk about it, and that's the real wound. # Brainstorming: Making It More Human **Loosen the plot's joints:** - Kill the deadline, or make it already blown — the claim window closed while she was in the crate, and the question becomes what you do with a child whose legal personhood just expired. Now the ending can't be a race; it has to be a choice. - Let Crane *not* turn heroically. Options: he walks away ("I'll refund the bond" — and his absence at the climax is the gift); he takes the girl and Mara has to get her back; he dies stupidly, off-principle; he turns but survives and they're stuck with him, an awkward fourth crew member nobody wanted. - Consider no shootout at all — or a shootout that's brief, ugly, and unchoreographed (three seconds, somebody shot in the back, nobody says anything cool). Western deconstruction (Unforgiven) over western pageant. - Let the ending cost something real and unredeemed: Trick dies and it *doesn't* mean anything; or Senna gets her world and it's a burnt ruin she'll be alone on, and Mara leaving her there feels like abandonment as much as victory; or the Combine simply wins the legal point and the "win" is the girl alive, anonymous, deedless — the land lost, the kid saved, which is a real western ending (the frontier closes; you save what you can carry). **Roughen the characters:** - Give Mara a flaw that *costs the crew*, not a noble guilt. Maybe she took the crate knowing it was hot because she needed the money, and lies about that to Okoye — the captain is the first betrayer. - Make Mara's past relationship to Halloran oblique: not "she cleared a settlement," but something smaller and more shameful — she repossessed homesteads, paperwork work, never fired a shot, which is exactly why the deed-child cuts at her. Or no relevant backstory at all: she helps because the kid is on her ship and that's that. Sometimes refusing the wound is the more human choice. - Okoye needs a want: she's saving out for her own ship and this job torches her stake; or she's devout in a way that complicates things; or she's the one arguing to dump the kid — let the *steady* one be the cold one, and the gambler be soft. - Senna: make her unlikeable longer. She lies. She steals from the crew. She doesn't bond — she negotiates, because adults have only ever been transactional with her. The thaw, if it comes, is one moment, not a curve. Specifics over traits: she won't sleep in a bed (sleeps in the empty crate); she keeps doing arithmetic out loud (acreage, water rights — she was raised on the claim's numbers); she never once says thank you. - Trick's betrayal: smaller, more deniable, never fully confessed. Or invert it — he's loyal under pressure, and the reader's seeded suspicion (and Mara's) wrongs him, and *that* injustice is the scar the crew carries out. **Roughen the voice:** - Ration the lyricism. Decide the narrator gets maybe one striking image per chapter and is otherwise plain to the point of flatness. Let her be a *bad* narrator in human ways: she skips things she doesn't want to talk about ("I'm not going to write down what the freight office smelled like"), gets defensive, contradicts herself. - Allow humor that isn't wry-aphorism humor: bickering about food, a running argument over a broken latrine, Senna mocking the ship's name. - Let dialogue misfire: people interrupt, answer the wrong question, refuse to have the conversation the scene "needs." - Some chapter endings should be soft or anticlimactic. The strongest prompt-honoring move might be that chapter 1 ends on the line as planned — and chapter 2 opens with them standing around the crate for two pages *not* opening it, arguing about whose problem it is. **Roughen the world:** - Vary name registers: mix the poetic with the bureaucratic and the dumb (a town called Meeker's, a station called Transfer Six, a corporate world with a trademark symbol). - Let one worldbuilding element stay unexplained entirely (what burned the freight office; what the aunt's plan actually was — maybe there was no plan, just panic and a shipping label). - The Combine should be banal, not sinister — the minders are bored contractors on per diem; the menace is that nobody involved hates Senna, she's a line item. **Structural risks worth considering:** - Uneven chapter weights: one chapter that's almost all a single conversation; one that covers ten days in summary. - Withhold the full crate-contents reveal information longer than is comfortable — *Senna herself* doesn't know she's the heir-key; she thinks her aunt was smuggling her to relatives, and the discovery devastates her (she was shipped as a document, not saved as a child) — that's a genuinely human wound the current plan misses entirely and might be the best idea in this whole brainstorm. The plan's spine (crate → kid → corporate pursuit → frontier endgame) is sound and fits the prompt. What needs to change is everything that makes its execution *inevitable*: the clockwork motivations, the full-coverage redemptions, the scheduled hooks, the prefabricated final image. The revision should choose two or three of the above roughenings and commit, rather than sanding every surface evenly — which would itself be an LLM move.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention A Space Western told in the first person by Captain Mara Vance of the freight-hauler *Pelican* — a plain name on purpose; the poetic names in this universe are rationed. The crate holds Senna, eleven, shipped as anonymous freight by a panicked aunt. Senna believes she's being smuggled to relatives. The truth, which Mara works out and Senna learns mid-story, is that Senna's genome is the last living key to her family's homestead claim on Cully's Rest — she was shipped as a *document*, and that discovery, not any chase, is the emotional center of the piece. The Halloran Combine wants the claim to lapse; the people they send are bored contractors on per diem, banal rather than sinister. **Key choices, made deliberately against my earlier draft's grain:** - **No ticking clock won at the wire.** The claim window has *already closed* by the time anyone understands what Senna is. The legal fight is unwinnable. The ending is a choice, not a race: the land is lost; the kid is saved. The frontier closes; you keep what you can carry. - **Crane doesn't turn heroically.** The bounty man parleys, realizes the cargo is a child, refunds his bond, and *leaves* — his absence is the gift, and it solves nothing, because the Combine just sends the per-diem men instead. - **The violence is brief and ugly** — seconds, not a choreographed street duel. No one says anything cool. - **Trick is never redeemed by a bullet because he never fully betrays them.** He answers one frightened question on an open comm — a small, deniable thing — and is suspected of worse by Mara, wrongly. That injustice, unresolved, is the scar the crew keeps. His gambling debt exists but stays a texture, not a plot gear. - **Okoye is the cold one.** The steady pilot is the one who argues, reasonably, for handing the girl over. She wants her own ship; this job burns her stake. She never apologizes for the argument and is never punished for it. - **Mara's backstory is withheld.** No mirror-guilt, no campfire confession. She helps because the kid is on her ship. The narrator skips things she doesn't want to discuss and says so. - **Senna stays difficult.** She negotiates rather than bonds, steals, sleeps in the crate instead of a bunk, does acreage arithmetic aloud. One thaw moment, late, small. She never says thank you. **Voice & craft:** Laconic, flat-by-default prose; one striking image per chapter, no more. Dialogue allowed to misfire and crosstalk. Uneven chapter weights — one chapter is mostly a single conversation; another covers days in summary. Not every chapter ends on a hook; some end on an image or mid-thought. Mixed name registers (Transfer Six, Meeker's, Cully's Rest). One thing stays unexplained: what the aunt's full plan was, if she had one. Avoid: triadic closing lines, stated themes, full-coverage resolution, quotable codes, lyrical mortar between every paragraph. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Crate.** Transfer Six, a freight interchange. Mara takes a sealed crate from a shaking woman who overpays in hard scrip and won't make eye contact — destination a dead-letter depot near Cully's Rest, no manifest. Establish the crew through friction, not résumé: Trick and Okoye arguing about a latrine pump; Trick ducking a comm from someone he owes; Okoye's ship-fund jar literal and visible in the galley. Mara doesn't tell them what she was paid. Two days out, Trick at the galley door, not coming all the way in: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." **Chapter 2 — Standing Around.** They don't open it for a long time. An argument about whose problem it is, about the no-questions money (Mara's lie of omission surfaces — Okoye does the math on the scrip and goes quiet). When they crack it: life-pod rig, a girl, dehydrated, furious, who bites Okoye and then demands — negotiates — to be resealed and delivered as addressed, because "Aunt Dess paid for door-to-door." She gives a name, Senna, and nothing else. She won't take a bunk. That night Mara finds her asleep back inside the open crate. Chapter ends there, no hook, just the image. **Chapter 3 — Freight News.** Days in summary: Senna stealing rations and hoarding them, reciting acreage and water-allotment figures in her sleep, mocking the ship's name. Trick takes a comm call he half-closes the door on — *seen but not understood*, by Mara and the reader. Mara digs: the freight office on Transfer Six burned the night they left; a Combine "asset recovery" bond is posted for stolen biological materials, no description. Mara works out what Senna is from the claim ledger sewn into the pod lining — and works out that the claim window closed nine days ago, while the kid was in the box. She tells no one yet. The narrator admits she sat with the ledger a long time and isn't going to describe what she thought about. **Chapter 4 — Crane.** Mostly one conversation. A hunter's ship matches course; Eli Crane hails — courteous, tired, specific about per diems and paperwork, scarier for his blandness. He's contracted for stolen Combine biologicals; he'd rather settle than chase. Mara stalls; Senna, listening at the comm door, hears Crane name the claim, the lapse date, the term *signatory remains* — and understands before Mara can stop it: her aunt shipped her as a deed, and the deed has already expired. Crane, seeing a child on the pickup, goes silent a long moment, says he'll be refunding his bond, and breaks off. No turn, no rescue — he just leaves. The Combine's problem is still the Combine's problem. **Chapter 5 — The Quiet One Argues.** Aftermath chapter, almost no plot motion. Senna won't speak to anyone; she's done the arithmetic out loud — the claim is dead, ergo she's nothing, ergo why is anyone still flying. Okoye lays out the cold case to Mara, reasonably, without villainy: the bond will be reposted to worse people; handing Senna to a sector magistrate is legal, survivable, and possibly even safest for the girl; this run has already eaten her ship fund. Trick won't side with either of them. Mara overrules Okoye without a speech — "She's on my ship" — and knows it isn't an argument, it's just a fact she's chosen. Okoye doesn't apologize and isn't asked to. Ends mid-thought, Mara setting course for Cully's Rest anyway, unsure what delivering an expired deed even means. **Chapter 6 — Meeker's.** Fuel stop at Meeker's, a half-dead waystation. Two Combine contractors are waiting — not gunslingers; bored men with a tablet and a tidy-up clause. They know the *Pelican* was coming. Mara assumes Trick sold them out; the reader half-believes it too. The violence, when it comes, lasts seconds: a grab for Senna in a fuel alley, Okoye breaking a man's arm with a coupling wrench, a shot that takes Trick through the shoulder as they run, nobody saying anything at all. They burn off the pad. In the medbay Mara accuses Trick; what actually happened was smaller — days ago he confirmed their heading to the voice he owed money to, frightened, not knowing who was listening. He can't prove it and she can't unbelieve her version. The wound between them stays open. **Chapter 7 — Cully's Rest.** They ground on the claim anyway. Wind, grass gone to seed, the burnt frame of the homestead, her father's orchard rows still legible in the dirt. Senna walks the boundary doing the figures one last time — and stops doing them. The claims beacon confirms what they knew: LAPSED. REVERTED. No Combine ships descending, no race; just a company that has already won by waiting. The one thaw: Senna asks Mara to help her dig up something buried by the porch stone — not money, just her family's paper compact, signatures in fading ink, legally worthless now. She takes it. She doesn't cry where anyone can see and she doesn't say thank you. The chapter's single image: the *Pelican* parked in the orchard rows like a thing that grew wrong there. **Chapter 8 — What You Can Carry.** No bow. Mara files a guardianship-of-record at the next sector office — paperwork, queues, a clerk who never looks up; the Combine doesn't contest because there's nothing left to contest. Okoye stays aboard but starts taking her own side jobs; her jar goes back on the galley shelf, lighter. Trick heals crooked and he and Mara still don't talk about Meeker's; the narrator admits she knows she may have wronged him and hasn't found a way to say so. Senna sleeps in a bunk now, finally — but keeps the crate, broken down flat, under it. Last scene: Senna teaching Trick her water-allotment arithmetic at the galley table for no reason except it's hers and she wants it used. The *Pelican* takes a freight job like any other. Mara doesn't tell us where to. End plain.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mara Vance **Background.** Fifty-one. Grew up on Hadley's World, a dryland agricultural colony that got bought out and consolidated when she was a teenager — her family took the settlement money and scattered, which she has never quite framed as losing anything, though she hasn't been back. Twenty years crewing other people's ships, eleven captaining the *Pelican*, which she owns outright because she has sacrificed everything else to that fact: no partner, no home address, a brother on Cassel Station she exchanges messages with maybe twice a year, always about nothing. Somewhere in her thirties there was a stretch she doesn't narrate — the reader will catch the edges of it: she knows how Combine bond paperwork is structured, she knows what an asset-recovery contract pays, she goes quiet when contractors are described as "just doing a job." Whether she did that work or was on the receiving end of it is never settled, and she'd tell you it doesn't matter, which is itself information. **Personality & flaws.** Competent, contained, allergic to declaration. Her core flaw is that she treats *withholding* as a form of protection — she didn't tell the crew what the crate paid because she'd already decided the risk was acceptable, and deciding *for* people is her lifelong habit. She is slow to extend trust and disastrously fast to withdraw it: the accusation of Trick at Meeker's isn't out of character, it's the character. She'd rather be wrong and silent than wrong out loud. She is also, quietly, a soft touch in ways she finds embarrassing — she's taken bad jobs before because somebody at a freight counter looked desperate, and she frames these afterward as business decisions. **Idiosyncrasies.** Does the ship's accounts by hand on paper because she doesn't trust numbers she can't cross out. Eats standing up. Refers to the *Pelican* as "the ship," never by name, never "she" — Trick named it, before her time as owner, and she kept the name out of some superstition she won't examine. Keeps a drawer of hard scrip from a dozen dead currencies. When she's thinking, she reads old manifests the way other people read novels. **Physicality.** Rangy, weathered brown skin, grey scattered through dark hair she cuts herself, badly, with clippers. Moves slowly and economically — people read it as calm; it's mostly conservation. Stands in doorways with arms folded rather than entering rooms. Looks at people a half-second too long before answering. Hands always slightly stained from the manifest ink. **Motives & relationships.** Wants: to keep the ship solvent, which is the acceptable name for keeping the only continuous thing in her life. Owes nobody money and considers that her one unambiguous achievement. Trusts Okoye's judgment more than her own in a fight and resents needing to. Is fond of Trick the way you're fond of a leak you've stopped patching. Her decision to keep Senna is not maternal awakening — she'd reject that reading angrily — it's the only place her code has left to stand: *the kid is on my ship.* **Voice.** > "I'm not going to tell you what the job paid, because then we'd have to have a conversation about whether it was worth it, and I already had that conversation by myself." > "Crane was polite the way an invoice is polite. I've been billed by men like him before. I'll leave it there." > "Okoye wasn't wrong. I want that written down somewhere, that she wasn't wrong. I just wasn't going to do it." # Senna **Background.** Eleven. Born on Cully's Rest, third generation of the compact — eight families originally, down to one by the time she was old enough to count, which she did, constantly, because counting was the family business. Her father managed the claim's water ledger and taught her the figures as a game and then as a duty: allotments, acre-feet, the arithmetic that proved the claim was worked and therefore valid. Her parents and grandmother died eleven months ago — "decompression event" on a supply run, officially. Aunt Dess, her mother's sister, came out from Transfer Six, lasted seven months on the land, panicked, and shipped her. Senna spent the time since the funeral being handled by adults who all wanted something signed. **Personality & flaws.** Transactional, vigilant, proud. She does not bond; she negotiates, because every adult interaction in the last year has been a negotiation conducted over her head, and she's decided she'd rather be a party to the contract than its subject. Her flaw is the armor itself: she treats kindness as an opening position and looks for the catch, which wounds people who don't deserve it and bores her grief in deeper. She lies easily and well about small things (where rations went, whether she slept) and is incapable of lying about numbers. When the claim lapses, the scaffolding of her entire identity — *I am the signatory, I am why the land is held* — goes with it, and her response is not tears but a horrifying flat logic: if the deed is void, so is she. The story's job is to get her from being a document back to being a kid, and it only half-finishes. **Idiosyncrasies.** Recites allotment figures under her breath when stressed, the way other people hum. Hoards food in specific quantities — three of everything — and gets agitated if the count is wrong. Sleeps in the crate because it's the last thing her aunt arranged for her, though she'd say it's because the bunks smell like coolant. Calls the ship "the *Pelican*" with audible contempt — "Pelicans can't even leave atmosphere" — and corrects anyone who misuses freight terminology. Never says thank you; says "noted" instead, which she got from a probate clerk. **Physicality.** Small for eleven, wiry, sun-scoured complexion gone sallow from the crate. Dark hair her aunt cut short for the journey, which she hates. Holds eye contact like it's a contest. Sits in corners with sightlines to doors. Quick precise hands — she really can fix things, but in this story her competence is mostly numerical, and she wields it as status, not charm. **Motives & relationships.** Wants, in order, as the story revises her: to complete the delivery as contracted; then, after chapter 4, nothing, dangerously; then, by the end, something small and real — for the figures in her head to be worth teaching to someone. Trusts no adult fully. Rates Okoye highest (Okoye doesn't pretend), finds Mara frustrating (Mara withholds, and Senna recognizes the technique), and is gentlest with Trick precisely because everyone else is hard on him. **Voice.** > "Aunt Dess paid for door-to-door. You opening the crate is a breach. I'm not mad, I'm just telling you what it is." > "Four hundred and twenty acre-feet, less seepage at six percent. You don't have to listen. It's not for you." > "Noted." # Okoye **Background.** Thirty-eight. Grew up on a generation-line hauler — born on a ship, raised on watches and rationing, never set foot on a planet until she was nineteen and found she didn't much care for them. Family scattered across three crews; she sends money to a sister with kids on Greer's Station and considers that the relationship. Flew ore runs, then medevac for a mining consortium (where she picked up the field-medic certification and a permanent contempt for company doctors), then signed on with Mara six years ago because the *Pelican* was the first ship where the captain's books were honest. Has been saving for her own ship the entire time — the jar in the galley is theater; the real fund is in three accounts she rebalances monthly. She is roughly four years from her down payment, or was, before this job. **Personality & flaws.** The most reasonable person aboard, which is her flaw: she mistakes reasoning for the whole of rightness. Her argument to hand Senna to a magistrate is genuinely well-constructed — safer for the girl, legal, survivable — and she will never understand why being correct didn't carry the day, only accept that it didn't. Unsentimental to a degree people read as cold; she'd say sentiment is a luxury good and she's still saving up. Doesn't apologize because she doesn't argue anything she hasn't already stress-tested. Loyal in the way of contracts: she signed on, so she stays, and she'd consider being praised for that insulting. Capable of sudden decisive violence (the coupling wrench at Meeker's) and entirely unbothered by it afterward, which unsettles Trick more than the violence itself. **Idiosyncrasies.** Eats the same three meals in rotation and has for years. Maintains the medbay to a standard wildly out of proportion to the rest of the ship. Counts the jar's contents in front of people, deliberately, when the ship loses money — her version of an editorial. Calls Senna "the passenger" long after everyone else uses her name; switches to "Senna" exactly once, in chapter 7, and it lands like a verdict. Sleeps four hours, twice a day, ship-style, and judges planet-sleepers silently. **Physicality.** Tall, broad-shouldered, very dark skin, head kept shaved. Spacer's body: strong grip, careful feet, never touches a wall or rail without meaning to. Economical face — expressions arrive late and small. When she disagrees, she doesn't frown; she gets *stiller*. Beautiful hands, always clean, medic-clean. **Motives & relationships.** Wants her own ship — not as escape but as arrival; it's the only ambition she's ever had. Respects Mara and is honest with her, which she considers the same thing. Finds Trick exhausting and covers his watches anyway. Is the only adult Senna doesn't test, because Okoye visibly wants nothing from her. **Voice.** > "I'm not arguing the kid's worth. I'm arguing the route. Those are different arguments and you keep answering the one I'm not making." > "I put six years into this ship's books being clean. You took dirty money and made me an accessory to it after the fact. I'll stay. I want it noticed." > "She bit me. Good instincts. Wrong target." # Trick **Background.** Forty-four, looks older. Born Patrice Okumu-Vasquez on Meridian, a corporate world with a trademark in its name; "Trick" since apprenticeship. A genuinely gifted drive engineer who has been fired from better ships than the *Pelican* — not for the work, ever, but for the gambling, which follows him like weather. He's been with Mara nine years, his longest berth, and he named the ship (after a bird in a picture book he had as a kid; he tells the story differently every time). Currently owes eleven thousand to a lending operation run out of Transfer Six by a soft-voiced woman called Maxa, and the debt is two payments past polite. **Personality & flaws.** Warm, talkative, conflict-averse to the point of damage. His flaw isn't dishonesty — it's *accommodation*: he answers questions because refusing feels like a fight, and fights feel like endings, and he can't bear endings. That's the whole of Meeker's: Maxa's voice on the comm asking, friendly as anything, where the *Pelican* was headed, and Trick — frightened, placating, wanting the call over — confirming it. Not a sale. A flinch. He knows the difference and knows nobody else will, and his deepest wound is that Mara's accusation matches what he's always feared he is, so he can't even defend himself with conviction. Generous with everything he has, which is part of why he never has anything. The only adult aboard who is simply *nice* to Senna from the start, without strategy, which is why she allows it. **Idiosyncrasies.** Narrates his repairs aloud to the machinery ("okay, okay, don't be like that"). Cooks elaborately on a two-burner galley when stressed; the worse things are, the better the crew eats. Carries a deck of paper cards he no longer lets himself play with — keeps it like a chip. Tells the ship-naming story three different ways across the book and seems unaware he's doing it. Hums when he's lying, which Mara knows and he doesn't know she knows. **Physicality.** Soft-bodied and strong under it, the way engineers get. Burn-scarred forearms, busy hands, a face that shows everything and shows it big — he winces, beams, crumples. Talks with the whole upper body. After Meeker's, carries the shoulder stiffly and develops a habit of standing slightly behind people, out of the line of rooms. **Motives & relationships.** Wants to stay — on this ship, in this crew, in any configuration where he isn't put out. The debt terrifies him less than the leaving it might cause. Loves Mara without illusion and is more hurt by her accusation than the bullet, and will never say either thing. Treats Okoye's competence as a place to shelter. With Senna he's found the one person aboard with no history to disappoint, and he tends that like a fire. **Voice.** > "I named her after a bird that catches fish and can't fly anywhere worth going. It was a joke. Nine years ago it was a joke." > "I told Maxa where we were headed because she *asked*, Captain. You ever had somebody ask you something friendly with a number behind it? No. You wouldn't have answered. That's the difference between us and you can have it." > "Kid. You eat standing in corners like the food's going somewhere. Sit. I made too much on purpose." # Eli Crane **Background.** Sixties. Asset-recovery contractor, bonded and licensed, twenty-plus years. One chapter of presence, so the profile is a single strong note: he's not a gunslinger, he's a *process*. Works alone, keeps immaculate paperwork, has outlived three Combine reorganizations because he never costs more than he recovers. Somewhere behind him is a family he supports on a world he visits between contracts; he mentions per diems the way other men mention their kids. **Personality & flaws.** Bland, thorough, unhurried — frightening because there's no heat anywhere in him to appeal to. His refusal of the Senna contract is not a moral awakening; it's an actuarial one with a moral floor under it: children are a category of cargo whose recovery has, in his long experience, never once stayed clean, legally or otherwise, and he is too old and too solvent to buy that exposure. If there's a code in it, he'd deny the word. His flaw, from the story's angle, is that his decency is *inert* — he won't do the wrong thing and won't lift a finger past that, and he leaves knowing exactly what the Combine will send after him. He sleeps fine. **Physicality.** Heard more than seen — a flat, unhurried voice on a comm, faint accent from nowhere in particular. Glimpsed once on a screen: thin, neat, reading glasses, a cabin behind him as orderly as a filing room. **Motives & relationships.** Wants to finish his contract year clean and go home. No relationship to anyone aboard; that's the point. He calls Mara "Captain" every single time, with a courtesy that never once becomes warmth. **Voice.** > "I'm contracted for recovery of biological materials, Captain. If your manifest says otherwise, one of us has been lied to, and my rates assume it's me." > "I'll be refunding the bond. That's not a favor to you. I want that understood before you waste any gratitude."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One — The Crate Transfer Six is a freight interchange, which means it's a place nobody is from and nobody stays. Six docking arms, a customs office that runs on bribes too small to feel like bribes, a strip of vendors selling fried things and tariff insurance. I've moved cargo through there maybe forty times. I couldn't tell you the color of the walls. We were three days into a layover, waiting on a contract that kept not arriving, and the *Pelican* was eating money the way a parked ship does — berth fees, air fees, the water meter that runs whether you drink or not. I do the books by hand, on paper, and that week the paper was bad. So when the woman at the freight counter flagged me, I went over. She was maybe sixty, maybe a hard fifty. Freight-office coveralls with somebody else's name stitched on them. She had a crate on a dolly behind her, standard half-length cargo unit, sealed, and she had the look — I knew the look before she opened her mouth. People get it when they've decided to do something and the deciding has used up everything they had, and now they're running on whatever's left. "You're the *Pelican*," she said. "Independent. No consortium flag." "That's right." "I have freight for the dead-letter depot at Carrow. Near Cully's Rest. You know it?" "I know where it is." "Sealed unit. No manifest. I'll pay door to door, hard scrip, half now." She named a figure. It was a little more than triple the lane rate. I'm going to be honest about what I did next, because the rest of this only makes sense if I am. I looked at the figure, and I looked at her hands, which were shaking, and I decided I didn't want to know which thing was making them shake. Then I took the job. She paid me out of an envelope, old bills, mixed currencies, the kind of money that's been under a mattress. She never once looked at my face. When the dolly handoff was done she said, "Don't open it. Don't be late." Then she went back inside the freight office and that was the last I saw of her, and I'm not going to describe the office, because later it mattered and I'd rather it didn't. --- I didn't tell the crew what the job paid. I told them we had a run to Carrow, which was true, and that it covered the layover, which was an understatement, and Okoye looked at me for a half-second longer than the information required and then went back to her preflight. Okoye's been with me six years. She keeps a jar in the galley, an actual glass jar, money in it, her ship fund — the real fund is somewhere smarter, I know that, the jar is for making points. When we lose money she counts it where I can see her. That week she hadn't counted it, which meant we were fine, which meant I'd bought myself some time on the conversation we'd eventually have. Trick spent the loading window in the engine room with the comm panel turned to face the wall. He'd been doing that all layover. Twice I'd heard the chime of an incoming call and twice heard him not answer it, and once I came into the galley and he was cooking — really cooking, three pans, something with onions that took an hour — and that's how I knew the debt was awake again. Trick cooks when things are bad. The worse it gets, the better we eat. I've never decided if it's an apology in advance. "Latrine pump's down again," Okoye said, over dinner. "It's not down," Trick said. "It's intermittent." "It's intermittent the way you're intermittent." "It needs the seal kit. I ordered the seal kit." "You ordered it at Greer's. That was four months ago." "It's *coming*." This is most of what life on a small ship is. People imagine other things and the other things happen maybe twice a year. The rest is the pump, the seal kit, the jar, the watches. We undocked from Transfer Six that night, and the crate rode in the forward hold, strapped and quiet, and I did the books, and the books were suddenly very good, and I sat with them longer than the arithmetic needed. --- The first day out was nothing. Trick rebuilt an injector he'd rebuilt before. Okoye flew and slept her two ship-sleeps and ate the second of her three meals — she rotates the same three, has for six years, and judges the rest of us silently for our variety. I read old manifests in my cabin, which is a thing I do, and which I've stopped explaining to people. The second day, Trick made bread. I want to be precise about that evening, because everything after runs from it. We'd eaten. Okoye had gone forward for her sleep. I was at the galley table with the ledger, crossing out a number I'd gotten wrong, and the ship was making its ordinary sounds — the air handlers, the tick of the drive cooling into its night cycle, all the sounds you stop hearing until the day they stop. Trick came to the galley door and didn't come through it. That was the thing I noticed first, before anything he said. Trick enters rooms the way weather does, whole-bodied, already talking. He stood in the doorway with his hand on the frame, and the hand was what I looked at, because the knuckles were pale, and a man who handles live drive components for a living does not hold onto a doorframe like it might leave. "Captain," he said. I put the pen down. "Captain, something's..." He stopped. He looked back down the corridor toward the forward hold, then at me, and his face — Trick's face shows everything, always has, it's the most honest part of him — his face was asking me to make what came next not be true. "Something's moving in that crate we picked up." The air handlers kept on. The drive ticked. "Moving how," I said. "Like knocking," Trick said. "Like something knocking to get out."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of opening chapter writing. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Craft** The first-person voice is immediately distinctive and consistent. Mara's narration is laconic, self-aware, and carries the exact quality described in the character profile: "allergic to declaration," withholding as protection. The line "I decided I didn't want to know which thing was making them shake" is a perfect encapsulation of her character — not callous, but deliberately incurious as a form of self-protection. The narrator's admission "I'm going to be honest about what I did next, because the rest of this only makes sense if I am" is a sophisticated move that establishes the confessional register without being maudlin. The prose is genuinely laconic without being affectless. It earns its flatness. "Transfer Six is a freight interchange, which means it's a place nobody is from and nobody stays" — this is efficient world-building that also characterizes the narrator's mode of perception. **Character Establishment** The crew is established through friction exactly as planned: the latrine pump argument, Trick's cooking as distress signal, Okoye's jar. These are economical and specific. The detail about Trick's comm panel "turned to face the wall" is excellent — it's behavioral, not explained, and it works. The bread detail ("The second day, Trick made bread") is a perfect beat. The woman at the freight counter is rendered in a few strokes that are genuinely evocative: "the deciding has used up everything they had, and now they're running on whatever's left." This is the one moment that approaches a more lyrical register, and it earns it. **The Ending** The chapter's final scene is well-executed. The detail of Trick's knuckles being pale, and the observation that "a man who handles live drive components for a living does not hold onto a doorframe like it might leave" — this is showing rather than telling, and it's specific and earned. The final exchange is appropriately spare. "Moving how." "Like knocking. Like something knocking to get out." This lands. **Potential Weaknesses** The chapter is almost entirely successful, but there are minor notes. The parenthetical about the freight office — "later it mattered and I'd rather it didn't" — is a slightly self-conscious foreshadowing gesture that calls attention to itself. It's in keeping with the narrator's voice but risks feeling like a writerly trick. The phrase "like weather does" in describing how Trick enters rooms is a small cliché. The observation about Okoye judging "the rest of us silently for our variety" is slightly over-explained — the detail of the three rotating meals already does the work. The plan called for "one striking image per chapter, no more" — the chapter arguably has two or three (the knuckles, the deciding-has-used-everything description, the drive ticking). This is a minor deviation but not a damaging one. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: Transfer Six established, the woman with the crate, the overpayment in hard scrip, crew friction through the pump argument and Trick's debt-cooking, Okoye's jar, Mara's omission about the pay, and the chapter-ending line delivered exactly as planned. The narrator's admission about not describing the freight office is a nice touch that honors the plan's note about the office burning later. **Overall** This is confident, controlled literary fiction in a genre register. The voice is sustained, the characters are distinct and consistent with their profiles, the world-building is efficient, and the ending delivers. It avoids the pitfalls of purple prose, forced metaphor, and over-explanation that plague lesser work in this mode. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two — Standing Around We stood around the crate for a long time. The forward hold of the *Pelican* is cold storage temperature, not because anything in it needs cold but because heating it costs money. The three of us stood in our jackets in a half-circle around the thing, and it knocked. Not constantly. It would knock — three, four times, a flat sound, palm or fist against the inside wall — and then stop, and we'd wait, and it would start again. "That's a person," Trick said. "You don't know that," I said. "Captain. That's a person knocking." "Could be livestock," Okoye said. "Could be a pump on a timer. Could be a recovery beacon with a loose arm." She didn't believe any of it. She was building the list out loud so we'd all have to hear how short it was. "It's sealed," I said. "We were paid not to open it." "We were paid," Okoye said, and let that sit. "Triple lane rate," I said, because she was going to do the math eventually and I'd rather hand it over than have it taken. "Half up front. Hard scrip." Okoye didn't say anything. She has a way of going still that is worse than most people's shouting. I watched her run the figures — what triple rate meant, what hard scrip meant, what *no manifest plus a sealed crate plus a captain who didn't mention the money* meant — and arrive where I'd already been standing for two days. "You knew it was dirty," she said. "I knew it paid." "That's the same sentence." The crate knocked. Four times, then quiet. "Okay," Trick said. "Okay, but we're opening it, right? We're standing here having a budget meeting and we're opening it. Because if that's a person, they've been in there since Transfer Six, and that's — that's a long time, Captain, that's air and water, that's—" "If it's a person," Okoye said, "then the second we crack that seal, this stops being a freight problem. Right now we're a hauler with a sealed unit and a destination. We don't know anything. That's worth something. You break the seal, you know things." "There is a *person knocking*—" "I'm not arguing the knocking. I'm arguing the order of operations." They went around it twice more. I'm not going to write it all out. The truth is I let them argue because it bought me a minute, and I spent the minute looking at the crate. Standard half-length unit, freight-office stencils, customs tags from Transfer Six that I now suspected were forged and well forged. And down low on one end, a line of small punched holes I'd taken for drainage when we loaded it. Drainage holes go on the bottom. "Trick," I said. "Get the bar." --- The seal was a freight seal over a second seal, and the second one was the kind you put on environmental units. Under the lid there was a rig — a life-pod conversion, somebody's competent ugly work, scrubber and water bladder and a heater strapped where heaters don't go, all of it wired to a battery flat enough that the gauge light had gone amber. The smell came up first. Eleven days of person in a small space. I've smelled worse, but not attached to anything I had to keep looking at. The girl was wedged into the corner of the pod with a stunner in both hands. It was a stripped unit, half its housing gone, wires bare, and she held it the way you hold a thing you've decided to use. She was small, dark-haired, the hair cut short and recent and badly, and her lips were cracked white and her eyes went between the three of us in a pattern — me, Okoye, Trick, the open lid, me — that wasn't panic. She was counting us. "Hey," Trick said, soft, the way he talks to the drive. "Hey, you're okay. You're okay, we've got water—" "Reseal it," the girl said. Nobody moved. Her voice was a ruin, two syllables and it cracked, and she said it again anyway. "Reseal it. You broke the seal. Aunt Dess paid for door-to-door, sealed unit, no inspection. You opening it is a breach. I'm not mad. I'm telling you what it is." "Kid," I said. "Your battery's amber." She looked at the gauge. I watched her do arithmetic on it — actual arithmetic, her lips moved — and watched the answer come out against her, and watched her decide not to care. "Then we should hurry," she said. Okoye crouched at the edge of the crate, unhurried, and reached in for the water bladder to check its level, and the girl bit her. Fast, no warning, teeth into the meat of the hand, and Okoye didn't snatch back — she lifted her arm slow with the kid's jaws still set in it, looked at her from close range, and said, "Good instincts. Wrong target." The girl let go. I think it was the not-flinching that did it. We got her out in stages, like unloading something fragile, which she fought the whole way while also cooperating, both at once, complaining about the breach, the liability, the depot at Carrow, citing the terms of an agreement she had plainly memorized off the underside of the lid. She drank the water we gave her in small disciplined sips when anyone could see her and gulped it when she thought nobody was looking. She would not give a surname. She gave "Senna" the way you give a serial number. "There's a bunk," Trick said. "Aft cabin, it's small, but—" "The bunks smell like coolant," she said, who had not been near the bunks. --- I went back down to the hold on the night cycle, before my sleep, to look at the rig again and think about a woman with shaking hands. The kid was in the crate. She'd put the lid on cock-eyed, leaving a gap, and she was asleep inside the pod with her ration bar — unopened — held in one fist, and the stunner gone, hidden somewhere already. Eleven days in a box and she'd climbed back into the box. I stood there a while. The hold was cold. I didn't move her.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of restrained, purposeful prose that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Prose Quality** The first-person voice is consistent and well-calibrated — laconic, observational, with a dry intelligence that never tips into self-congratulation. "She was building the list out loud so we'd all have to hear how short it was" is a genuinely good line: it characterizes Okoye efficiently while showing Mara's perceptiveness. The prose earns its flatness; it doesn't feel like laziness but like a chosen register. The chapter avoids the trap of over-poeticizing, with one notable exception: "the smell came up first. Eleven days of person in a small space. I've smelled worse, but not attached to anything I had to keep looking at" — this is effective, grounded, not forced. The single strong image the plan called for is the final one: Senna asleep in the crate with the lid cock-eyed, ration bar in fist. It lands cleanly and earns its weight without being over-explained. The chapter correctly ends on image rather than hook, as planned. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The argument about opening the crate is genuinely good — Okoye's "order of operations" framing, Trick's escalating distress, Mara's tactical silence. The crosstalk feels real. Senna's first words ("Reseal it") and her subsequent freight-terminology negotiation are exactly right for the character profile: transactional, proud, memorized from the lid. "I'm not mad. I'm telling you what it is" is a perfect character note. Okoye's "Good instincts. Wrong target" lands as written in the character profile and works in context. The dialogue misfires and crosstalk the plan called for are present and functional. **Character Consistency** All four characters behave in accordance with their profiles. Mara withholds the payment information and lets the argument run while she thinks — consistent with her habit of deciding for people and her discomfort with declaration. Okoye's cold reasoning ("you know things") is exactly her profile's "mistakes reasoning for the whole of rightness." Trick's warmth and conflict-avoidance ("Hey, you're okay") are right. Senna's negotiating-not-bonding, her arithmetic lip-movements, her return to the crate — all consistent with the profile's description of her treating kindness as an opening position and sleeping in the crate because it's the last thing her aunt arranged. **Chapter Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely: the argument about whose problem it is, Okoye doing the math on the scrip and going quiet, the life-pod rig, Senna biting Okoye, the negotiation about resealing, the name given as serial number, the refusal of the bunk, and the final image of Senna asleep in the crate. The plan's note that "she won't take a bunk" and "that night Mara finds her asleep back inside the open crate" is executed faithfully. The chapter ends "no hook, just the image" as specified. **Weaknesses and Criticisms** The chapter is not without flaws. The drainage-holes observation ("Drainage holes go on the bottom") is a bit too neat — a clever-detective moment that feels slightly constructed, though it's minor. The transition from the argument to "Trick, get the bar" is slightly abrupt in a way that works but could be smoother. The description of the life-pod rig is functional but slightly list-heavy: "scrubber and water bladder and a heater strapped where heaters don't go, all of it wired to a battery flat enough that the gauge light had gone amber" — this is competent but the most workmanlike passage in the chapter. The phrase "like unloading something fragile, which she fought the whole way while also cooperating, both at once" is good but the "both at once" is slightly redundant after the image already conveys it. Small issue. There's no purple prose to speak of. The metaphors that exist are grounded and earned. The chapter is not amateurish. The darkness (child in a box, cracked lips, amber battery gauge) is well-earned rather than deployed for effect. **Overall Assessment** This is a confident, disciplined chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterization, and delivers its emotional payload through restraint rather than declaration. The final image is genuinely affecting. The dialogue is the best element. The prose occasionally settles for functional when it could be more precise, but this is a minor complaint against a chapter that knows exactly what it's doing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three — Freight News The run from Transfer Six to Carrow is eleven days at the *Pelican*'s economical burn, fourteen at her actually-economical burn, and I'd plotted the fourteen before any of this, because the books had been bad. I mention it because it mattered later, and because for the first stretch of those days, almost nothing happened, and I want to put down what the nothing was like. The kid stole food. Not like a starved thing — like a quartermaster. Rations went missing in threes: three protein bars, three juice pouches, three of the little salt crackers Trick buys by the case and nobody else eats. Okoye found a cache behind the water reclaimer, neat as shelving, and left it alone, and a second one turned up in the forward hold inside a coil of strap webbing, and we left that alone too. At meals Senna ate standing in the corner of the galley with her back to the junction box, and watched us, and afterward took her plate to the sink and washed it like she was paying a bill. She talked when she had a correction to deliver. Trick called the forward hold "the bay" and she informed him a bay implies external doors and ours was a hold. Okoye logged a cargo shift as *settling* and Senna, reading over her shoulder uninvited, said settling was for granular freight and what we had was strap creep. The third day she asked what the ship was called, and Trick told her, and she looked at him for a moment and said, "Pelicans can't even leave atmosphere," and went back to her corner. Trick laughed about it for two days. He'd have given her the ship if she'd asked. At night she did figures. I heard it first on the fourth night, passing the hold on my way from the engine room. She was asleep in the crate with the lid cocked, and through the gap came her voice, small and flat and asleep: numbers. I stood and listened longer than I should have. *Four hundred and twenty acre-feet. Less seepage at six percent. Carryover to the dry quarter.* It had the shape of a prayer, the way anything does if you say it lying down in the dark, but it wasn't a prayer. It was a ledger. Somebody had taught that kid a ledger so thoroughly it ran on its own when she stopped guarding it, and I went back up to my cabin and did not write in mine. --- Trick took a call on the sixth day. I came around the corridor bend with a filter housing in my hands and his comm cubby door was half-shut, which on this ship is the loudest thing a door can do, and I heard his voice doing a thing it does — bright, agreeable, fast — and one phrase through the gap: *"—no, it's a real job, it's a contract job, I told you—"* and then he heard me, or felt me, and the brightness went up a register. I kept walking. I want to be honest that I kept walking for my own comfort, not his privacy. Trick owes money. Trick has always owed money. It was Maxa's operation on Transfer Six this time, I knew that much from the layover, and a man's debts are his own right up until they aren't, and I told myself we were eight days out from anywhere his problems could find us. He was humming when he came to dinner. I know what the humming means. I didn't ask. --- The freight office burned the night we left. I found it the way you find anything, not looking for it. We were in range of the Carrow lane's relay buoys by then, and the news packets come in stale and compressed, and I read them with my coffee the way I read manifests — for the rhythm of it. Page four of the Transfer Six bulletin: structure fire, dock annex, freight office, total loss. One line about an ongoing investigation, which on Transfer Six means no investigation. No casualties listed, which is not the same as no casualties. I sat with that. Then I went and pulled the bond registries, which any hauler can read and most don't. It was there. Posted two days after we undocked. *Asset recovery — biological materials, proprietary, removed from Combine custody in transit. Bonded recovery authorized. Halloran Combine, Claims & Reversions Division.* No description of the materials. A contact channel and a bond number and a fee that was not large, which frightened me more than a large one would have, because it meant they expected this to be easy. Claims and Reversions. I knew what that division did. I'm not going to get into how I knew. --- The pod lining gave it up. I'd gone back over the rig with a knife and a light, alone, on the eighth night while the kid slept her figures in the crate ten feet from me, and sewn flat into the insulation blanket was a folder of actual paper. A homestead compact — Cully's Rest, eight family signatures going back three generations, water schedules, the works. A genome-attestation card with a child's bloodline string on it and the words SIGNATORY HEIR. And a claims-office notice, the kind they print on grey stock so you know it's the government talking, stating the renewal terms: continuous habitation or signatory presence, verified at the claims beacon, no later than a date. I read the date three times. Then I did the arithmetic the way the kid would have, out loud, alone in the cold hold, because some numbers you have to hear. The date, less our departure from Transfer Six, less eleven days at economical burn. The window had closed nine days ago. She'd been in the crate when it closed. Whatever Aunt Dess had been racing, she'd lost the race before she ever found my ship, or she'd never done the math, or she'd done it and shipped the kid anyway because a sealed crate moving was better than a girl on Transfer Six standing still. I don't know. I never found out. I want that on the record: I never found out what the plan was, or if there was one. I sat on the deck next to the crate for a long time with the folder on my knees. The kid recited her water numbers in her sleep, allotments for land that wasn't hers anymore and hadn't been for nine days, and she didn't know, and I was the only soul in the universe who did. I'm not going to describe what I thought about. I put the folder back.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of restrained, laconic prose that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Craft** The first-person narrator voice is consistently maintained and genuinely distinctive. Mara's habit of withholding, her self-awareness about withholding, and her flat declarative style all come through cleanly. Lines like "I kept walking for my own comfort, not his privacy" and "I'm not going to get into how I knew" are exactly right — they characterize through omission, which is the plan's stated goal. The narrator's admission that she won't describe what she thought about at the end is earned because the chapter has established this as a genuine personality trait, not a writerly dodge. **Imagery and Metaphor** The plan calls for "one striking image per chapter, no more." The chapter largely honors this. The strongest image is Senna's sleep-recitation of water figures: "It had the shape of a prayer, the way anything does if you say it lying down in the dark, but it wasn't a prayer. It was a ledger." This is genuinely good — it earns its slight lyricism by immediately undercutting it. The image of Mara doing the arithmetic "out loud, alone in the cold hold, because some numbers you have to hear" is also effective. There's no purple prose to speak of; the writing is disciplined. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional. Senna's corrections ("a bay implies external doors") are perfectly in character — transactional, precise, slightly contemptuous. The fragment of Trick's call ("no, it's a real job, it's a contract job, I told you") does real work: it establishes the debt situation, the half-closed door, and Mara's deliberate non-intervention, all in a few words. The dialogue is not weak; it's appropriately minimal and each instance carries weight. **Plot and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the food-hoarding, Senna's corrections, the sleep-figures, Trick's half-closed comm call, the freight office fire, the bond registry, the pod lining revelation, and the devastating arithmetic. The structural choice to end on Mara sitting with the knowledge alone — "I'm not going to describe what I thought about. I put the folder back" — is exactly what the plan called for and it lands. **Character Consistency** Senna's quartermaster-style theft, her corrections, her sleep-figures — all consistent with the profile. Trick's half-closed door and the humming tell are used well. Okoye's brief appearance (leaving the cache alone) is characteristically unsentimental. Mara's voice is consistent throughout. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter's emotional core — Mara alone with the knowledge that the claim window closed while Senna was in the crate, that the girl has been reciting figures for land that's already gone — is genuinely affecting. The restraint makes it more so. The line "she didn't know, and I was the only soul in the universe who did" is the chapter's emotional peak, and it's earned rather than manufactured. **Weaknesses** A few minor issues: "I sat with that" is slightly flat even for this voice — it's a common enough phrase that it reads as filler. The transition from the bond registry to "Claims and Reversions. I knew what that division did. I'm not going to get into how I knew" is effective but the technique (withholding with a wink) is used twice in the chapter, and the second instance is slightly less fresh than the first. The section breaks (dashes) are functional but the chapter's rhythm is slightly uneven — the Trick section feels a touch rushed compared to the careful pacing of the food-hoarding opening and the revelation ending. **Creativity and Surprise** The chapter avoids the obvious moves. The freight office fire is introduced as a news item, not a dramatic revelation. The bond is described in bureaucratic language that's more frightening than any thriller prose would be. The revelation that the claim window closed while Senna was in the crate — that she was shipped as a document for a deed that had already expired — is genuinely devastating and handled with appropriate quietness. **Overall** This is skilled, disciplined work that honors its plan and its voice. It's not flashy, but it's not trying to be. The restraint is genuine rather than performed. The emotional weight is real. Minor quibbles about a few flat phrases and slight repetition of technique don't significantly diminish what is a very strong chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four — Crane The ship found us on the tenth day. It came up the lane behind us matching our burn exactly, which is a sentence — a coincidence overtakes you or falls behind; only intent matches. Okoye watched it on the scope through a full watch without saying anything, and then said, "Captain," in the tone she keeps for things that have stopped being theoretical. The hail came an hour later. Text first, very proper: *Bonded recovery vessel REMITTANCE requests voice contact, master to master.* Then the voice. "Captain Vance. My name is Eli Crane. I hold recovery bond four-four-one-seven, posted by Halloran Combine, Claims and Reversions. I'd like a few minutes of your time." I'd put us on the galley comm because the bridge panel crackles, and so the whole conversation happened at the table where we eat. Okoye stood at the counter. Trick sat across from me looking like a man at his own hearing. I'd sent the kid aft with a juice pouch and a job — inventory the strap webbing — and I will get to how that went. "Mr. Crane. I don't know anything about a bond." "No reason you should. I'll summarize. Eleven days ago a quantity of biological materials left Transfer Six without authorization. The Combine's traffic analysis flags four independent haulers in the window. Yours is the third I've contacted. The first two were clean and I was on my way inside an hour, which I mention so you know how this can go." His voice was flat and unhurried and came from nowhere in particular. He sounded like a man reading you your meter. "We're hauling general freight for the Carrow depot," I said. "I'm sure most of it is. Captain, I want to be straightforward about my situation, because I find it saves everyone time. I'm contracted for recovery of biological materials, proprietary. That's the language on my bond. If your manifest says otherwise, then one of us has been lied to, and my rates assume it's me — I bill verification hours either way. So the only question I need answered is whether you'll consent to a tally inspection at the next waystation. You will be compensated for the delay at lane rate. That's in the bond too." "And if I don't consent?" "Then I file that you didn't, and I keep matching your burn, and the Combine decides what it wants to pay for next. I don't board ships, Captain. Boarding is a different bond class and the men who hold it are not careful men. I'd rather neither of us met them." Okoye had not moved. Trick had his hands flat on the table like he was holding it down. "What are the materials?" I said. "Specifically." There was a pause with paper in it. Actual paper — I heard it turn. "Specifically. Biological assets pertaining to a reverted homestead claim, Cully's Rest, compact number eight-eight-one. The claim lapsed for want of signatory presence on the fourth of this month. Materials capable of attesting signatory status were removed from Combine custody before reversion processing completed. The bond language says *signatory remains*, Captain, which I took at face value when I posted it. Tissue. Records. That class of recovery. It's ugly work but it's filing-cabinet ugly, you understand. I've done a hundred of them." I want to be accurate about the next part because it happened fast and I've gone over it many times. The galley has two doors. The aft one was open, and the kid was in it. I don't know how long she'd been there — long enough. She had the juice pouch in one hand and the strap inventory in the other, an actual written list, she'd done the job, and her face had nothing on it at all, which is how I learned what nothing looks like on her. "The fourth," she said. Not loud. Crane heard it anyway. The comm carries. The silence on the line went on for a long time. Long enough that Okoye reached over and checked the channel was still up. It was. We could hear his cabin — a vent fan, the paper again, once. "Captain," Crane said finally, "how old." I didn't answer. "How old." "Eleven," I said. The fan ran. Trick had his eyes shut. The kid stood in the doorway doing arithmetic — I could see it, the lips, the carry — taking the date she'd just heard and running it against eleven days in a crate and a renewal deadline she'd known by heart her whole life the way other kids know their birthdays, and arriving, and I watched her arrive, and there was nothing I could do about the answer from across a galley. "Mr. Crane—" "I'm going to tell you what happens on my end," Crane said, same flat meter, maybe slower. "I contact the Claims and Reversions desk and inform them that the bond as posted misrepresents the recovery class. I file my verification hours. I refund the bond fee, in full, today. That's not a favor to you, Captain, and I want that understood before anyone wastes any gratitude. I have a rule about cargo that can be eleven, and the rule is for me, because in twenty-six years no recovery in that class has ever stayed inside its paperwork. Not one. I'm too old and too solvent." "And the Combine?" "Reposts the bond at a higher fee with the language cleaned up, and somebody takes it. Less careful men, like I said. That's not my problem and I'm not going to pretend it's not yours." A pause. "For whatever it's worth, the claim's dead, Captain. The fourth came and went. Whatever you're hauling her toward, it isn't there anymore. The Combine's already won. They're just tidying." The burn signature on the scope changed while he was still talking — the *Remittance* peeling off the lane, unhurried, a man finishing his coffee on the way out. "Good luck to you," Crane said. "I mean that in a general way." The channel closed. Nobody at the table moved. In the doorway, the kid looked down at her strap inventory, squared the pages against her leg twice, set the list on the counter where lists go, and walked aft. We heard the hold door. We heard the crate lid. She didn't do figures that night. I went down twice to check. The hold was cold and the crate was silent, and the silence was worse.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of controlled, laconic fiction that executes its plan with near-surgical precision. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Prose Style** The first-person narration maintains Mara's established voice throughout — flat, economical, occasionally self-interrupting in ways that feel earned rather than affected. "A coincidence overtakes you or falls behind; only intent matches" is a strong opening observation that works as both practical freight-lane logic and character revelation. The prose never overreaches. When it does reach — "a pause with paper in it" — it lands cleanly because it's specific and sensory rather than decorative. The chapter largely avoids the triadic closing lines and lyrical mortar the plan warned against. **Crane's Characterization** Crane is exactly what the profile demands: a process, not a person. His dialogue is metered, procedural, and genuinely unsettling in its blandness. "I bill verification hours either way" is chilling precisely because it's so administrative. His exit — "I mean that in a general way" — is perfect: a courtesy that never becomes warmth, exactly as specified. The detail of actual paper turning on the comm is a small, precise touch that does real work. **The Central Revelation** The chapter's emotional core — Senna in the doorway, hearing herself described as "signatory remains" — is handled with exceptional restraint. The model resists the temptation to editorialize. "Her face had nothing on it at all, which is how I learned what nothing looks like on her" is the chapter's best line: it's Mara's voice, it's specific to Senna's established character, and it carries enormous weight without announcing itself. The arithmetic detail — watching her "arrive" at the answer — is devastating and consistent with Senna's established idiosyncrasy of reciting figures. **Structural Execution** The plan called for "mostly one conversation" and the chapter delivers. The galley setting, the crew positioned around the table, the aft door open — these are established efficiently without feeling like stage directions. The chapter ends on the image of the silent crate, not a hook, exactly as planned. "She didn't do figures that night" is a perfect closing note: the absence of her compulsive arithmetic signals the depth of the wound without stating it. **Dialogue** The dialogue is strong throughout. Crane's lines are the standout — they feel genuinely written for a specific character rather than a generic antagonist. Mara's terse responses ("And if I don't consent?", "And the Combine?") are appropriately minimal. The one moment where the chapter slightly strains is "I want to be accurate about the next part because it happened fast and I've gone over it many times" — this is a slight tell, a narratorial intrusion that signals importance rather than trusting the scene to carry it. It's a minor flaw in an otherwise confident chapter. **Consistency with Plan and Profiles** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Crane hails, parleys, Senna overhears the key information, Crane exits without heroism. His departure is explicitly not a rescue — "That's not my problem and I'm not going to pretend it's not yours." Trick's body language (hands flat on the table, eyes shut) is consistent with his conflict-averse, expressive character. Okoye's stillness and single action (checking the channel) is exactly right for her profile. Senna's response — doing the inventory job she was given, then the arithmetic, then the silent retreat to the crate — is perfectly calibrated to her established character. **Minor Weaknesses** The line "I want to be accurate about the next part" is the only moment that feels slightly self-conscious. The chapter is otherwise remarkably clean. One could argue the chapter is almost too controlled — there's very little that surprises in terms of structure — but the plan called for this, and the execution is so precise that the restraint reads as mastery rather than timidity. **Overall** This is a chapter that does exactly what it sets out to do, does it with considerable craft, and trusts its reader throughout. The emotional darkness is entirely earned by the specificity of the scene rather than by authorial insistence. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five — The Quiet One Argues The kid stopped talking on the tenth day and I'm going to spend this part of the account on what that was like, because more happened in the not-talking than in anything Crane said. She came to meals. That surprised me — I'd braced for a hunger strike, for the crate lid staying shut, for having to make decisions about a child who wouldn't eat. Instead she came to the galley at the exact hour, took her plate, stood in her corner, ate all of it, washed the plate, and left. She answered direct questions in the smallest unit of language that would discharge them. Did she want more. "No." Was she warm enough in the hold. "Yes." Trick made the salt crackers into something — fried them in fat with the last of the onions, a thing that should not work and did — and set a plate of it at the end of the counter near her corner without a word, and she ate it, and washed the plate, and said nothing, and Trick looked like a man whose fire had gone out in the rain. Her caches turned up dismantled. Okoye found the one behind the reclaimer cleared, everything returned to ship's stores in threes, the count exact. I understood it before I wanted to. The hoarding had been logistics. Provisioning for a future. A person keeps stores against the dry quarter, and there wasn't one anymore. On the eleventh night I went down to the hold and sat on a crate near hers and didn't say anything, which was the only approach I had confidence in. After a while her voice came through the gap in the lid, awake this time, flat. "You knew before he said it." I thought about lying. "Yes. Two days." A long nothing. Then: "The seepage figures are wrong anyway. Dad's six percent was for the old liner. They redid the liner the year I was seven. It's closer to four." A pause. "It doesn't matter.
