He loses an afternoon: comes to standing in the yard at dusk, hammer in hand, three new deck boards laid, no memory. Blames the gabapentin. A fight about the thermostat that's really about nothing — petty, lived-in, both of them unkind. That night the chair is empty and somehow that's worse. **Chapter 3 — The Drawer.** Maren at her sister's overnight — first time she's left him alone, and he hears her on the porch telling Iris *one night, he'll be fine, he's been quiet*. He searches. The locked drawer in her sewing room: a notebook of dates and durations (*Jan 9 — 4 min. Jan 26 — 11 min. Asked about Hollis*), a small voice recorder, a hospice invoice he doesn't read closely because the recorder frightens him more. He plays thirty seconds: darkness, breathing, and Maren's voice, soft, asking a question — and a voice answering that he doesn't recognize and decides, because he must, is hers gone wrong. He builds the case: she is possessed, she's been logging *her* episodes, the salt keeps something in, not out. He locks the drawer and that night does something he'll be ashamed of: stages a test, pretends to sleep, then sits up suddenly and grabs her wrist in the dark to see what looks out of her. What looks out of her is fear. Of him. **Chapter 4 — A Good Day.** No horror beats. They drive to the lake house at Stockton because the weather breaks. Maren laughs at something dumb he says about the bait shop. Sandwiches, cold hands, a memory of their honeymoon that they tell each other in alternating pieces like always. He nearly tells her everything — the drawer, his fear — and doesn't, because the day is too good and he is suddenly, desperately unwilling to lose it. One small wrongness, unremarked: she watches him skip stones with an expression he files under love. Drive home at dusk. He sleeps in the car. (On reread: whose day was it? The notebook will later show a gap here. He never finds out, and neither do we.) **Chapter 5 — Eleven Minutes.** The crack. He goes back for the recorder while she showers, listens with headphones, properly this time. The wrong voice is answering in *his* cadences, his lake-house stories, slightly off — and then his own voice, unmistakably his, breaking in confused: *Maren? What time is it? Why is it dark?* — and her, steady, practiced: *It's okay. It's Tuesday. You're home.* Date on the file matches a gap in his memory. He checks the notebook against everything: the durations are *his* lost time inverted — the entries log the minutes he was *present*. The hospice invoice, read closely now: his name, a date twenty months ago, the words he can't make mean anything else. The chapter ends in the bathroom, gripping the sink — no mirror-lag theatrics, just his own face, ordinary, and the question of who shaved it this morning. **Chapter 6 — What He Can Piece Together.** No confession. He confronts Maren obliquely at dinner — puts the notebook on the table. She doesn't explain; she goes very still and then very practical, talking about his medication, and only when he says the date from the invoice out loud does she stop pretending. What she gives him is broken and partial: *I couldn't do it. Iris said — it doesn't matter what Iris said. You were already gone in there, Tom, you were gone weeks before.* No mechanics, no terms. She won't say what she did or to whom. He fills gaps himself, some surely wrong. Worst discovery is on the recorder, that night, a file from months ago: Maren talking softly, comfortably, to the body when he isn't in it — easy, domestic, laughing at something it said. He feels jealousy of his own hands. He realizes she stopped crying in the chair a long time ago and he doesn't know what the staring is *for* anymore — and when he asks her, she can't answer. **Chapter 7 — The Other Husband.** Tom's windows shorten — three minutes, five — and the narration shows it: scenes that cut off mid-paragraph, small contradictions (he says it's Thursday; the mail says Saturday). He experiences the other one from inside: watching his hands finish the deck, neatly, patiently; hearing his mouth tell Maren goodnight in his voice. It isn't cruel. It does the dishes. It remembers their anniversary, which Tom, twenty months dead, had forgotten. Iris visits once — won't come past the porch, looks at him too long, says only *Does she know you're you right now?* and leaves before he can answer. In a window, he asks Maren to end it. She says she doesn't know how anymore, or doesn't say it's that she won't, and he can't tell the difference, and the chapter ends with him surfacing in the chair — *he* is in the chair, at 3 a.m., watching Maren sleep, and he doesn't know how long he's been doing it or who started. **Chapter 8 — The Window.** Tom decides the one thing his minutes can hold. Not a ritual — he doesn't know any. He understands, or believes, that the thing stays because she keeps it: the watching, the notebook, the recorder, the whole apparatus of vigil is the tether (he may be wrong; we never learn). In a window, he burns the notebook in the kitchen sink and erases the recorder, and writes her a note in his own hand — plain, clumsy, not eloquent — telling her to let him sleep. He doesn't get to finish the note. The narration cuts. One last surfacing, brief, days or weeks later, no way to tell: morning light, the smell of coffee that tastes like coffee, Maren across the kitchen with her back to him, not watching. He doesn't know if the thing is gone, or if it's him that's going, or whether the note worked or she simply chose. He watches her butter toast. The story ends inside the window, on the ordinary kitchen, before we learn whether the eyes that opened this morning were his — ends mid-vigil, his this time, holding still so she won't turn around.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Tom Calloway **Background.** Fifty-four. Grew up in Joplin, Missouri, son of a high school shop teacher who never praised anything until it was finished — which is why the half-finished deck bothers Tom more than it should, in ways he can't articulate. Worked twenty-six years as an estimator for a commercial roofing company: a job built on walking other people's buildings and pricing what it costs to keep weather out. He liked it. He was good at small certainties — square footage, material costs, what a thing should take and what it actually takes. Met Maren at thirty-one when she was cutting his sister-in-law's hair; he came in to drop off keys and stayed forty minutes. No kids — they tried for a while in their late thirties, then quietly stopped trying, and quietly stopped talking about having stopped. The pancreatic cancer was diagnosed at fifty-two. He doesn't remember the hospice. He doesn't remember most of the last year of being alive, which the version of him narrating this story attributes to the illness and the medications, because the alternative hasn't occurred to him yet. **Personality & flaws.** Mild on the surface, watchful underneath. A noticer — of gutters, of grout, of people's tells — but a noticer who keeps his findings to himself until they've fermented into something worse. His worst marital habit, long predating the illness: he doesn't ask, he *tests*. If he suspects Maren forgot his birthday plans, he won't mention the date; he'll wait and watch her fail. This trait, harmless-petty in a healthy marriage, becomes the engine of the story's cruelest scenes. He's not brave so much as stubborn. He has a dry, deflating humor that surfaces at wrong moments. He is also — and this matters — a man who has always quietly believed he loved Maren more than she loved him, an old insecurity with no real evidence behind it, which the discovery of the recordings will feed like gasoline. **Idiosyncrasies.** Eats the same breakfast for years at a stretch, then switches without comment. Can't sleep without his back to the wall side of the bed. Keeps a coffee can of orphaned screws and fasteners in the garage, organized by no system anyone else can detect. Does arithmetic when he's anxious — squares of numbers, material estimates for rooms he's sitting in. Calls the dog "old man" and never by his name. **Physicality.** Big-framed but going soft, the body of a man who walked job sites for decades and then stopped. Since the illness: twenty pounds lighter, clothes fitting like they're borrowed. Moves deliberately, hands first — touches doorframes, counters, the truck hood, as if confirming things are still solid. Resting expression reads as mild skepticism. His hands are his most expressive feature and the thing Maren watches. **Motives & relationships.** Wants, in order of his own awareness: to feel like himself again, to protect Maren, to understand what's wrong in the house. Beneath all of it, unawares: to matter, to not be replaceable. Loves Maren with the specific gravity of a long marriage — not romantic so much as load-bearing. Distrusts Iris mildly and always has (she's the sister who said, audibly, at the wedding, *he's a bit beige, isn't he?*). Misses the dog more than he admits. **Voice.** - *"The salt was for ants, she said. I didn't say anything. It was February. Ants don't come in February. I filed it where I file things."* - *"I figured the square footage of the bedroom while she watched me. Eleven by thirteen. A hundred forty-three. You can know a number's right and still want to count it again."* - *"I'm not a man who grabs his wife's wrist in the dark. I want that written down somewhere. I want someone to check."* # Maren Calloway **Background.** Fifty-one. Grew up in Carthage above her parents' laundromat, the younger and supposedly flightier of two sisters — a reputation she's resented for forty years, because Iris got to be "the responsible one" by being two years older and louder. Cut hair for three decades; owns a two-chair shop she's been letting an employee mostly run since Tom got sick. Raised vaguely Baptist, lapsed at nineteen, but kept a magpie streak for the folk-practical: her grandmother's habits — salt, certain words said over certain things — which she half-believed the way you half-believe weather superstitions, right up until the hospice, when half-belief became the only door left. What she actually did, she will never fully say, and the story will never fully know. Iris helped her find it and has not slept properly since. **Personality & flaws.** Practical to the point of armor. A talker by trade — thirty years of small talk over scissors — who deploys chatter as concealment; her real tells are silences. She manages dread by *administering* it: the notebook, the logged minutes, the recorder. The vigil began as devotion and has calcified into procedure, and she knows it, and hates knowing it. Her deepest flaw is the one she can't look at: some days the other one is *easier*. It doesn't test her. It doesn't go quiet for three days over a forgotten errand. It thanks her for dinner. She has caught herself laughing with it and felt like an adulteress in her own kitchen, with her own husband's body, and there is no one alive she can tell this to — not Iris, who'd take it as proof, and certainly not Tom, who only exists eleven minutes at a time and deserves better minutes than that. **Idiosyncrasies.** Cuts Tom's hair herself, always has — lately she does it in near silence and stands behind him longer than the work requires. Hums when she's lying; goes quiet when she's telling the truth. Keeps her grandmother's thimble in her apron pocket and touches it like a worry stone. Writes in the notebook in the same shorthand she used for salon appointments — durations, initials, a private economy of grief. **Physicality.** Small, quick, capable hands with a hairdresser's permanent slight stoop and strong forearms. Gray growing out in a deliberate stripe she refuses to call a choice. Reads people's faces in mirrors out of professional habit — looks at reflections more naturally than at people. Tired in the specific way of long caretaking: not haggard, *maintained*, like a house kept clean because cleaning is the only thing that obeys. **Motives & relationships.** Began wanting one thing: not to lose him. Now wants something she can't name — for the arrangement to hold, or to end, or to never have been, depending on the hour. Loves Tom; loved him before, loves what's left, and can no longer cleanly separate the two, which is the true shape of her haunting. Owes Iris everything and can barely stand to be in a room with her. Sent the dog away because Hollis would not stop growling at the body, and she couldn't bear what that meant said out loud nightly. **Voice.** - *"It's Tuesday. You're home. I've got coffee on."* (Said so many times, to both of them, that it's worn smooth as a key.) - *"Iris asks me how I do it. Like there's a how. You do the next thing. Then the next thing. You don't look down."* - *"He used to test me. Little traps, all our marriage, to see if I loved him right. Now I sit in that chair and run the same test every night, and I don't know anymore which answer I'm hoping for."* # Iris Dunmore **Background.** Fifty-three. Maren's older sister, widowed young, one grown daughter (Casey, twenty-five, lives with her again, between things). Worked county records administration her whole career — a life of documents, of things being officially so. Was the sister who actually kept their grandmother's practices alive past childhood, half as heritage-hobby, half in earnest, which is why Maren came to her twenty months ago, and why Iris will carry to her grave the knowledge that she could have said no and didn't. She said no for two days. Then she watched Maren sit in the hospice parking lot for six hours and gave her what she asked for. **Personality & flaws.** Blunt, organized, frightened — in that order. Copes by withdrawal: stopped visiting the Calloway house within a month, took the dog, screens Maren's calls and then calls back at safe hours. Her bluntness is real but her courage is selective; she can say a hard thing on a porch and then not cross the threshold behind it. Guilt has made her religious again in a hedging, transactional way. She loves her sister past reason, which is precisely the problem, since past reason is where this all happened. **Idiosyncrasies.** Won't say Tom's name — says *him*, *the house*, *over there*. Keeps Hollis inside when anyone visits. Has read everything obtainable about what they did and understood almost none of it, a records administrator defeated for the first time by missing documentation. **Physicality.** Taller and broader than Maren, iron-gray hair kept short, reading glasses on a beaded chain she clicks when agitated. Stands in doorways with a hand on the frame — always positioned to close something. **Motives & relationships.** Wants it undone and is terrified of what undoing costs; wants her sister back from the vigil almost more than she wants Tom at rest. Keeps Casey away from all of it, fiercely. Her one unforgivable thought, visited nightly: *it should have been allowed to end in that hospice, and I am the reason it didn't.* **Voice.** - *"You shouldn't be here. No — I'm not saying it to be cruel. I'm saying you* shouldn't be. *Anywhere. Do you understand me?"* - *"She tells me he's quiet, like that's good news. Quiet. Lord. The laundromat was quiet after Daddy died too. Empty buildings generally are."* # The Other One (unnamed) **Background & nature.** Whatever Maren's grandmother's words reached, twenty months ago, in a hospice room in Carthage. It has no backstory the narrative will disclose, and that refusal is deliberate — no rules, no mythology, no name. What can be characterized is its *behavior*, because Tom watches it from inside and Maren watches it from across the table. **Personality, such as it is.** It is diligent. It learned Tom from Tom — his cadences, his lake-house stories, his arithmetic habit (it does the calculations *correctly*, which Tom notices with something like nausea) — and it performs him with the faint over-fidelity of a careful student: remembers the anniversary Tom forgot, finishes the deck with cleaner joinery than Tom's, thanks Maren for meals Tom would have eaten in silence. It is not cruel and never menacing, which is the horror: it cannot be caught doing anything wrong except *being*. Whether it loves Maren or renders love the way it renders a deck joint is the question the story will not answer. Its few divergences from Tom are small and dreadful: it doesn't test people. It doesn't sulk. It sleeps on its back, dead center of the bed. **Physicality.** Tom's body, used slightly better — steadier hands, no touching of doorframes, no confirming the world is solid. It is sure of things. Maren can tell them apart in seconds and could not explain how to anyone, and lately, in low light, takes longer than seconds. **Voice.** It speaks rarely on the page, always plausibly: - *"Coffee's good this morning."* (Tom never said that. Tom drank coffee like it was a duty. Maren noticed; said nothing.) - *"You should sleep, Mar. I'm not going anywhere."* (True, the way it says everything: simply, and without comfort.)

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One — The Chair I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping. Three twelve by the clock on the dresser. The furnace had cycled off and the house was doing its February settling, ticking down the hallway like something walking away. Maren was in the chair by the window. She'd pulled it around weeks ago — to read in, she said, though I never saw a book in her lap — and she sat in it now with her hands folded and her face toward me, and she was not reading. I kept my breathing slow. I don't know why. You'd think a man who wakes to find his wife of twenty-three years watching him sleep would say something — *hey*, or *can't sleep?*, or anything human at all. But I lay there with my eyes open a slit and breathed like a sleeping man, and she watched me, and the furnace ticked, and neither of us was honest about being awake. After a while she whispered something. Too quiet to carry. Two words, maybe three. Then she sat back and the chair creaked and she stayed there until I actually did fall asleep, which took a long time, and felt like a kind of losing. In the morning she was at the stove and the kitchen smelled like bacon and the radio was doing the farm report, and she said, "There's coffee," and I said, "Thanks," and that was the whole conversation about it. That's how it goes with us. We have a place we put things instead of saying them, and the place was getting full. I should explain about me. Twenty months ago I was sick. Sick enough that I don't remember most of it — the doctors said to expect that, the gaps, though when I try to picture which doctor said it, in what room, wearing what face, there's nothing there. Just the fact of it, smooth and round like a creek stone: *memory loss is normal. Expect gaps.* I came home lighter by thirty pounds with a pill organizer the size of a tackle box, and Maren took a step back from the shop to look after me, and we have been doing this since. Recovering. That's the word we use. She uses it more than I do. The coffee tasted like nothing. It has for a long while now — not bad, not bitter, just absent, like drinking warm. I stopped mentioning it around Thanksgiving because every time I did, Maren would go still in a way I didn't like, and then say something about my medications, and hum while she said it. So now I drink it and say thanks. A hundred small treaties like that. That's a marriage, or it's ours anyway. The mail came at ten. Two bills and a seed catalog addressed to E. Crandall, who owned this house before us and has been dead eleven years. We've lived here nine and the catalogs still come, spring and fall, faithful as geese. Burpee's finest, for a man under the ground at Park Cemetery. I used to find it funny. Lately it sits different — somebody's name still arriving at a door they're never coming back to — but I put it in the recycling like always and didn't examine the feeling. I spent the afternoon in the garage with the space heater, sorting the screw can. I wasn't sorting it for any reason. It's a thing my hands do while the rest of me works on something else, and what the rest of me was working on was the chair, and the whisper, and how many nights this had been going on. I tried to count. That was the trouble — I couldn't get the count to hold still. I could remember catching her Tuesday. I could remember another time that felt like a week ago, frost on the window behind her, her face lit blue-gray by the yard light. And then earlier ones that went soft when I reached for them, the way the hospice months go soft, and I stood there with a handful of drywall screws thinking, *eleven by thirteen is a hundred forty-three*, which is the bedroom, which is a number I know is right and wanted to count again anyway. Hollis would've been some help, is a thing I thought. The dog. He lives at Iris's now — Maren's sister — on account of allergies Maren developed two winters ago, sudden, the way she tells it. Fifteen years with that dog and no allergies, and then one winter her eyes couldn't take it. I drove out to Iris's in October to see him and he stood at the far end of the yard and looked at me, and Iris kept her hand on the gate the whole visit, and nobody had a good time. Old man, I called him, *come here, old man*, and he didn't. At supper Maren talked about the shop — Dana wants to put in a third chair, the water heater's going — and I watched her hands while she talked. She has quick hands, strong in the forearm, thirty years of scissors. They were steady. She looked fine. She looked like my wife eating pork chops on a Thursday, and I thought: you're building something out of nothing, Tom. A tired woman sat in a chair. Married people watch each other sleep. It's probably tender, if you think about it right. I cleared the plates. Through the window over the sink the yard was going dark, and I could see the deck. Here is the thing about the deck. It comes off the back door, framed and half-decked, joists bare on the north side, a stack of boards under a tarp weighted with bricks. Good work, what's done of it. Square. And I don't remember starting it. I've stood out there twice now with my coffee, looking at cuts I'd have been proud to make, and tried to summon the day I rented the post-hole digger, the trip to the lumberyard, anything. Smooth stone. *Expect gaps.* I told myself it was last fall, before the weather, when the medications were heavier. I told myself a man doesn't forget a thing his own hands did. I told myself both of those, and they don't fit together, and I put them in the place we put things. That night I went to bed at ten and lay on my side, back to the wall like always, and waited. I'm not proud of it. There are men who'd have just asked. But I've always been a man who runs the test instead, who waits to see what the other person does when they think no one's watching — Maren could tell you, it's the worst thing in me and she's had twenty-three years of it. The furnace cycled off at quarter to twelve. At one ten, the hall floor gave its particular creak, third board from the door. I breathed slow. The chair took her weight in the dark.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished opening chapter. The prose achieves something genuinely difficult: it establishes a domestic horror atmosphere through accumulation of small, precise details rather than through any overt supernatural machinery. The voice is consistent, controlled, and convincing throughout. The opening line executes the prompt perfectly — it's the first sentence, and it immediately establishes the central dynamic of surveillance and counter-surveillance. The chapter then earns its dread through patience. The prose style is exactly what the plan called for: plain, concrete, declarative. "Just the fact of it, smooth and round like a creek stone" is one of the few lyric moments, and it lands because it's earned by the surrounding plainness. The arithmetic habit ("eleven by thirteen is a hundred forty-three") is deployed naturally, not as a tic but as a genuine character reveal — a man who counts things to feel certain when certainty is slipping. This is showing, not telling. The E. Crandall detail is masterful — a name still arriving at a door they're never coming back to — and the narrator's observation that "it sits different" without over-explaining why is exactly the kind of reread value the plan aimed for. The reader who knows the truth will feel this land differently. The dog scene is quietly devastating. Hollis standing at the far end of the yard, Iris keeping her hand on the gate — this communicates volumes without explanation. The "old man" call that goes unanswered is genuinely affecting. The deck is introduced with perfect restraint. The narrator's two rationalizations that "don't fit together" are placed with surgical precision. The chapter plan called for Tom to notice the deck and not remember starting it, and this is executed with exactly the right amount of weight — not overplayed, not underplayed. The self-awareness about his worst trait — "I've always been a man who runs the test instead" — is character work of a high order. It's confessional without being maudlin, and it sets up the later cruelty of Chapter 3 with genuine foreshadowing. The ending is restrained and effective. "The chair took her weight in the dark" — this is the right note to end on. It doesn't reach for a gut-punch; it simply closes the loop of the opening. If there are weaknesses, they are minor. "Felt like a kind of losing" is slightly soft — "losing" is vague where the rest of the prose is specific. "The place was getting full" is a touch more writerly than the surrounding prose, though it doesn't break the spell. The farm report radio detail is perhaps slightly generic. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is exactly right for this chapter. "There's coffee" / "Thanks" — this is the whole conversation about it, and the narrator's gloss on it ("That's how it goes with us") is earned rather than explanatory. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the 3:12 a.m. opening, the feigned sleep, the whisper, the morning ordinariness, the illness backstory, the E. Crandall catalogs, the dog at Iris's, the deck, the test-running character flaw, and the ending with him waiting in bed. All present, all executed with craft rather than mechanical compliance. The horror here is entirely earned through intimacy. There are no cheap moves. The darkness is well-earned because it emerges from the texture of a real marriage rendered with specificity and care. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two — Housekeeping The salt I found on a Sunday. I'd dropped a sock crossing the bedroom and when I bent for it there was a line of white along the threshold, fine as sugar, swept tight against the doorjamb where the vacuum wouldn't take it unless you meant to. I ran my finger through it and tasted it. Salt. "Ants," Maren said, when I asked. She didn't look up from the laundry she was folding. "They come in along the door sweep. Salt turns them back." "Huh," I said. It was February ninth. The yard had been frozen for a month. I have lived in Missouri my whole life and I have never once seen an ant in February, and Maren grew up over a laundromat in Carthage and knows more about household pests than any exterminator, and she folded a towel in thirds and hummed something under her breath, low, three or four notes. I filed it where I file things. But the cabinet was getting full, like I said, and things had started touching other things in there. So I began keeping track. I want to be straight about what that means, because there's a version of it that sounds reasonable — a man noticing his circumstances — and a version that's a man spying on his wife, and I was somewhere on the road between them and moving. I noted the salt. I noted that there was salt at the bedroom door and the back door but not the front, and I stood at the front door a while trying to make that mean something and couldn't. I noted the smell in the bathroom some mornings, burnt and green at once, like someone had lit a match to a weed. Lavender, she'd have said, if I'd asked. I didn't ask. I was past asking and into watching, which is its own confession. Wednesday I came in from the garage and she was on the phone in the kitchen. She had her back to me but the floor announced me — third board, same as the hall — and she said, "I've got to go, there's a pot on," and hung up. There was no pot on. We stood in the kitchen with no pot on, both of us looking at the stove like it might back her up. "Iris?" I said. "Mm." She was already moving, wiping a counter that was clean. "She wants help with Casey's taxes." It's a fact about my wife that she hums when she's lying. I don't think she knows it. Twenty-three years and I've never told her, because it's been useful, which tells you something about me that I'd rather it didn't. She wasn't humming now, though. She'd gone quiet, dishrag going in circles, and her quiet is a different thing altogether, and I didn't know yet what it meant. I know now. Quiet is when she's telling the truth, or near it. There was a pot on somewhere. It just wasn't on the stove. Thursday I lost the afternoon. I can tell it the way I pieced it, which is not the same as remembering. After lunch I sat in the recliner with the crossword. The clue I remember is *four across, river to the Caspian*. Then it was dusk. I was standing in the yard with no jacket on, cold to the bone the way you get when you've been cold a long time without noticing, and there was a hammer in my hand. My breath was steaming. Down at the deck, three new boards were laid and fastened, screwed clean on the lines, the cordless drill sitting on the joist with the bit still in it. The light in the kitchen window was on, and Maren was at the sink, and she was watching me through the glass. Not alarmed. Not waving me in out of the cold. Just watching, the way you'd watch weather. I came in and she said, "You were out there a while," in a voice flat as a level, and I said, "Lost track of time," and we ate supper. Gabapentin. That's what I told myself, lying awake doing arithmetic. They warn you about it — drowsiness, confusion, the leaflet says, and the leaflet is in the tackle box with all the others, and I've read it so many times I could recite it. A man takes what I take, a man was as sick as I was, an afternoon can slide. The screws were driven straight, though. Every one of them. I'm a fair hand with a drill but I was cold-stiff and apparently working in twilight, and there wasn't a stripped head or a proud screw in the lot, and I lay there doing the math on three boards — cuts, placement, fastening — and the math kept coming out to a man who knew what he was doing the whole time. Friday we fought about the thermostat. I'm not going to pretend it was about something else, because at the time it wasn't — it was sixty-six degrees in that house and I'd raised it twice and twice it had gone back down, and I said something about not living in a meat locker to save four dollars, and she said something about my mother's house, which was a thing she knew the weight of, and I said fine and put on a sweater in a way that I made sure she heard. Stupid. Married-people stupid, the fight you've had forty times and could do as a radio play. And there was a comfort in it, honest to God. Standing in the kitchen being genuinely irritated with my genuinely irritating wife about four dollars — it felt like the realest thing that had happened all month. No salt in it, no chairs in the dark. Just us. She caught my arm as I went past her at the doorway. Didn't say sorry — we don't, right away, we orbit a while first — but she pressed her thumb into my sleeve, into the muscle of my forearm, hard, like she was checking something under the cloth, and then she let me go. That night the chair was empty. I lay with my back to the wall and waited for the third board. Eleven o'clock. Midnight. The furnace cycled and ticked itself down the hall, and I caught myself straining for the creak the way you'd strain for a heartbeat, and I want to set down what I felt when it never came, because it surprised me then and shames me a little now. Not relief. You get used to a thing being there in the dark. A train past the house, a dog on the foot of the bed. Take it away and the quiet has a hole in it. I'd been watched for — weeks, months, I couldn't get the count to hold still — and one night without it and I lay in our bedroom at two in the morning feeling like a door left open, like a thing unattended, and somewhere down the hall my wife was sleeping, or wasn't, and I almost got up to go and check on her. Almost went and sat in a chair to do it. I want that written down too.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of domestic horror writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Craft** The first-person voice is exceptionally well-realized. Tom's cadences are exactly as specified: plain declaratives, hard nouns, a dry deflating humor that surfaces at wrong moments. "I filed it where I file things. But the cabinet was getting full, like I said, and things had started touching other things in there" — this is a genuinely original metaphor for psychological accumulation that earns its place without straining. The voice never breaks character. The arithmetic anxiety ("the math kept coming out to a man who knew what he was doing the whole time") is perfectly deployed — it's both characterization and horror delivery simultaneously. **Execution of the Chapter Plan** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the salt discovery, the phone call ended on entry, the lost afternoon with the deck boards, the thermostat fight, the empty chair. Every beat lands. The plan called for "petty, lived-in, both of them unkind" in the thermostat fight, and the chapter delivers exactly that — "something about my mother's house, which was a thing she knew the weight of" is a devastating small detail that implies decades of marital history without explaining it. The plan's note that "the empty chair is somehow worse" is executed with real psychological acuity: Tom's near-compulsion to go sit in the chair himself is the chapter's best moment. **Character Consistency** Tom's "noticer who keeps findings to himself until they've fermented" quality is perfectly rendered. His self-awareness about his own surveillance ("somewhere on the road between them and moving") is exactly the mild-surface, watchful-underneath personality specified. Maren's tell — humming when lying, silence when telling truth — is introduced with the right amount of subtlety, and Tom's admission that he's never told her because "it's been useful" is a small, damning character revelation consistent with his profile. The detail about her pressing her thumb into his forearm "like she was checking something under the cloth" is chilling and consistent with her vigil-keeper role. **Horror Mechanics** The horror here works through accumulation and implication rather than event. The lost afternoon is the chapter's most overtly supernatural moment, and it's handled with admirable restraint — the horror is in the arithmetic, in the "proud screw" detail, in the quality of the workmanship. The chapter never explains; it just presents evidence and lets Tom (and the reader) draw conclusions. The Gabapentin rationalization is exactly right — plausible enough to hold, thin enough to see through. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is correct for this story. "I've got to go, there's a pot on" followed by the observation that there was no pot on is economical and effective. The thermostat fight is rendered mostly in summary, which is the right choice — we get the texture without the tedium. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly flawless in execution, but a few minor observations: "flat as a level" is a slightly too-neat simile for a narrator who otherwise avoids figurative language — it's good, but it's the kind of thing that could tip into self-conscious craftedness. The "door left open, like a thing unattended" construction at the end is slightly more lyric than the voice typically allows, though it earns its place given the emotional weight of the moment. These are minor quibbles. **The Ending** The final line — "Almost went and sat in a chair to do it" — is the chapter's masterstroke. It's the moment where Tom's vigil-watching of Maren and Maren's vigil-watching of him collapse into each other, and it arrives without announcement or underlining. This is exactly the kind of reread value the plan called for. The horror of the possessed man almost performing the possessed man's wife's ritual is delivered with complete restraint. **Overall Assessment** This is sophisticated, disciplined horror writing that trusts its reader completely. The prose is clean without being spare to the point of affectation. The characterization is layered. The horror is earned through domestic specificity rather than atmosphere-signaling. It follows its plan faithfully while feeling organic rather than schematic. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three — The Drawer The first night she'd left me alone in twenty months. I heard them on the porch — Iris had driven in to get her, wouldn't come past the steps, and the storm door has a gap that carries voices into the hall whether you want it to or not. "One night," Maren was saying. "He'll be fine. He's been quiet." Whatever Iris said back, I didn't catch. Just the pitch of it. You can hear a question without hearing the words, and you can hear what kind of question. Then Maren: "Because I can't keep asking Dana to close. One night, Iris. I'll have my phone." *He's been quiet.* A man can stand in his own hallway and hear his wife say that about him in the voice you'd use for weather, or a dog, or a pot that might boil over, and then walk into the kitchen and kiss her goodbye and say drive safe. That's what I did. Her cheek was cold already from the open door. Iris looked at me from the bottom step the whole time, glasses chain clicking, and didn't say my name, and I stood in the doorway and watched the taillights go and then I went and got the flashlight. I'm not going to dress it up. I searched my own house like a burglar. The sewing room was hers the way the garage is mine, and I want it understood I'd respected that for nine years. Her grandmother's machine, the fabric shelves, the long table. And the cabinet under the window with the shallow drawer that locks — it came with the machine, old oak, and I'd never once wondered what was in it because the answer was obviously bobbins. It was locked. Bobbins don't get locked. The key took me forty minutes and I found it where I should have looked first, in the pocket of her apron hanging on the door, down with the thimble she worries like a rosary. Small brass key. My hands weren't steady and I want that on the record too — I stood there with the key in the lock for a good while, because there is a last moment before you know a thing, and some part of me was old enough to recognize it. Inside: a notebook, a hospice invoice folded in thirds, and a voice recorder. The notebook was the kind she used at the shop, spiral, grease pencil mark on the cover. Her shorthand in it — I know her shorthand, near enough, dates and durations, the way she'd log appointments. Except these went back twenty months and the entries were short. *Jan 9 — 4 min.* *Jan 26 — 11 min. Asked about Hollis.* *Feb 2 — 6 min. Bad. Don't leave the porch light off again.* *Feb 14 — 2 min.* Pages of it. Some weeks dense, some weeks nothing. Durations, never longer than maybe fifteen minutes, mostly shorter. Some entries just a dash. One, back in the fall, said *lake?* with a question mark, and one in December said *he counted the ceiling tiles out loud* and I stood there in my wife's sewing room while the furnace ticked and could not make myself understand what I was reading, except that something in my chest already did, and was getting loud. Four minutes of what. Eleven minutes of what. The invoice I barely looked at. Hospice letterhead, Carthage, a date, columns of billing — I registered my own name on it and my eyes slid off the way they slide off your own face in a group photo, too fast, and I folded it back in thirds. I have asked myself since why I didn't read it then, sitting right there in my hands, and the honest answer is the recorder frightened me more, and a man only has so much, and I spent mine on the wrong thing. The recorder was a cheap digital one, the kind they sell for students. Dozens of files. Dated. I played thirty seconds of one from January. Dark — you could hear the dark, if that makes sense, the particular dead air of our bedroom with the furnace off. Breathing, slow. Then Maren's voice, very soft, very close to the microphone, the way you'd talk in church or to something sleeping: "Can you hear me? It's me. It's just me." And a voice answered her. I want to be careful here, because I've turned this over more nights than I can count, what I actually heard versus what I decided. It was low. It was wrong — wrong the way a familiar room is wrong with one piece of furniture moved, nothing you can point at. It said a few words I couldn't make out and the cadence of it went through me like cold water, and I stopped the file with my thumb jammed on the button, and I sat down on her sewing chair because my legs asked me to. And then I built it. Right there in the dark with a flashlight, I built the whole thing in about four minutes, the way you'd frame a wall — fast, because every piece I had fit. The staring at night. The salt at our bedroom door — keeping something *in*, not out. The whispering from the chair. The sister who wouldn't cross our threshold, who kept the dog, the dog who wouldn't come to the yard gate — animals know, everyone says animals know. The notebook: she was logging it. Logging her own episodes, the way you'd track seizures. Durations. *He's been quiet* — talking about it in front of me in code. My poor wife, sitting up nights in that chair, fighting some thing that had gotten into her — when? in the hospice, all those months in that place, all that grief in her with the door open — and too proud or too far gone to tell me. It explained everything. I have never in my life felt anything like the relief of that explanation arriving, and I am not able, even now, to fully forgive how good it felt. I put it all back. Notebook, invoice, recorder, drawer, lock, key in the apron under the thimble. I am my father's son; I leave a site clean. She came home Sunday afternoon with a casserole dish of Iris's and a headache, and I watched her move around my kitchen and I was so tender with her it made her suspicious. She kept giving me looks. I made her tea. I was gentle as a nurse, and proud of it, God help me — protecting her, I'd have said. And then that night I ran the test. I lay with my back to the wall and breathed slow until the third board creaked, until the chair took her, until I'd counted off ten minutes of her watching me in the dark. Then I sat up fast and caught her wrist. I had it in my head — I'd had it in my head all evening — that I'd see it. The thing. That surprised in the dark, before it could arrange her face, something else would look out at me, and then I'd know, and we could start fighting it together. Her wrist was thin in my hand. She made a sound I'd never heard her make. And what looked out of her, in the yard light through the window, was fear. Plain animal fear, eyes white at the edges, her free hand up between us — fear of the man holding her arm in the dark, fear of *me*, immediate and total and without one ounce of surprise in it. Like something she'd been expecting.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of horror writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Voice** The prose is exactly what the plan called for: plain, declarative, hard-nosed, domestic. "I'm not going to dress it up. I searched my own house like a burglar." This is Tom's voice — the estimator, the man who prices what things cost, who leaves a site clean. The voice is consistent throughout and never strains for effect. The arithmetic anxiety surfaces naturally ("forty minutes," "four minutes," "ten minutes"), and the self-consciousness about documentation ("I want that on the record too") is perfectly in character for a man who has always quietly believed he needs to prove himself. The chapter's central horror — Tom building the wrong explanation from correct evidence — is executed with genuine sophistication. The reader assembles the truth while Tom assembles his inversion of it, and the gap between those two constructions is where the dread lives. This is not a cheap trick; it's structurally earned. **The Ending** The final image — "Like something she'd been expecting" — is the chapter's best moment and it earns its weight. It reframes everything: Maren's vigil, her fear, the salt, the sister who won't cross the threshold. The horror isn't that something looks out of her; it's that what looks out is ordinary terror of an ordinary man who has been doing something she can't name for twenty months. The line doesn't announce itself as a gut-punch; it arrives quietly and lands hard. **Dialogue and Scene** The porch dialogue is economical and revealing. "He's been quiet" does enormous work — it's the line Tom fixates on, and rightly so, but for the wrong reasons. The recorder scene is handled with restraint; the model doesn't over-describe the wrongness of the voice, which is the correct choice. "Wrong the way a familiar room is wrong with one piece of furniture moved, nothing you can point at" — this is one of the chapter's few figurative moments, and it lands cleanly. **Weaknesses** The notebook entries are perhaps slightly over-explained in their significance — "Four minutes of what. Eleven minutes of what." is good, but the paragraph that follows where Tom "builds the whole thing" is slightly more tell-heavy than the rest of the chapter. The model tells us Tom felt relief, tells us he was proud of his tenderness, where a more rigorous version might trust the reader more. The line "I have never in my life felt anything like the relief of that explanation arriving, and I am not able, even now, to fully forgive how good it felt" is good but slightly over-articulated for a narrator who otherwise keeps his findings to himself. The "I want it understood" and "I want that on the record" constructions are used twice in close proximity, which slightly dilutes their effect. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the locked drawer, the notebook of dates and durations, the recorder, the hospice invoice he doesn't read closely, the wrong-case-building, the wrist-grab, Maren's fear. The plan's note that Tom "stages a test, pretends to sleep, then sits up suddenly and grabs her wrist in the dark to see what looks out of her" is executed precisely. The chapter also correctly avoids explaining mechanics or providing exposition dumps. **Overall Assessment** This is strong literary horror writing — patient, intelligent, and genuinely unsettling. The horror is domestic and psychological rather than atmospheric or supernatural, which is harder to sustain and is sustained here. The chapter's greatest achievement is making Tom's wrongness feel earned rather than revealed: we see him become the thing Maren has been afraid of, and we understand why, and it's worse for the understanding. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four — A Good Day I let go of her wrist and we didn't speak of it. She sat in the bathroom a long while with the fan on, and I lay on my side of the bed listening to it, and in the morning there were the small sounds of breakfast same as ever, and the place we put things took one more thing. Although I noticed she wore long sleeves, and I noticed I couldn't look at her forearm, and we were very polite for three days, the way you're polite around a crack in a windshield. Then Thursday came up fifty-nine degrees and sunny, one of those February days Missouri deals off the bottom of the deck, and Maren stood at the window with her coffee and said, "Lake's probably pretty." "Probably is," I said. "Dana's got the shop." So we went. Just like that, like twenty years ago — sandwiches in wax paper, the good thermos, her digging through the glovebox for the tape adapter that hasn't worked since Obama and putting it back like always, *one of these days*. Stockton's an hour and ten. She drove. I watched the fence lines go by and the cattle standing in the bright cold looking personally offended by it, and somewhere around Greenfield I felt something in my chest let go a notch, like a strap loosened. The bait shop at the turnoff has a sign that says WORMS & COFFEE & TACKLE & PRAYER, and it's said it as long as we've been coming, and I said what I always say, which is that I'd hate to be the man who had to rank those, and Maren laughed. Not the polite laugh. The real one, the one that comes up through her like it's surprised her on the way, that I'd been hearing maybe twice a year since I got sick. She laughed and hit the wheel with the flat of her hand and said, "Every time. Twenty years, same joke," and I said it wasn't a joke, it was a theological position, and she laughed again. I'd like to set the day down plainly, because of what it was. We parked at the point and walked the shore path to the flat rocks. Water was high for February and that off-green it gets under a clear sky. Cold enough that we had it all to ourselves, just a pair of coots working the shallows and one bass boat way out, somebody's gray morning of faith. We ate the sandwiches on the rocks with our shoulders touching because there's exactly one comfortable spot and we've never once fought over it, we just fold into it, her on the left, the way two people wear into each other like stairs wear. We told the honeymoon story. Not to anybody — to each other, which is the only way we tell it, in alternating pieces like a duet we never rehearsed but never get wrong. Branson, 1999, the cabin with the wasp nest, the manager with the hook hand whose name we fight about every single time — she says Dale, I say Earl, the truth is lost to history. The flat tire on 76 in the rain and Maren holding the umbrella over me with one hand and a road flare in the other like the Statue of Liberty of the Ozarks. Her piece, my piece, her piece. She remembered the part about the wasps getting into the window unit and did the sound effect, and I laughed until my eyes ran, and a coot flushed off the water at the noise of us. I'll tell you what I had decided by about noon. I'd decided I was going to tell her everything. Sitting on that rock with the thermos lid going back and forth between us, it all looked different — the notebook, the recorder, the salt. It looked *manageable*, is the word. Whatever had its hooks in her, we were still us; this day was proof of it. There on the rocks was the woman herself, the real one, available, laughing — and I thought, now. Tell her now what you found, tell her you know something's wrong, tell her she doesn't have to sit up nights alone with it. I got as far as taking the breath. I remember taking the breath. And then she skipped a stone — four skips, she's always been better at it than me, lower release, it's physics, she says, it's wrist, I say — and she handed me a flat one she'd been warming in her pocket, handed it over without looking, like a nurse handing an instrument, because that's a thing she does, has always done, stocked her pockets with good ones for me. And I didn't want to lose the day. That's the whole of it. I had one good day in my hand after months of salt and chairs and four-minute entries in a notebook, and I was not willing to spend it, and I let the breath out and skipped the stone. Two skips. She made a sound of deep professional pity. I skipped maybe a dozen more. The sun was getting long and the cold was coming up off the water, and at some point I turned to say we should start back, and caught her watching me. She was sitting up on the rocks with her knees drawn up and her chin on them, and she was watching me skip stones with an expression I knew, or thought I knew — soft, unguarded, a little gone, the way you'd look at something you didn't expect to get to see. I filed it under love. I want to be exact: it *was* love, I'd put my hand on a Bible. But there are different occasions of love. There's how you look at your husband, and there's how you look at old film of him, and on a witness stand I could not tell you which one I saw on those rocks, and the question has a lot of teeth at three in the morning. "What," she said. "Nothing. Getting cold." "Five more," she said, and dug in her pocket and handed me another stone. We drove back at dusk with the heater going and the radio low, and somewhere past Greenfield I fell asleep against the window, which I never do in a car, and I mean never — a man who can't sleep without his back to a wall doesn't sleep sitting up in a moving vehicle. But the day had been long and bright, and I went down like a stone in still water, no skips, and if I dreamed I don't have it. I woke in our driveway. Engine off, yard light on. Maren was sitting behind the wheel, not moving, keys in her lap, and she was looking straight ahead at the garage door, and her face had nothing in it at all. However long we'd been parked there, it had been a while; the windshield had started to fog from our breathing. "We're home," she said. "Must've conked out." "Mm," she said, and got out, and went in, and left the porch light on for me. It was a good day. I'd swear to that in front of anybody. The best in a year, maybe the best since I got sick, and I have gone over every hour of it, the rocks and the wasp story and her hand passing me stones, more times than I've gone over anything in my life. I never found a thing wrong with it. That's the trouble. There's not a crack in it anywhere, and yet I can't ever quite get it to take my weight.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable achievement in controlled tonal modulation. The plan called for "no horror beats at all" with a chapter that "curdles only on reread," and the execution delivers precisely that. The writing maintains genuine warmth throughout while seeding the dread so subtly that a first-time reader experiences only the good day, while a rereader finds the horror distributed evenly across every paragraph. The prose voice is exceptionally well-calibrated to Tom's character profile. The short declaratives, the estimator's precision ("I'd like to set the day down plainly, because of what it was"), the arithmetic anxiety displaced into stone-skipping physics — all of this is consistent with the established character. The line "Missouri deals off the bottom of the deck" is a clean, earned regional metaphor that doesn't strain. "Like stairs wear" is similarly grounded and precise without being showy. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is exactly right. "Lake's probably pretty." / "Probably is." — this is two people who have been married long enough that full sentences are optional. The bait shop joke exchange is perfectly rendered: we believe in the twenty-year repetition of it, and Maren's response ("Every time. Same joke") lands as both affectionate and, on reread, slightly uncanny — who is she laughing with? The dialogue never overexplains or delivers theme; it does the work of intimacy. The honeymoon story sequence is the chapter's emotional center and it earns its warmth. "Dale, I say Earl, the truth is lost to history" — this is the texture of a real marriage, the small contested myths that couples carry. The Statue of Liberty of the Ozarks line is the chapter's one moment of genuine humor, and it lands because it's earned by context rather than inserted for effect. The chapter's structural intelligence is in how it handles the horror. The plan specified one "small wrongness, unremarked" — the watching expression on the rocks. The execution gives us something more sophisticated: Tom himself notices it, files it "under love," and then immediately qualifies that filing with devastating precision: "there are different occasions of love. There's how you look at your husband, and there's how you look at old film of him." This is the chapter's best line, and it works because it's Tom's own observation, his own uncertainty, not authorial intrusion. The ending is masterful. The fogged windshield, Maren sitting motionless with keys in her lap, "her face had nothing in it at all" — this is the horror arriving without announcement. "We're home" echoes the recorder line from the plan ("It's okay. It's Tuesday. You're home") in a way that will devastate on reread. The final paragraph — "I never found a thing wrong with it. That's the trouble. There's not a crack in it anywhere, and yet I can't ever quite get it to take my weight" — is the chapter's closing image, and it works because it's Tom's own idiom (the estimator, the man who prices what it costs to keep weather out) applied to emotional experience without being forced. The chapter follows the plan faithfully: the lake house, the bait shop joke, the honeymoon story with alternating pieces, the stone-skipping, the watching expression filed under love, falling asleep in the car (which Tom "never does"), waking in the driveway. The plan's note about the notebook gap ("On reread: whose day was it?") is honored by the chapter's refusal to answer. Minor criticisms: "went down like a stone in still water, no skips" is a slightly too-neat callback to the stone-skipping that tips its hand a little — it's the one moment where the author's hand is visible. The opening paragraph, while necessary to bridge from Chapter 3's wrist-grabbing, is slightly more expository than the rest of the chapter's show-don't-tell discipline. "The place we put things took one more thing" is a good line but slightly opaque in a way that reads as reaching. The character consistency is excellent. Tom's self-awareness about his own worst habits ("I was not willing to spend it"), his insecurity about Maren's love, his physical habits (the stone-warming detail, the sleeping against the wall), his dry humor — all present and correctly weighted. Maren's laugh "that comes up through her like it's surprised her on the way" is a beautiful character detail consistent with her profile. Overall, this is a chapter that achieves its difficult goal: genuine warmth that doesn't feel manipulative, horror that doesn't announce itself, and a marriage rendered with enough specificity to feel real. The prose is disciplined, the voice is consistent, and the structural intelligence is high. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 19 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five — Eleven Minutes Saturday morning Maren took a shower, and the water in this house gives you eleven minutes of hot if you want it all the way hot, and I knew her habits to the minute, and I am telling you the truth when I say I hated myself crossing the hall. I'd thought about the recorder all week, the way your tongue thinks about a broken tooth. Thirty seconds was what I'd given it that night in the dark, thirty seconds and a slammed thumb, and I'd built a whole church on it. Some part of me — the estimator, the part that walks the roof himself instead of trusting the photos — knew thirty seconds wasn't a survey. So when the pipes knocked on and the fan started, I went into the sewing room with my reading glasses and the headphones from the kitchen junk drawer, and I sat in her chair, and I did it properly. The files sorted by date. I picked one from mid-January because the notebook had said *Jan 26 — 11 min. Asked about Hollis*, and I wanted — I don't know what I wanted. To check her math. Dead air. Furnace tick. Then Maren, close to the mic, soft: "Hey. Hey. There you are." And the low voice answered, and this time I didn't jam my thumb down. This time I sat in the lamplight with my eyes shut and *listened*, the way I should have the first time, and listening was the end of everything. Because it wasn't a strange voice. That was the lie I'd told myself in the dark with a flashlight, that it was wrong, foreign, a thing speaking through her. It wasn't strange at all. It was answering her questions in long, easy sentences, and it was telling — I went cold in stages, like wading in — it was telling the honeymoon story. Branson. The wasps in the window unit. The manager — *Dale*, it said, *his name was Dale*, taking her side, and she laughed on the tape, low so as not to wake anyone, and the voice told the flat tire part, my part, the umbrella and the road flare, the Statue of Liberty of the Ozarks, my words, my pauses, my joke laid in the spot where I keep it. It was my voice. I want that down plainly. Not similar. Mine — my cadence, my stories, polished and slightly wrong the way your own voice on a machine is always slightly wrong, and I had sat in this room two weeks ago and called it a stranger because the alternative wasn't a thought a man can have first. I checked the file date against the wall calendar in my head. A Tuesday. I made myself remember that Tuesday and there it was: the crossword, the river to the Caspian, and then dusk, and the hammer, and the cold. The day I lost. The recording was time-stamped 2:11 a.m., which I had not lost, which was the middle of a night I'd have sworn I slept through, and on the tape the voice that was mine talked on, comfortable, unhurried, about wasps, while somewhere upstairs in the actual past my body lay in our bed with my wife beside it holding a recorder, and where was I. That's the question I sat there with. Where was *I*. Then, eight minutes in, the tape did the thing I will hear for the rest of whatever this is. The voice stopped mid-sentence. A long breath. And then — same throat, same air, but I knew it the way you know your own footsteps — my voice, *actually* mine, small and thick like a man surfacing from anesthesia: "…Maren? What time is it? Why's it dark?" And her, instantly, smooth as a hand on a horse: "It's okay. It's Tuesday night. You're home. I've got you." "Is Hollis out? I heard —" "Hollis is at Iris's, remember. He's fine. He's good." A pause. The furnace. Then my voice, *my* voice, already blurring at the edges: "I had a — I was somewhere —" and her: "I know. It's okay. Stay with me a minute," and the tape ran on through small talk, her asking did I want her to read to me, me saying something about the deck I couldn't make out, and at some point — you can't hear the seam, that's the horror of it, there is no seam — the answers got longer and easier and turned back into the other thing, and she said goodnight to it, and the file ended. Eleven minutes. *Jan 26 — 11 min. Asked about Hollis.* She wasn't logging her episodes. She was logging mine. No — turn it over, all the way over, the way I had to sitting in that chair with the shower running: she was logging my *visits*. The durations weren't the lost time. They were the found time. Four minutes, six minutes, eleven. A dash for the nights nobody came. She'd been sitting in that chair in the dark for twenty months taking attendance, and the man she was waiting on showed up in single digits, and everything outside those single digits, every breakfast and thermostat fight and casserole — the arithmetic stood up out of the notebook and looked at me, and I have done estimates my whole life, I know what it is to total a column, and I sat in my wife's sewing room and would not total it. The invoice was still folded in thirds under the notebook. I read it this time. Carthage Hospice and Palliative Care. Patient name: Thomas E. Calloway. The columns were room and board, medication management, the dailiness of dying itemized at in-network rates, and none of that mattered against two lines at the bottom. Final billing date, twenty months ago, October the ninth. And below it, in the box where they close the account, a single phrase the software must put there, that some clerk never thinks twice about, that I read maybe forty times with the steam from Maren's shower coming under the door: *Account closed — date of death 10/9.* There are words you can't make mean anything else. I tried. I am a man who has argued with paperwork for a living, and I sat there and tried *clerical error*, tried *another Thomas Calloway*, tried it as hard as I've ever tried anything, and the paper just lay in my hands being itself. The water shut off. Eleven minutes. I put it all back — notebook, invoice, recorder, the key under the thimble — hands doing the work clean while the rest of me stood elsewhere, and I crossed the hall to the other bathroom and ran the cold tap and gripped the sink. The mirror over the sink is just a mirror. I want to be fair to it. The face in there was mine, fifty-four, needs a shave, no shadow behind the eyes, no flicker, nothing from a movie. Ordinary. That was the worst available outcome, I think. I stood and looked at an ordinary face and did what I do, did the only thing I know — I ran the arithmetic. Twenty months. Call it six hundred days. The notebook entries averaged, what, five minutes, and not every day, not near every day. Total it. Don't total it. I gripped the sink and looked at the face, the razor sitting in its cup on the shelf, the jaw shaved clean as church every single morning of those six hundred days, and asked it the question that had been waiting under all the others since the chair, since the salt, since the coffee went to nothing: Who shaved you this morning?
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a genuinely accomplished piece of horror writing that executes its central revelation with considerable craft. The plan called for Tom to listen to the recorder properly, discover the notebook's true meaning, read the hospice invoice, and end on the question of who shaved his face — and the chapter delivers all of this with disciplined, controlled prose. The voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Tom's estimator's mind — the man who walks roofs himself, who totals columns, who argues with paperwork — is deployed not as a character quirk but as the structural engine of the revelation. The way he processes the horror through arithmetic ("Total it. Don't total it.") is psychologically precise and consistent with the character profile. The line "I have done estimates my whole life, I know what it is to total a column, and I sat in my wife's sewing room and would not total it" is genuinely excellent: it shows a man's coping mechanism failing in real time without announcing itself as such. The tape sequence is handled with real skill. The plan specified that Tom's own voice breaks in "confused" and Maren responds "steady, practiced" — the chapter delivers this, and the detail that "you can't hear the seam, that's the horror of it, there is no seam" is one of the chapter's best moments. It earns its horror through specificity rather than atmosphere-mongering. The honeymoon story callback is well-placed. The reader has been told about this story in the character profiles and earlier chapters, so its appearance here — told by the wrong thing, in Tom's voice, with his pauses and his joke "laid in the spot where I keep it" — lands with genuine dread. The phrase "polished and slightly wrong the way your own voice on a machine is always slightly wrong" is a precise, earned observation rather than forced poetry. The invoice section is handled with appropriate restraint. The chapter doesn't linger on the revelation with theatrical horror; instead, Tom tries to argue with it the way he'd argue with a bad estimate — "tried clerical error, tried another Thomas Calloway" — which is both character-consistent and more disturbing than any amount of gothic atmosphere would be. The ending question — "Who shaved you this morning?" — is the chapter's final beat, and it works because it's been earned. The razor in its cup, the clean jaw, the six hundred days: the arithmetic has been laid out, and the question is the natural terminus of a mind that processes through inventory. Some minor criticisms: The opening paragraph's "I am telling you the truth when I say I hated myself crossing the hall" is slightly self-conscious, a moment where the narrator addresses the reader in a way that briefly breaks the intimate, inward quality of the prose. The "broken tooth" simile in the second paragraph is functional but not particularly distinguished. The phrase "the end of everything" is a small lapse into the kind of declarative drama the rest of the chapter carefully avoids. The dialogue on the tape is excellent — Maren's "It's okay. It's Tuesday night. You're home. I've got you" is exactly the worn-smooth quality described in her character profile ("Said so many times, to both of them, that it's worn smooth as a key"). The detail about Hollis is a nice callback. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and precisely, hitting every beat: the proper listening, the honeymoon story, Tom's voice breaking in, the notebook's true meaning revealed, the invoice read closely, the bathroom mirror with no theatrical effect, and the final question. The plan's instruction that there be "no mirror-lag theatrics, just his own face, ordinary" is honored exactly. The prose is plain and hard-nouns throughout, with figurative language used sparingly and almost always landing. "I went cold in stages, like wading in" is the chapter's best simile — physical, earned, not decorative. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. Character consistency is high. Tom's petty self-awareness ("I want that down plainly"), his arithmetic coping, his estimator's methodology, his old insecurity about being loved less — all present and functioning. Maren, heard only through the tape, is consistent with her profile: practical, worn smooth, managing dread by administering it. The chapter is coherent, emotionally engaging, and earns its darkness through accumulation rather than theatrical gesture. It is, by the standards of the genre and the plan it was executing, a very strong piece of work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six — What He Can Piece Together I carried it four days. I don't know who carried it the rest of the time. That's not a joke. That's the bookkeeping I lived inside that week — I'd come to at the kitchen table with toast crumbs on a plate I didn't remember eating from, and think: was that mine? Did I have that hour? You can't audit it. There's no receipts. There's a notebook in a locked drawer, and the woman who keeps it. Wednesday I put it on the table. Literally — that's the only way I knew how. She'd made pot roast, and we'd eaten, and she was telling me something about Dana's water heater, and I went to the sewing room and came back and set the notebook down next to the trivet, and sat, and folded my hands. Maren stopped with her fork in the air. And here's the thing about my wife, here's thirty years of small talk over scissors: she didn't gasp, she didn't go white. She went *efficient*. She set the fork down parallel to her knife and said, "You went through my things," in a level voice, and then, in the same level voice, like changing lanes, "Have you been doubling up on the gabapentin? Because Dr. Okafor said if the confusion got worse —" "Maren." "— we'd adjust it, and you've seemed foggy, Tom, you have, even Dana said —" "October the ninth," I said. The furnace ticked. I'd swear the house leaned in. She looked at the notebook. Then she looked at me — and this is the part I keep, this is the frame I'd save from the whole reel — she looked at me the way she looks at a mirror at the shop, checking the work, checking the *line* of a thing, and whatever she was checking for, she found it. Her shoulders came down. Twenty months came off her or settled into her, I couldn't say which. "Okay," she said. Quiet. No hum in it anywhere. "Okay." I had questions in a row like fence posts. What happened. What did you do. What is it. I got almost none of them driven, because she didn't tell it like a confession, she told it like wreckage washes up — a piece, then nothing, then a piece from some other part of the ship. "You were gone before you died." That was the first thing she said, and she said it angrily, which I didn't expect, and the anger wasn't at me exactly. "People say that and it's a comfort, *he was gone before he went*, it is not a comfort, it's the worst part. Weeks, Tom. You counted ceiling tiles. You called me Dorothy — I never found out who Dorothy was. The last whole thing you ever said to me —" and she stopped there, and never finished it, and I will not know the last whole thing I ever said to my wife. "What did you do," I said. She turned her wedding ring around her finger, once, like checking a fitting. "Grandma Esther had things she did. Words over things. You'd have laughed — you did laugh, the salt under the windowsills when we moved in here, you thought it was sweet." She wasn't looking at me. "Iris kept all that up. Iris had — more than I knew she had. I asked her for it and she said no, and I sat in that parking lot until she stopped saying no." "What did you *do*, Maren." "I asked for you back." Flat. Final. "And something said yes." And that's it. That's the whole testimony. I asked what it was and she said she didn't know. I asked it differently — what did she *think* it was — and she looked at me for a long time and said, "I think it's whatever was close enough to hear me," and I have turned that sentence over five hundred times and it has no bottom. I asked about the terms, because there are always terms, every contract I ever estimated had terms — and she said, "It's not a contract, Tom. It's more like — " and her hands made a shape in the air, cupped, like holding water, and she put them back in her lap and didn't try again. I built the rest myself. I want to flag that clearly, because some of what I believe about my own situation, I framed alone with no level and no square. I believe it came back with the body from Carthage. I believe the nurse — there was a nurse, Maren said two sentences about a nurse from the hospice who came by the house that first month and left before her coffee cooled and quit the profession — saw something in that room on October the ninth that the paperwork couldn't hold. I believe the salt at the bedroom door is for it and the porch light matters and that *Feb 2 — Bad. Don't leave the porch light off again* is a worse story than I'll ever be told. Maybe none of that's right. She wouldn't say. The not-saying wasn't cruelty. She'd been the only adult at this particular crash site for twenty months and she'd learned what I was learning at speed: there's no use for most of the facts. There's just the next thing, and then the next thing, and you don't look down. We sat at that table a long time. Pot roast going cold and the gravy skinning over. At some point I said, "The chair," and she nodded, and I said, "You sit there because —" and she said, "Because nights are when you come up. Mostly nights, near sleep. I don't know why." And then, quieter, looking at the trivet: "It used to be I could always tell. Right away." *Used to be.* I let it by. I shouldn't have, but you only have so much, and I'd spent mine. It was that night, late, that I went back to the recorder. Not for proof — I was past proof. I think I wanted to hear her voice saying *there you are*, the way she said it in January, to somebody who was me. The files sort by date and I fat-fingered one from October. Last fall. Maren's voice, but daytime — birds in it, the kitchen window open. And the low voice, my voice with the seams pressed flat, and they weren't doing a vigil. They were doing a crossword. She read a clue, it answered, she argued, it held its ground, she laughed — the real laugh, the one that comes up through her surprised — and it said, "Coffee's good this morning," and she said, "You always say that now," with something in her voice I had no name for and then did: *fondness*. Ten minutes of a Tuesday morning. Comfortable as old stairs.
