Ends there, small, no quotable line. **Chapter 7 — Irongate.** They walk in the front gate, her wrists wrapped in chain that doesn't lock, his arm bad, the plan already fraying: the magistrate's clerk Vesper meant to reach has been bought or scared off; they're inside Corvath's hall before they know the witnesses aren't coming. Corvath — alive, scarred, smug, the collar on a velvet cushion — gloats, but won't confess anything useful; he's not a fool. Vesper talks anyway, riffing, needling, half panic and half genius, steering him toward the strongbox and the forged papers — and gets it *partly*: enough said in front of his own house guard about the porter's death being pinned on her, enough greed shown, that one guard's face changes. Then Corvath tires of it and orders the collar brought to her throat. Ends mid-motion: Brand's good hand on his knife. **Chapter 8 — Turnip.** Short, ugly violence: the enforcer dies hard, Brand's shoulder tears open, it's Vesper's fire and the turned guard's hesitation that get them out — with the forged indenture papers snatched, not the strongbox, and Corvath alive behind them, screaming for the watch. They run the back gate. The papers go to the magistrate who hates Corvath; the bounty is voided as fruit of a forged indenture — but there's no reward, no triumph, just a writ and a city they shouldn't linger in, and Corvath ruined enough to lack reach but alive enough to hate them. Brand takes his last purse to Maren's door; she looks at it and at him and says to stop coming — not cruelly; it's finished, and he has to let it be. Final scene: the wagon, the far road, Vesper on the bench beside him talking about the sea, about eel, about everything, and Brand telling the horse — *Turnip, walk on* — without noticing he's said it. She notices. She doesn't stop talking. The story ends mid-sentence of hers, road still going.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Brand **Background.** Brandt Kessel, called Brand since the army shortened it for him at nineteen. Twelve years in the Marchlands infantry, sergeant by the end, mustered out after a campaign nobody won. Fifteen years since as caravan guard, then bounty work because it paid better and he was good at the patience of it. He partnered with Joss Maren for nine of those years — Joss talked, Brand didn't, it worked. Joss has been dead four years. How is the thing Brand doesn't say, and the story honors that; what's visible is the shape of the wound: he pays Joss's widow monthly, he hasn't taken a partner since, and he doesn't drink, in the deliberate way of a man who used to. Three years ago he delivered a forger named Ellec Tane to a baron in Veshport. Tane swore the whole road that the evidence was planted, and was probably right — the seal on the warrant was newer than the crime. Brand collected, left, didn't ask after him. He knows what happened to Tane the way you know weather is coming. That's the failure Vesper arrives *after*. He's not a good man being tested for the first time; he's a man who already flunked, quietly, and built his routines around never being tested again. The rule about not talking to cargo dates from the day after Veshport. **Personality & idiosyncrasies.** Economical to the point of rudeness, but not actually rude — he answers questions, just with the smallest true unit available. Methodical: he checks the wagon's rear axle every morning whether it needs it or not, counts coin twice, sleeps facing the road. He has a dry, almost subterranean humor that surfaces maybe once a day, deadpan, easy to miss — Vesper learns to fish for it. He's good with animals and bad at admitting it; he talks to the horse when he thinks no one's listening, two or three words at a time. He mends his own clothes with surprising neatness. He cannot cook — burns porridge, over-salts everything — and is genuinely defensive about it, which is the one cheap door into him that Vesper finds on day one and never stops using. **Flaws.** His guilt is partly self-serving — the Maren money has become a ritual that lets him feel like a man paying a debt rather than a man who got his friend killed, and somewhere under it he knows Maren doesn't want him at her door, and he goes anyway, because the going is for him. He mistakes silence for honesty and withholding for integrity. He's a coward in exactly one dimension: he will face four armed men before he'll face a direct question about himself. He's also, frankly, complicit — he's been telling himself for years that the warrant is the warrant and the magistrate's seal does his thinking for him, and he's old enough to know better. **Physicality.** Mid-forties. Big through the shoulders, going thick at the middle, gray salting a brown beard he keeps short with a knife. A pale scar splits his left eyebrow; two fingers on his left hand don't fully straighten. Moves slow and deliberate — economy again — but is fast for about four seconds when it matters, then pays for it. Sits a wagon bench like furniture. When he's uncomfortable he checks equipment: straps, buckles, the horse's feet. He looks at people's hands before their faces. **Motives, allegiances, relationships.** The Maren payments structure his whole year — this bounty exists because the winter payment is short. Maren herself: Joss's widow, raising a boy, Tam, who's eleven now and barely remembers his father; she has been polite and increasingly tired about Brand's visits for two years. No guild loyalty, no friends he'd name, an innkeeper in Dollis Cross he's slept with on and off without either of them calling it anything. The horse, a brown gelding, deliberately unnamed — naming things is how you start owing them. **Voice.** *"I've been doing this eleven years. You know how many of them talked the whole way? All of them. You know how many talked me round? Eat your bread."* *(Internal:)* The warrant had Corvath's seal and a magistrate's seal and that was supposed to be the end of his part in it. That had always been the end of his part in it. He checked the axle again. The axle was fine. *"I don't season it wrong. You're tasting it wrong."* # Vesper **Background.** Vesperatha — a temple-registry name; tieflings in the eastern cities get catalogued like imports. Born in the Sallowmarket quarter of Irongate, mother a laundress who died of the gray cough when Vesper was nine, father unknown and the horns nobody's surprise. Raised loose — wash-house work, then market work, then a memory-act in a tavern (she can hold a fifty-item list, recite it backward; it's a genuine knack and her one party trick) which got her noticed by a factor who got her indentured, papers half-forged from the start, debts she never incurred compounding annually. Sold up the chain twice. Corvath bought her contract eight months before the story — for the memory, ostensibly, a walking ledger for accounts he'd never write down. The collar was so the ledger couldn't leave or testify. She saw it on his desk, etched, fitted, *waiting*, three days before she ran. The escape: she did empty the strongbox — and spent a third of it in two months, badly, gloriously, on a fur cloak and oysters and a private bath every day, and feels a complicated defiant non-guilt about that. And there was Oti — a porter's boy, fourteen maybe, wrong corridor, dark, her knife already drawn for Corvath's man behind her. She has rebuilt the moment so many times she no longer trusts her own memory of it, which for *her*, with her knack, is its own specific horror. She talks around it the way a tongue avoids a broken tooth. **Personality & idiosyncrasies.** The talking is the engine and it is genuinely compulsive — it started in childhood (talking to her mother through the cough, filling the room so the rattle wasn't the only sound) and now it's what she does instead of fear. She knows this about herself and hates being known to do it. Crucially: she is not always *good* at it. She repeats herself. She tells the eel recipe three times across the story, differently each time. She has stories with no endings and she'll say so — "that's it, that's the whole thing, I don't know why I started it." She misjudges — pokes the Joss wound on day one thinking it's a soft target like the cooking, and the silence that comes back scares her into an hour of genuinely tedious chatter about crows. Her actual gifts: she reads people fast and accurately, she remembers *everything*, and she's funny in a low, sideways way, more often filthy than clever. **Flaws.** She lies reflexively about small things — what she ate, whether she slept — pure habit, control where she can get it, and it nearly poisons the trust at exactly the wrong moment. She's vain about her hair and unembarrassed about the vanity. She runs first; that's not a one-time beat in chapter 4, it's *her* — she has survived twenty-nine years by being elsewhere when the door breaks, and the coming-back is the new and frightening thing. She performs invulnerability so well that people stop offering her care, then resents them for stopping. And she spent the money. She'd spend it again. She wants you to have a problem with that so she can win the argument. **Physicality.** Late twenties. Copper-red hair, heavy and disorderly, her one luxury — she finger-combs it constantly, even chained. Skin a dusky terracotta, hot to the touch; horns sweeping back close to the skull, one chipped at the tip (she'll give three different stories for it). Eyes ember-orange, no whites, hard for strangers to read and she uses that. A long tail she has imperfect control of when emotional — it lashes when she's frightened and betrays her in negotiations, which she knows and tries to sit on, literally. Slim, quick, no fighter — when she fights in chapter 4 it's graceless, screaming, all flame and terror. Her fire, unbound, is small and ragged: a cone of it, a few seconds, then she's spent and shaking. **Motives, allegiances, relationships.** Survival first, always, and she's honest about that. What she wants under it: to be *unrecorded* — no ledger, no registry, no collar, no contract with her name in someone else's strongbox. The sea, which she has never seen and talks about constantly, is shorthand for that. Allegiances: none living. Her mother. Oti, who she owes in a currency that doesn't exist. By midstory, Brand — and it terrifies her that she came back, because she doesn't have a category for it, and her response to not having a category is, naturally, to talk. **Voice.** *"So the eel — no, listen, this matters — you don't gut it first, that's the whole secret, my mother had it from a Vellish woman who — actually maybe you do gut it first. One of those. Either way you need wine and we don't have any, so. Forget the eel."* *"You want to know what I'd have named the horse? Turnip. Look at his face. That is a Turnip's face and you know it, and not naming him doesn't make him not yours, it just makes you rude."* *(After the silence, ch. 5:)* "Say something. You never say anything and right now I need you to be a different sort of man for about a minute, and then you can go back." # Corvath **Background.** Magister Dren Corvath, Irongate. Not nobility — that's the load-bearing fact. Grain-factor's son who clawed up through customs clerking, bought his magistracy at forty, and has spent twenty years being subtly condescended to by men with older names. His fortune runs on forged indentures: debts invented, compounded, sold — a paper plantation. Hundreds of contracts, maybe a dozen people in the city who understand the whole web, and he keeps it that way by keeping the ledger in his head and in trusted memories. Vesper was to be the perfect ledger: total recall, legally voiceless, collared so she could never be subpoenaed or fled. The knife she put in him cracked two ribs and missed everything vital. He has told the wound as an assassination attempt at four dinner parties since. **Personality & idiosyncrasies.** Not a fool and not a cartoon — careful, patient, and genuinely intelligent about money and law; the con in chapter 7 fails to crack him because he doesn't gloat about *crimes*, only about *winning*. His exploitable seam is status: he can be needled about his accent, his bought seat, his name — that's where the temper lives, and that's the thread Vesper pulls. Fastidious: changes gloves when handling coin, has a horror of being touched. Collects automata — little brass birds — because they perform on command and never want anything. He is polite to servants in front of guests and a void to them otherwise. He doesn't think of himself as cruel; he thinks of himself as the only honest man in a city of hypocrites, since at least *his* exploitation is written down. **Flaws.** Vanity about self-control — he believes he can't be played, which is the only reason he can be. He underestimates Brand completely (hired muscle, a known quantity, paid and forgotten) and that's where the chapter 7 violence comes from his blind side. He has nursed the strongbox theft into obsession out of proportion to the money: it's not the gold, it's that a *catalogued asset* outmaneuvered him, and the bounty's absurd size is the public price of his pride. **Physicality.** Sixty, spare, upright, expensively plain — grays and blacks, one ring. Scar tissue pulls slightly at his side; he's developed a way of turning his whole body instead of twisting, and Vesper clocks it instantly and mentions it sweetly. Voice low and even; he gets quieter as he gets angrier, until the moment he doesn't. **Motives, allegiances, relationships.** Wants Vesper back alive and collared — dead, her memory dies clean, but he needs to know what she's already told and to whom, and he needs his household to watch the asset come back. Owns the loyalty of his enforcer, Hesst (a debt-bonded swordsman — Corvath owns *everyone* near him by paper), and believes he owns one of the city magistrates, which is half-true. The house guard are paper-bonded too, which is why one of them turns: the porter Oti was the nephew of the man whose face changes. **Voice.** *"A bounty is just a price, and a price is just a sentence with the sentiment removed. I find I prefer documents to people for precisely this reason."* *"You may tell me about my accent again if it comforts you. The contract doesn't care how I pronounce it."* # Maren **Background & function.** Sera Maren, Joss's widow, mid-thirties, runs a dye-house in Dollis Cross that she built back up herself after Joss died — a fact Brand's guilt-arithmetic has never fully credited. Her son Tam is eleven. For the first two years the money mattered; for the last two it has mostly been a monthly reopening of a door she's trying to close. She doesn't hate Brand — that's the point of her, and what keeps her scene in chapter 8 from being a beat of cruelty or absolution. She's just *done*, and kinder about it than he deserves, and clear in a way he can't argue with. **Physicality & manner.** Dye-stained hands she's stopped apologizing for. Stands in doorways rather than inviting in — has done for a year, which Brand has noticed and not let himself understand. Direct eyes, an economy of speech that's actually a match for Brand's, which is maybe why Joss loved them both. **Voice.** *"Tam asked who you were, last spring. Did I tell you that? Not your name. Who you were. That's when I knew this had stopped being for him."* *"Keep it. Not because I'm angry. Because you walking here every season with your hat in your hands isn't a debt, Brand, it's a confession, and I'm not a priest."* # Hesst (minor) Corvath's enforcer — debt-bonded swordsman, big, quiet, competent, and pointedly *not* a sadist: a mirror Brand can't look at comfortably, a man doing ugly work because of money he owes, ten years further down the same road. Their fight in chapter 8 is two tired professionals, no banter, and Brand kills him without hating him, which is worse. One line earlier, in the hall, flat and almost kind: *"Don't make it a thing. It's never worth being a thing."* He says it to Brand like advice from a colleague. It is.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One: Cargo "The thing about crows," Vesper said, "is that they remember faces. That's true. A woman in Sallowmarket threw stones at one and for two years after, every crow in the quarter knew her. They'd follow her to the well. They'd wait. And that one—" a chain clinked as she pointed, presumably, at the sky behind him— "has been with us since the bridge. So either you've thrown a stone at it, which I doubt, because you'd consider it a waste of a stone, or it knows something we don't." Brand watched the road. "It's the second one. It's always the second one with crows." Three days out from Irongate. The red-headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king's ransom — that is, if she didn't talk him into an early grave first. She had started at dawn, the way she'd started at dawn every day since Hollowford, the way he had come to understand she would start at dawn until he handed her over and collected and rode away into a silence so large he could live in it. "You haven't fed your horse the good oats. He knows. You can see it in his face. That is the face of a horse who knows there are better oats." The horse's ears turned back, traitorously. "What's his name?" Nothing. "He has to have a name. You can't just call him *horse*. That's like calling me *cargo*." A pause that was not nearly long enough. "Turnip. Look at him. Look at the face. That is a Turnip's face." Brand checked the right-side trace, which did not need checking. The bounty paper rode in his breast pocket, folded in quarters, soft at the creases now from handling. Magister Dren Corvath's seal in black wax, a magistrate's seal beside it in red, and a number underneath that he'd had to read twice in the posting-house. A number that didn't make sense for a runaway indenture, which was a thing he had decided not to think about, the way you decide not to think about a sound the axle makes. He thought about the axle instead. The axle was fine. He'd checked it that morning. He'd check it again at noon. They came down into Pellet's Ford a little before midday, a village of one street and a shrine and a baker who looked at the wagon, looked at what was in it, and decided, the way most people decided, to look at something else. Brand bought two loaves and a piece of hard cheese and a bag of the good oats, and didn't examine why. At the wagon he counted his coin twice into his palm, moved most of it back into the purse on his belt, and slid four silver into the second purse, the worn one, the one that lived buttoned inside his coat and didn't get spent from. The leather of it had gone dark and soft as a dog's ear. Vesper watched him do it. She watched everything. That was the other thing about her, under the noise — the eyes never stopped working. "You've got two purses," she said. "Observant." "He speaks." She rattled her chains in a small celebration. Her wrists, he noticed, were going raw under the iron. "One purse for spending and one purse for — what. Not spending. A church purse. You don't seem like a church man. A woman, then. No — a *debt*." She said the word like she was tasting it. "It's a debt. You count it like a debt. Spending money you count once. Debt money you count twice." Brand handed her a loaf and half the cheese, which was more cheese than the arrangement strictly required, and climbed back up onto the bench. "I knew a man in Irongate kept three purses," she went on, around bread. "One for the watch, one for the temple, one for a woman called Pell who wasn't his wife. The trick is — actually I forget what the trick is. He drowned. That's not how the story ends, that's just what happened to him later, the story doesn't really have an ending. I don't know why I started it." The road climbed out of the village through cut fields, stubble going gray under a sky that promised weather tomorrow or the day after. The crow, or a crow, kept pace in long lazy stitches from fence post to fence post. She was quiet for almost a mile, which he had learned meant she was working on something, the way other people were quiet when they were sharpening a knife. "Brand." He hadn't told her his name. The posting-house clerk in Hollowford had said it in front of her, once, and that had been that. He understood now that she kept everything, every word that went past her, the way the road kept wheel ruts. "Have you done this before?" "Eleven years." "That's not what I asked." Her voice had changed. The performance had gone out of it, and what was left was smaller and harder to ignore. "Have you done *this*. Taken somebody in when you knew. When the paper said one thing and you knew another thing, and you took them anyway, because the paper's the paper and the seal's the seal and a man can't be expected to go behind every seal in the Marches." The horse walked. The wagon creaked. Somewhere off left a dog was barking at something with great conviction. "Because you don't look at me," she said. "Eleven years of cargo, you'd think you'd be easy about it by now, you'd look at me like furniture. The ones who look at you like furniture have never thought about it once. You look at the road like the road owes you money." Brand reached back without turning and dropped the second loaf in her lap. "Eat your bread," he said. She ate her bread. The crow went on ahead of them, fence post to fence post, patient as paper, and Brand watched the road behind them out of habit, both ways, the way he always did, though there was nothing on it yet but their own dust hanging in the cold air, and the day going on, and Irongate three days out and getting no farther away no matter how slow he drove.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished opening chapter that demonstrates sophisticated craft across nearly every dimension. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Narration:** The close third-person narration from Brand's POV is exactly as planned — dry, flat, weathered, with figurative language rationed carefully. The few metaphors that appear earn their place: "soft at the creases now from handling" for the bounty paper, "dark and soft as a dog's ear" for the worn purse, "the way you decide not to think about a sound the axle makes" for Brand's deliberate non-thinking. These are grounded, tactile, and consistent with the character's sensibility. None of them feel forced or decorative. **Vesper's Voice:** This is the chapter's greatest achievement. Vesper talks the way the plan describes — compulsively, digressively, with stories that go nowhere ("He drowned. That's not how the story ends, that's just what happened to him later, the story doesn't really have an ending. I don't know why I started it."). This is genuinely funny and genuinely characterful. The crow observation, the Turnip naming, the two-purse deduction — each beat reveals her intelligence and her compulsion simultaneously. The shift in her voice at "Have you done this before?" is handled with real skill: "The performance had gone out of it, and what was left was smaller and harder to ignore." This is showing, not telling, and it works. **Brand's Characterization:** His silence is active, not passive. The detail of checking the trace that doesn't need checking, the axle that's fine, the twice-counted coin — these behavioral tics establish character without exposition. The two-purse detail and Vesper's reading of it is elegant: it tells us about the Maren money without explaining it, exactly as planned. **Dialogue:** The dialogue has genuine friction and economy. Brand's responses are minimal but not artificially so — "Observant," "Eleven years," "Eat your bread" — each lands differently. The "Eat your bread" ending is perfect: deflection, care, and refusal simultaneously. **Following the Plan:** The chapter hits every planned beat: the crow, the eel recipe is absent (replaced by the two-purse story, which is fine — the eel comes later), Turnip naming, the bounty paper establishment, the village stop, the Maren purse (unexplained), and the final question about whether he's done this before with someone who didn't deserve it. The chapter ends flat, on him watching the road behind them, exactly as specified. **Minor Criticisms:** The line "Irongate three days out and getting no farther away no matter how slow he drove" at the very end is slightly more writerly than the rest of the narration — it's the one moment where the prose reaches a little. It's not a serious flaw, but it's the one place where the flatness wavers. The eel recipe doesn't appear in this chapter (it's in the plan), though the two-purse story substitutes effectively. The crow motif is established well. **Coherence and Plot:** The chapter establishes the situation cleanly without over-explaining. The bounty paper's unusual size is noted and deliberately not examined — this is exactly right for Brand's character and the story's themes. The Maren purse is planted without explanation. The chapter does what a first chapter should: establishes voice, character, situation, and a central tension (the question Vesper asks at the end) without resolving anything. **What's Exceptional:** The moment where Vesper reads Brand's two-purse system is genuinely impressive — it shows her intelligence, his guardedness, and the dynamic between them all at once. The horse's ears turning back "traitorously" is a small perfect detail. The crow as structural motif is handled with a light touch. This is a very high-quality piece of genre fiction that executes its plan with precision and genuine literary skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two: The Warrant Says He made camp early, in a fold of ground off the road with a stream and the remains of somebody else's fire ring. Old habit: never the obvious spot, never the spot with the view. He picketed the horse, gave him the good oats, said two words to him that nobody heard, and built a fire that behaved itself. The porridge did not behave itself. "You've burned it," Vesper said, with the reverence of someone witnessing a rare bird. "How. There's water in it. It is mostly water. You've burned water." "It's caught a little." "It's *caught*. Is that the word. Like the pot did it." She took her bowl anyway, two-handed, chains gathered in her lap, and ate it all, which undermined her position, and she knew it, and ate it anyway. Afterward he came around the fire with the little tin of salve and the spare linen, and crouched, and said, "Wrists." She went still in a way she hadn't been still all day. The eyes worked at him, ember-orange, no whites to give her away, and her tail — he'd noticed this — curled tight around her own ankle like a rope securing a boat. "Festered wrists in this cold," he said, "you'll fever by Irongate. Damaged goods come cheaper." "Damaged goods," she repeated. "Right." She held them out. The iron had chewed through the skin in two places on the left, one on the right, and the flesh around it was hot — hotter than fever-hot, hot the way the bowl had been hot, and he must have hesitated at it because she said, "We run warm. It's not infection, it's just me. You can check. Infection smells; I don't. I smell like — what do I smell like." She considered her own wrist with academic interest. "Woodsmoke and ill-gotten gains." He worked the salve in around the iron where he could and bound linen between metal and skin, and his hands were careful because that was how his hands did things, and the excuse about merchandise sat there between them and neither of them poked it. "Ask me," she said. "Ask you what." "Whatever it is you've been not asking since Hollowford. You chew on it after supper. You're chewing on it now. Your face does a thing." Brand tied off the linen. The fire popped. Somewhere up the dark the horse shifted his weight and sighed like a paid mourner. "What did you do?" "Ha." No triumph in it, though. She drew her knees up, arranged the chains so they'd lie quiet, and looked into the fire the way people look into fires when they're about to be either very honest or very good. "What does the paper say I did?" "Theft. Flight from lawful indenture." He left the rest where it was. "Lawful." She turned the word over. "Here's a thing about indenture paper. The clerk who drew up my first contract had a wart, here—" she touched her jaw— "with one black hair growing out of it, and the whole time he read the terms I watched the hair move. Eleven years old. They put debts on me from before I could write. My mother's lying-in costs. Did you know you can be born owing? It compounds. That's the beautiful part. By the time I could read the contract I owed more for the years I'd spent not reading it." "Corvath." "Corvath bought me eight months ago. Third owner. Paid more than the paper was worth and everybody pretended that wasn't strange." She picked at the linen edge until he looked at her hands and she stopped. "He wanted my memory. That's my knack — I keep things. Lists, numbers, faces, conversations. All of it, all the way down. He'd sit me in the corner of his study like a clock and read out figures, and I'd keep them, and there'd be no ledger anywhere a magistrate could lift. A walking strongbox. Brass birds on every shelf — he collects them, little automata, you wind them and they sing the same eleven notes, and he'd watch them with more feeling than he ever looked at a living thing." She stopped. "Why am I telling you about the birds. The birds don't matter." Brand put another length of wood on the fire and waited, which was a thing he was better at than most people. "There was a collar on his desk," she said. "Three days before I ran. Velvet box, open, like jewelry. Binding-work — you've maybe heard of it. It doesn't hurt you. That's what everyone gets wrong. It just makes you *unable*. Can't speak of what the holder forbids, can't pass the boundary the holder sets, can't testify, can't write it, can't sing it. They etch your name inside, against the skin, so the work knows you." Her tail had come loose from her ankle and was moving, small and constant. "Mine was already etched. He showed me. He thought I'd be — I don't know what he thought I'd be. He has a way of giving you a horror like it's a kindness, and you're meant to thank him, because at least *he* writes things down." "So you ran." "I emptied the strongbox first. He kept it under the window seat — anyway, the lid had this hunting scene worked into it, dogs and a boar, and one of the dogs was laughing. Whoever made it gave a dog a laugh. I had time to notice that, that's how long his house takes to wake. It was raining. The kitchen passage—" and here, between one word and the next, she went somewhere and came back, the join almost seamless, almost— "I went out through the kitchens and over the wall by the midden, and he caught up to me himself at the gate, which I didn't expect, and I had a knife, and I put it in him. I thought he was dead. He should be dead. I was sure for two months and wrong for two months." The fire ate its wood. Brand looked at her, which she had accused him, accurately, of not doing. "And the money?" "Spent a third of it." Chin up, daring him. "Furs. Oysters. A bath every single day, hot, with oils. Best two months of my life. I'd do it again." He didn't give her the argument she'd set the table for. He banked the fire, checked the picket, took the bounty paper out of his coat, and read it again by the low light, though he knew it. Corvath's seal. The magistrate's seal. *Theft of chattels and coin. Flight from lawful indenture.* And below those, in the clerk's same tidy hand, a line he'd read in Hollowford and folded away from ever since: *Murder of a household servant.* A servant. Not Corvath. Somebody else, in that rain, between the strongbox and the wall. He looked across the fire. She'd curled on her side, eyes shut or nearly, hair over her face, breathing already gone long and even — or doing a fair imitation of it. Brand folded the paper along its soft creases and put it away, and didn't ask.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of genre fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through the key elements. **Characterization:** Both Brand and Vesper are rendered with precision and consistency. Brand's economy of speech is maintained throughout — his answers are minimal, his actions speak louder, and his internal life is conveyed through behavior (checking the picket, re-reading the warrant, the careful hands). Vesper's talking is genuinely compulsive rather than quippy — she does repeat herself, she does go on tangents (the birds, the dog on the strongbox lid), and she does misjudge (the chin-up defiance about the money that he refuses to reward with an argument). The tail as emotional barometer is used well. Both characters match their profiles closely. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is genuinely strong. "It's caught a little." / "It's caught. Is that the word. Like the pot did it." — this is sharp without being theatrical. The exchange about the wrists ("Damaged goods come cheaper" / "Damaged goods. Right.") carries real weight in its brevity. Vesper's monologue about the indenture papers is digressive in exactly the right way — the wart with the black hair, the birds she then dismisses, the hunting scene on the strongbox lid. These feel like authentic compulsive speech rather than exposition delivery. **The seam:** The chapter plan called for the seam in Vesper's story to be half-visible to the reader but uncaught by Brand. This is executed well. "The kitchen passage—" followed by the almost-seamless return is subtle enough that a reader might miss it on first pass, and the warrant's final line about the household servant lands with quiet force. The plan's instruction that Brand "doesn't ask" is honored. **Metaphors and figurative language:** The writing is restrained with figurative language, which is correct for this voice. The few images used mostly land: "like a rope securing a boat" for the tail, "the horse shifted his weight and sighed like a paid mourner" — this last one is slightly overworked (the "paid mourner" is a bit theatrical for the register), but it's not egregious. "Looks into fires when they're about to be either very honest or very good" is a nice, earned observation. The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. **Pacing and structure:** The chapter moves well. The porridge opening establishes tone efficiently. The wrist-tending scene does the work of physical intimacy without sentimentality. The fire-talk is the right length. The ending — Brand reading the warrant, seeing the servant's death listed, looking at her apparently sleeping, and choosing not to ask — is quiet and effective. **Minor weaknesses:** "The fire ate its wood" is a slightly flat transition. The "chin up, daring him" moment is the one beat that edges toward telling rather than showing — though it's brief. The plan called for Brand to notice the seam but not catch it yet; the execution has him not catching it, but the narration's "almost seamless, almost—" is slightly heavy-handed in flagging the seam to the reader. A lighter touch there might have been more trusting of the reader. **Faithfulness to plan:** The chapter follows its plan closely — the porridge, the wrist-tending framed as merchandise protection, the fever-warmth noticed, her account with the seam, the warrant's final line about the servant. The plan's note that "the reader half-does" catch the seam is honored. The chapter ends on Brand folding the paper away and not asking, exactly as planned. **Overall:** This is confident, controlled writing that earns its effects through restraint and behavioral specificity rather than emotional declaration. The dialogue is the strongest element. The few metaphors are mostly earned. The characters are consistent and three-dimensional. It's not a masterpiece — there are small moments of over-signaling — but it's genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# Chapter Three: Sleet The weather arrived a day early, which was the only thing about the day that came ahead of schedule. It started as cold rain mid-morning and graduated to sleet by noon, a hard slanting gray that hissed in the stubble fields and turned the road to soup. Brand put the spare canvas over the back of the wagon, and Vesper, under it, kept up a running judgment of the storm's character — "no ambition, this weather, a real storm commits" — until the ford. The ford was the Sallow, running brown and fast with two days of upstream rain. He walked it first, picked his line, and was halfway across with the horse leaning into the traces when the rear wheel found a hole the river had dug since the last man crossed. The wagon lurched. Something in the back went over with a crash and a word he hadn't heard a woman use in some years. For a long, grinding moment the wheel hung — and Brand was in the water to his thighs with his shoulder under the corner of the bed, swearing at the horse in a flat conversational voice, and the horse, perhaps moved by being spoken to at such length, hauled. The wheel climbed out. The wagon came up the far bank streaming like something landed in a net. "You talk to him when it matters," Vesper said from under the canvas. Her hair was plastered dark across her face. "I heard you. Whole sentences." "Anything broken back there?" "Only my faith in your driving." There was no dry wood that night. There was no dry anything. He got a grudging, smoky fire going in the lee of a rock shelf, fed it splinters from the broken crate slat by slat, and produced supper that was hot in the strict legal sense. The sleet kept on. The cold came up out of the ground, through the blankets, patient as a tax. He gave her both blankets first. He told himself this was the merchandise argument again, and the telling lasted until somewhere past full dark, when he heard her teeth. She ran warm. He'd learned that with the salve. But warm-blooded meant warm-spending, she informed him, voice clattering, the body throwing heat out into the night like a fool spending in a strange town — "we burn fast and we burn down, it's why you don't see old tieflings begging, we don't last to be old beggars" — and that was, by his rough audit, the first thing she'd said all day designed to make herself smaller rather than larger. "Come here," Brand said. A pause, in which the sleet ticked on the canvas. "Why, sergeant," she said, but the joke shivered apart in the middle. He arranged it as logistics, because it was logistics: his back against the wagon wheel, the canvas pitched off the wagon's side to break the wind, both blankets over the both of them, her chains gathered and wrapped in sacking so the iron wouldn't lie against either of them like a dead man's hand. She fit herself against his side with a kind of businesslike caution, rigid for the first while, and the heat came off her the way it comes off a banked stove, through wet wool, through everything. The shivering stopped. Hers, then, a while after, his. "This is good thinking," she said. "I'm a resource. Like the oats." "Go to sleep." She did not go to sleep. But the talk changed, out there at the bottom of the cold. It slowed. The performance went out of it, the way it had gone out the night before for a moment, only now it stayed out, and what was left was a low voice near his collarbone, going where it went. She told him about the collar. Not the facts of it — she'd done the facts. She told him how Corvath had presented it. The velvet box turned to face her, like a ring. How he'd explained the binding-work the way you'd explain the features of a good coat: the etching, the boundaries, the silence she'd be able to keep at last — *his* word, *able*, as though it were a power he was conferring. How he'd seemed genuinely puzzled at her face, even hurt, because in his arithmetic he was promoting her. Things that get written down get kept, he'd told her. He was making her permanent. "He'd have wound me up like one of the birds," she said. "Same eleven notes forever." And then she was quiet, which was somehow louder than the talking. The fire was down to a red argument among the splinters. Brand watched it and heard himself say, "Maren." She didn't pounce. She held still against him, the way you hold still when a wild thing has come near the fire, and let him do it at his own pace, which was slow. "Joss Maren's widow. The second purse. Four silver a month, more when the work's good. There's a boy. Tam. Eleven." The whole of it laid out flat, like coins counted on a board. "Joss was my partner. Nine years." "How did he die?" The sleet ticked. The horse shifted on the picket. Down in the dark the Sallow went on hauling its mud to the sea she was always on about. She felt the answer not come. He felt her feel it. And — this was the thing he didn't expect — she let it lie. No prying, no joke to lever the gap open. She just shifted her head, and her hair came against his jaw, cold at the surface and warm underneath like everything else about her, and she said, "Four silver a month," in the voice of someone filing it where she filed things, and left him alone in a way that was somehow company. She slept. Genuinely — he knew the difference now; the imitation was too even. Her breath went ragged at the edges, and once she said part of a word, and her tail crept across and lay over his boot like a dropped rope. His hand was on the ground an inch from her hair. He was aware of the inch. He left the hand where it was and the inch where it was, and stared at the canvas going pale and dark with the fire's opinions, and did arithmetic. The bounty, against the debt. Five winters of Maren's purse, six. The number the posting-house clerk had read twice. He set it against four silver a month and it should have ended the question. It kept not ending the question. He ran it again, and the figures came out wrong again, in a way that had nothing to do with figures, and somewhere in the running of it he noticed the fire was out and the sleet had stopped. Far off down their backtrail, through a gap where the rock shelf fell away, there was a light. Small. Steady. A campfire, two miles back, maybe three, in country where there was no reason for one that wasn't following the same road. Brand watched it a long time, not moving, the tiefling's warmth coming through his coat like a hand laid flat against his ribs.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of craft that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The opening line — "The weather arrived a day early, which was the only thing about the day that came ahead of schedule" — is economical and dry, establishing tone without straining. It earns its small wit. The ford scene is excellent. The physical action is rendered with precision: "Brand was in the water to his thighs with his shoulder under the corner of the bed, swearing at the horse in a flat conversational voice." This is showing at its best — Brand's character (methodical, physical, talks to the horse) demonstrated through action rather than description. Vesper's observation "You talk to him when it matters. I heard you. Whole sentences." is perfectly calibrated: it advances the horse-naming motif, reveals her watchfulness, and lands without being cute. The logistics framing of the shared warmth is handled with real skill. "He arranged it as logistics, because it was logistics" — the repetition is intentional and works, the narration letting the excuse stand without comment exactly as planned. The detail of the chains wrapped in sacking "so the iron wouldn't lie against either of them like a dead man's hand" is the chapter's best image: specific, functional, and carrying emotional weight without announcing itself. The collar scene is the chapter's emotional core and it's well-executed. Corvath's framing — "able to keep at last" as though conferring a power — is genuinely chilling and characterizes him efficiently. "He was making her permanent" lands hard. The shift to "She'd have wound me up like one of the birds" is earned by the character profiles and feels organic rather than planted. The Maren fragment is handled with restraint. Brand's delivery — "flat, like coins counted on a board" — is consistent with his voice. Vesper's response, letting the unanswered question about Joss's death lie, is the chapter's most sophisticated character moment. The plan called for "no prying, no joke to lever the gap open" and the execution honors that: "she let it lie... and left him alone in a way that was somehow company." This is the kind of paradox that earns its place. The ending — the distant campfire, the Greyjacks foreshadowed — is well-placed. It doesn't announce itself as a cliffhanger; it's observed with the same flat attention Brand gives everything. **Weaknesses and concerns:** Some metaphors strain. "The fire was down to a red argument among the splinters" is clever but slightly precious — "argument" is doing a lot of figurative work for a fire description. "The Sallow went on hauling its mud to the sea she was always on about" is better, more organic. "Her breath went ragged at the edges" is a slightly odd construction — breath going ragged at edges suggests something more like distress than sleep. It's trying to be precise and lands slightly off. "The figures came out wrong again, in a way that had nothing to do with figures" — this is the chapter's weakest moment. The plan explicitly called for "the numbers keep coming out wrong in a way that has nothing to do with numbers" and the execution is nearly verbatim from the plan, which suggests the model is transcribing rather than writing. The line itself is also the most "tell" moment in the chapter: it explains the emotional subtext rather than trusting the reader to feel it. "Her warmth coming through his coat like a hand laid flat against his ribs" — this is a good image but slightly over-extended. The simile is doing work the scene has already done. The line "warm-blooded meant warm-spending" and the riff about old tieflings is good Vesper voice, but "we burn fast and we burn down" is a touch on-the-nose as a metaphor for tiefling mortality/vulnerability. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and closely — almost too closely in places, which occasionally makes it feel like execution rather than discovery. But the execution is genuinely skilled. **Dialogue assessment:** The dialogue is largely excellent. Vesper's voice is distinct, her humor lands without being quippy, and Brand's minimal responses are calibrated correctly. "Come here" as his response to her shivering is perfect. The only weak moment is "This is good thinking. I'm a resource. Like the oats." — the joke is fine but slightly too neat, too self-aware. **Overall:** This is accomplished literary erotica-adjacent fiction that earns its emotional beats. The chapter plan is followed with fidelity and intelligence. The prose is controlled, the characters are consistent, and the darkness (the collar, the Maren wound, the distant fire) is well-earned. Minor overreaches in metaphor and one tell-don't-show failure keep it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four: Greyjacks They came at first light, which is when professionals come. Brand had moved the camp before dawn — wagon hauled into the throat of a gully, horse on a short picket, Vesper awake and informed in six words — and so the four men coming up through the gray didn't find them where the fire had been. It bought him the length of a breath. It bought him the first one. He heard the boot on shale, stepped out around the rock with the heavy knife already moving, and that one went down with no noise worth the name. Then the gully was full of men. Greyjacks — he knew the cut of them, a free company out of Dollis Cross that worked bounties in packs and split thin. The big one in front had a falchion and no interest in talking, which Brand respected right up until the moment he had to deal with it. Steel met steel twice, ugly, no fencing in it, two heavy men in wet grass trying to be the one who got to keep breathing. Brand was fast for four seconds. He spent all four. The falchion went wide off his quillons and he put his shoulder through the man's balance and the knife through the gap that opened, and was already turning toward the third man when the world hit him in the left shoulder like a thrown anvil. He was on the ground. That was new. The crossbow bolt stood out of him at an angle that belonged to somebody else's body, and the pain hadn't arrived yet but its messenger had, a vast white wrongness, and his sword was in the grass a yard away, which might as well have been Irongate. Things happened in pieces after that. The third man coming downhill with no hurry in it. The crossbowman on the gully rim, recranking, taking his time, because time was all theirs now. And behind the wagon — the keys. He'd had the keys out at dawn to shift her, and they were in the mud somewhere by the wheel, and Vesper was down there in the mud with them, both hands working, her breath going in high thin sounds he'd never heard her make, no words in them at all. The left cuff came off her. He saw it. He saw her wrist come out of the iron slick with blood where the raw place tore, and he saw her look at him — one look, ember-orange, the whole face naked of performance for the first time since Hollowford — — and she ran. Up the far side of the gully, low and fast, red hair a streak through the dead bracken, one cuff still swinging from her right wrist like a hawk's jess. The third man checked, half-turned to mark where she went, and decided in the practical manner of his trade that a chained bounty loose in open country could be ridden down after, and the man with the knife wound was now. He came on. Brand got the sword. Getting it cost him the world's worth of white wrongness, and he was on one knee with his left arm dead at his side, and the math was the math: one man at quarter strength against a man at full, with a crossbow above for whenever the angle pleased. He didn't blame her. He noticed that, distantly, the way you notice the weather of another country: that he didn't blame her at all. He'd have run. Anyone built to last would have run. The falchion man's friend feinted high and Brand met it badly, and the next exchange put him on his back with the sword wedged in a deadfall and a boot coming down, and over the boot's shoulder the crossbowman on the rim stood up to find his angle — — and the rim of the gully caught fire. That was what it looked like. A sound came with it that he never managed to describe afterward, a scream and a furnace having the same thought at once, and a cone of ragged orange swept the rim and the crossbowman was in it. He went over backward, burning, the bow discharging into the sky, and Vesper came down the gully wall behind her own fire like something out of a chapel painting of the bad afternoon. There was nothing fine about it. That was what the stories got wrong, he thought, lying in the grass with the messenger of his pain finally delivering. She couldn't fight. She came down screaming, the cuff whipping on its chain, flame guttering off her hands in spurts and rags — it was small fire, close fire, spent fast — and the man standing over Brand turned into it because anyone would, and she gave him the last of it full in the face and then simply ran into him, all her slight weight, both hands, and they went down together in the bracken and Brand never did see exactly what happened there, only that she came up and the man didn't. The fourth Greyjack, the one Brand had cut in the opening seconds, was crawling for the gully mouth. She stood watching him go, shaking, the spent fire still crawling in dying threads along her knuckles like something looking for a way back in. The crawling man reached the open and kept going and the country swallowed him. Then it was quiet, except for her. She came and stood over Brand with his sword — she'd picked it up somewhere, out of the deadfall, and held it wrong, both hands strangling the grip — and she was talking. He understood, through the white noise of his shoulder, that she'd been talking the entire time, that the high thin sounds had never stopped, they'd just been underneath the burning, and now they surfaced and ran: "—up, you have to get up, I don't know how to drive the wagon, I mean I do, I've watched you, you hold the things and you disapprove of the horse, but the *bolt*, there's a bolt in you, did you know that, of course you know that, stop *looking* at it—" Her tail lashed. Her wrist sheeted blood down over the cuff, dripping off the swinging iron. The road behind her stood open all the way to the gray horizon, no chain on her left hand, a dead man's horses somewhere up the rim for the taking, every direction in the world unlocked. She knelt down in the bracken beside him and laid the sword in the wet grass, and her hands, when they came at the laces of his jerkin, were fever-hot and shaking and would not stop, and neither would the talking, and Brand lay there with the sky going strange above him and understood at last, with the simple clarity that arrives on the far side of a crossbow bolt, what the talking was *for* — that it was the sound she made instead of screaming, that it had always been, since long before him — and that some unmapped border had just been crossed in the bracken, by both of them, and there was no road back over it. "Vesper," he said. It was the first time he'd used her name. The talking stopped for one full second. "The bolt stays in till you've cut the shirt," he said. "Knife's in my boot." "I know where your knife is," she said, unsteadily. "I know where everything is. I keep things." And she went on talking, and cut.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely impressive piece of action writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Execution** The opening line — "They came at first light, which is when professionals come" — is economical and characterful without being showy. It establishes Brand's worldview (he thinks in professional categories) and sets tone efficiently. The action sequence that follows is rendered with unusual clarity; fight scenes are notoriously difficult to write without either becoming confusing or tipping into choreographic tedium, and this one avoids both. The "fast for four seconds" callback to the character profile is seamlessly integrated. The crossbow bolt's arrival is handled with real skill: "the world hit him in the left shoulder like a thrown anvil" is a strong image, and the follow-up — "the pain hadn't arrived yet but its messenger had, a vast white wrongness" — is genuinely good. The messenger metaphor is earned and specific rather than decorative. **Vesper's Return** The plan called for her return to be "ugly, not cool," and the chapter delivers on this. "She couldn't fight. She came down screaming" is exactly the right note. The fire described as "small fire, close fire, spent fast" and the image of flame "crawling in dying threads along her knuckles like something looking for a way back in" — this last image is the chapter's best, specific and strange without being overwrought. The talking-as-compulsion payoff is handled with restraint. The plan specified that Brand should understand "what the talking was for" in this chapter, and the narration delivers it without over-explaining: "that it was the sound she made instead of screaming, that it had always been, since long before him." This is told rather than shown, but it's a moment of internal realization that earns the slight tell. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. Vesper's flood of words after the fight — "I don't know how to drive the wagon, I mean I do, I've watched you, you hold the things and you disapprove of the horse" — is genuinely funny and characterful, capturing her voice from the profile accurately. Brand's first use of her name is a strong beat, and his follow-up ("The bolt stays in till you've cut the shirt") is perfectly in character: practical, deflecting, but the name-use has already done the emotional work. **Weaknesses** The chapel painting simile — "like something out of a chapel painting of the bad afternoon" — is the chapter's weakest moment. "The bad afternoon" is trying too hard for a wry register that doesn't quite land; it's the kind of phrase that calls attention to itself as writing. "Some unmapped border had just been crossed in the bracken, by both of them, and there was no road back over it" is the chapter's most significant failure. The plan explicitly warned against "something shifted between them" narration, and this is precisely that — abstract, summarizing, telling the reader what to feel rather than trusting the scene. It's the one moment where the writing loses its nerve and reaches for a quotable line instead of letting behavior carry the weight. The phrase "with the simple clarity that arrives on the far side of a crossbow bolt" is slightly too neat, a little too self-consciously aphoristic. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows its plan closely: the Greyjacks attack at dawn, Brand is wounded, Vesper gets a cuff off and runs, she comes back with fire, the chapter ends on the turn. The plan's note that "it's ugly, not cool" is honored. The plan specified ending "on the turn; this is the chapter that's allowed to" — and the chapter does end on a genuine turn, though the "unmapped border" line slightly over-explains it. Brand's characterization is consistent: the "he didn't blame her" observation, the professional assessment of the math, the economy of his speech even wounded. Vesper's characterization is excellent — the talking-as-compulsion, the graceless violence, the return. **Overall** This is strong work with one significant lapse (the "unmapped border" sentence) and a few smaller ones. The action is clear and purposeful, the character voices are maintained under pressure, and the emotional beats are mostly earned through behavior rather than narration. The chapter accomplishes its structural purpose — the power shift, the first name, the understanding of the talking — without sentimentalizing any of it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
