# Intention **Plot:** Five survivors starving through winter in a fenced farmhouse discover the spore mutants — the things that took half their town, including Mara's wife — are delicious. The story follows the communion arc: hunger, the first shameful taste, abundance and gallows humor, a quietly bent moral rule, transformation experienced as gifts they rationalize together, a betrayal in a sickroom, and an ending that is domestic, small, and unaware of its own chill. **The core human change from the draft plan:** Mara's wife, Lena — Theo's sister — was taken by the spores two winters ago and was never found dead. She may be walking out there. This gives Theo's refusal teeth ("you don't know what you're eating"), gives Mara's appetite a name for its guilt, and gives Pelle's enthusiasm a flavor of revenge. Nobody is a debate position anymore; everyone is eating (or not eating) their grief. **Characters:** Mara (narrator, ex-line cook, feeds people as love, doesn't examine herself); Theo (Lena's brother, the holdout, prickly not noble); Junie (eight, Pelle's kid, honest mouth); Pelle (hunter, eats with vindictive relish, jokes too much); Gran Odessa (runs the stores, hoards the last sugar, no aphorisms — her transformation symptom is unfinished sentences). Plus Biscuit, the dog, who gets a real arc: won't eat the meat, eventually won't sleep inside, and the family lets her go. **Voice & structure:** First-person kitchen journal. Mostly plain, practical prose — weights, chores, smoke times — with lyric moments rationed so they land. Each chapter opens with a short recipe/journal fragment that stays *bland and practical*; dread lives in what the recipes don't remark on, not in winking foreshadowing. Chapter shapes vary: one is nearly a single meal in real time; one is a fragmented month of dated entries. **Aiming for:** Gallows humor (the household euphemism arc — "fence pork"); ugly true physiology (refeeding pain, the first butchery going badly); complicity (one frightening symptom they rationalize together — Mara salivating at Theo's wound); a rule they quietly break in a lean week and never discuss; a betrayal that actually happens and leaves a mark; an ending that is one small domestic image with the narrator entirely guileless about it. **Avoiding:** Aphorisms, thematic mouthpieces, the locket beat (replaced by a supermarket name tag and an unprovable recognition), curated symmetrical ambiguity, "Pass me the bowl" as a staged devastator, the narrator co-signing the reader's dread, prose that performs. The fungus is never explained. Several things are raised and never resolved — deliberately, including Lena. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — Nine days of potato soup (~1000 words)** Epigraph: a genuinely thin recipe — one potato, snowmelt, "salt gone since Tuesday." Establish the household through Mara's inventory of the near-empty cellar: who eats how much, Gran's locked sugar tin, Junie's loose tooth coming out early (malnutrition — stated flatly, not mourned). The fence; the walkers beyond it, slow and sad. Establish Lena in two sentences, sideways: Mara still sets the salt where Lena liked it; Theo won't look at the fence line. Pelle shoots a walker pressing the wire. Hauling it to the burn pile, Mara smells the split carcass: roasting chestnuts. She doesn't tell anyone. She writes the smell down in the journal anyway, then writes *don't*. Ends flat: "We had soup." **Chapter 2 — First taste (~1000 words)** Epigraph: practical butchery notes, matter-of-fact ("parts like firm mushroom, no blood, the smell is the worst part because it isn't bad"). Junie listless; the last potato gone two days. Mara confesses the smell. The argument is short, tired, and ugly rather than philosophical — Theo's line is not a thesis but an accusation: "You don't know what's in them. You don't know *who's* in them." Pelle's counter is revenge, not pragmatism: "Good. Let them be useful for once." Gran unlocks the sugar tin instead — it's nearly empty too; that settles it without a speech. The butchery goes badly: Mara's hands shake, she does it wrong, has to start over. First bite is glorious — and then everyone who gorged is doubled over by midnight (refeeding after starvation). They eat anyway, smaller, the next day. Theo eats pine-needle tea and watches. Biscuit sniffs the meat and walks away. **Chapter 3 — Fence pork (~1000 words)** Epigraph: smoking and drying instructions, with a complaint about Pelle hogging the good rack. The cozy peak, played with humor: the cellar fills, and the household solves the naming problem because Junie refuses to eat anything called "mutant" — they audition names over dinner ("walker bacon," "shroom steak") and settle on *fence pork*. Junie rates dishes out of ten. Junie's ninth birthday: candle stubs, Pelle's disastrous attempt at fence-pork dumplings, real laughter — Mara notes it's the first since Lena. Two wrong notes, unremarked: Biscuit now sleeps in the barn; and at breakfast Junie describes a dream of a warm yellow forest, and Pelle goes still, because he had it too. Nobody says anything. Mara writes the dumpling recipe down and not the dream. **Chapter 4 — Dale (~1000 words)** Epigraph: none — just a dated entry, two lines, about burying instead of butchering. Pelle drags in a kill and Mara, butchering, finds a plastic name tag fused into the chest wall: HI I'M DALE, the supermarket logo still legible. She knew a Dale, vaguely — checkout lane four. The household conference is quiet and procedural; the rule gets made (nothing wearing anything, nothing that hesitates at the fence) and Mara is honest in the journal that the rule is mostly so they can sleep. Theo doesn't say anything cutting; he just doesn't come to dinner, and his absence is worse. The chapter's last movement: three weeks later, a lean stretch, a kill at dusk, something on its wrist that might be a watchband or might be growth. Pelle and Mara look at each other and butcher it anyway. The journal entry for that day is only the weight of the meat. **Chapter 5 — A good winter, considering (~1000 words)** Structure variation: a fragmented month of short dated journal entries. The gifts arrive scattered among chores: Mara's burn scars from her line-cook years gone smooth; Pelle hauling water bare-armed in a hard frost; the shared yellow-forest dream now nightly, and pleasant, and *populated* — they can find each other in it. Gran starts leaving sentences unfinished, and laughs about it. Amber flecks in Junie's eyes, noted between a bread recipe and a complaint about the woodpile. Walkers stop testing the fence; one stands at the tree line most mornings, and Junie waves. Biscuit leaves through a gap in the wire; nobody fixes the gap for a week; Mara writes that the dog was old anyway, and the entry is plainly a lie she's telling herself. The complicity beat ends the chapter: changing the dressing on a small cut of Theo's hand, Mara catches herself salivating — and that night, by the stove, she and Pelle almost talk about it, and instead Pelle says "it's the smoking salt," and they both agree it's the smoking salt. **Chapter 6 — Pine-needle tea (~1000 words)** Epigraph: the tea recipe, "for Theo," with steeping notes. Theo gashes his thigh on the woodpile; in his half-starved body the infection moves fast. Medicine box: empty since fall. The sickroom over four days, nearly real time at the worst of it. The unspoken thought sits at his bedside with all of them. Gran says no — not as wisdom, but angrily, mid-sentence, losing the words. Theo, lucid and frightened, makes Mara promise: not while he can't choose. The betrayal: on the worst night, fever talking, Mara strains fence-pork broth until it could pass for anything and spoons it into him, and writes in the journal only "he kept some broth down." He turns the corner in two days — too fast, and his wound knits clean, and he is not stupid. He looks at the smooth pink scar, then at her. Neither says it. He stops eating anything she cooks. He starts boiling his own water. **Chapter 7 — The one at the gate (~1000 words)** Epigraph: a recipe for company — "stretch the dumplings, count the chairs." False spring: green shoots in February, only within sight of the house. A walker comes up the lane and stops at the gate and waits — not pressing, waiting. Pelle raises the rifle and then doesn't, and can't say why he doesn't. It stands there through the morning. Junie watches from the window and narrates it like weather. By afternoon it's gone, leaving a cluster of golden fruit-bodies on the gatepost, sweet-smelling. Nobody eats them. Nobody throws them away either; Gran puts them in a bowl on the sill like cut flowers, and can't finish explaining why. The Lena thread surfaces once, unbearably, and is not resolved: Theo, at the window, very quietly — "It walked like —" and stops, and leaves the room. Mara does not write that down. The chapter ends with her writing down the dumpling count instead. **Chapter 8 — Setting the table (~1000 words)** Epigraph addressed forward, but guileless — practical directions for whoever finds the kitchen: where the salt is, how long to sear the meat, "eat slow if you've been hungry a while, we learned that the hard way." Spring proper. The long table goes outside. The household is visibly changed and easy about it — amber eyes, Gran humming instead of talking, Junie barefoot in frost — and still themselves at the surfaces that matter: the chore chart, the dish ratings, Pelle's bad jokes. Walkers stand at the tree line like shy relatives. No staged devastator for Theo: instead, one morning Mara finds a used bowl in the basin, fence-pork stew, washed badly the way Theo washes things. She washes it again properly and puts it away and writes nothing about it. The final image is small and domestic: Mara setting an extra place — maybe for Theo's pride, maybe for the next hungry travelers, maybe, never said, for Lena — and banking the stove so the soup stays warm overnight, because you never know who'll be hungry, and feeding people is love. She means it completely. Last line stays in her plain register — something like the soup keeping fine till morning. The reader does all the flinching.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mara **Background:** Thirty-nine. Worked the line at a mid-range bistro in town for sixteen years — never head chef, never wanted it; she liked the station, the heat, the rhythm. Met Lena there (Lena delivered produce, flirted badly, kept inventing reasons to come back). Married eleven years. The farmhouse was Gran Odessa's; Mara and Lena moved in to help when Gran's hip went, eight months before the spores came. So Mara is, technically, living in her dead wife's grandmother's house with her dead wife's brother — family by marriage, twice over, with the marriage gone. She has never once said the word "widow," in the journal or aloud. **Personality & idiosyncrasies:** Practical to the point of evasion. Mara processes everything through tasks — when Lena was taken, she scrubbed the stove for six hours. She measures food by eye and is always right, and is quietly vain about this. Keeps the journal because Lena gave it to her ("write your recipes down, you'll die and take them with you" — a joke, then). She still sets the salt cellar on the left of the stove, Lena's side. She has a habit of feeding people instead of answering them: someone starts a hard conversation, Mara hands them a bowl. She knows she does this. She does it anyway. **Flaws:** Her great flaw is that her love is genuinely indistinguishable, to her, from feeding — which means she cannot conceive of feeding someone as a harm. This is what makes the Theo betrayal possible and what makes the ending chilling: she will never, ever interrogate the impulse to fill a bowl. Secondary flaws: she lies to her journal by omission and half-knows it; she is conflict-avoidant in a way that lets worse people (or worse decisions) lead; she's a quiet snob about food even at the end of the world. **Physicality:** Solid, broad-shouldered, forearms scarred from years of oven racks — until chapter 5, when the scars smooth out, which unsettles her precisely because she misses them. Moves economically, no wasted gesture; in a kitchen she's almost graceful, anywhere else slightly stiff. Talks with a spoon or knife in hand. When upset, she gets quieter and faster, not louder. **Motives & relationships:** Keep Junie fed. Keep the household a household. Underneath: an unexamined hope, never written down, that Lena is still out there walking — and an unexamined terror about what that means once they start eating walkers. Loves Theo with an awkward sibling-by-marriage love made of obligation and genuine tenderness; he is the last piece of Lena. Trusts Pelle's hands, not his judgment. Defers to Gran on everything except the kitchen. **Voice:** - *"Theo says I answer questions with food. He said it like an accusation, so I made him toast."* - *"Four pounds dressed weight off the small one. The smell when it sears is the worst part, because it isn't bad. I keep waiting for it to be bad."* - *"I'm not going to write about the dog."* # Theo **Background:** Thirty-four, Lena's younger brother. Was a middle-school science teacher two towns over — earth science, not biology, a distinction he makes pointlessly often. Came to the farmhouse after the fall because Lena was here; stayed after she was taken because there was nowhere else and because leaving felt like agreeing she was gone. He and Mara have a relationship neither chose: two people who loved the same person, neither of them the family the other would have picked. **Personality & idiosyncrasies:** Prickly, pedantic, allergic to euphemism — he's the only one who never says "fence pork"; he says "it" or, when he wants to wound, "them." Corrects people's facts when he's scared (his fear-tell; Junie has learned to spot it). Not noble — his refusal to eat is tangled and partly spite: refusing is the last thing he can do *for* Lena, or *at* Mara, and he can't tell which. He's funny in a dry, mean way when relaxed, which is rare. Keeps his own small rituals: boils his water exactly seven minutes, reads the same four paperbacks in rotation, mends everyone's clothes well and acts put-upon about it. **Flaws:** Self-righteousness as armor — he needs to be the one who didn't, and some part of him knows the household's survival lets him afford his principles. Holds grudges in complete silence; punishes by withdrawal. Physically reckless when angry (the woodpile gash is not entirely an accident of attention). **Physicality:** Tall, thin to begin with and gaunt by midwinter — increasingly the visible odd-one-out as the others get hale. Hunches like a man perpetually in a doorway too short for him. Pushes his glasses up with the back of his wrist (one arm of them snapped and splinted with wire). After the fever: a limp, a too-clean pink scar he can't stop touching. **Motives & relationships:** Stay human — by which he means stay *Lena's brother*, the version of himself she knew. Loves Junie without complication; he's teaching her fractions off the chore chart and it's the gentlest he ever is. With Mara: the deepest bond and the deepest wound in the story — they are each other's last witness of Lena, and after the broth, that's exactly why the betrayal can't be spoken. He doesn't hate her for it. That's the problem. **Voice:** - *"It's not pork. Pigs were animals. Animals had parents. You're eating the weather."* - *"I had a sister. You all had soup."* (drunk on nothing, the one time he says it) - *"Seven minutes. I know it only needs three. I know."* # Junie **Background:** Eight, then nine. Pelle's daughter; her mother left for the coast evacuation in the first month and the radio went quiet six weeks later — Junie talks about her in the present tense and nobody corrects her. Was five when the world ended, so the fence is normal, walkers are normal, and that's its own quiet horror: she has no baseline to lose. **Personality & idiosyncrasies:** The honest mouth of the house — not innocent-wise (avoid the trope) but genuinely kid-logical, which is sharper. She rates every meal out of ten with stern objectivity ("six. The dumplings were a four but the broth carried it"). Refuses to eat anything with a scary name, which is how "fence pork" happens. Collects bottle caps and trades them with herself. Asks the unbearable questions at the worst times, flatly, between bites: "Is Aunt Lena one of the ones we don't eat?" She is not being cruel. That's what makes it land. **Flaws:** Adapts too well — she's the first to wave at the walker at the tree line, the first whose eyes change, the least bothered. Lies about small things (the bottle caps, who left the gate latch open) with total composure. Starting, lately, to put things in her mouth — bark, snow, a button — old toddler habit come back, which nobody lets themselves notice. **Physicality:** Small for her age from the lean years, then suddenly *not* — chapter 5 onward she's growing, robust, pink-cheeked in frost, barefoot when she shouldn't be. Gap-toothed (the lost tooth from chapter 1). Amber flecks in grey eyes, first of anyone. Climbs everything. Sits on the counter while Mara cooks; it's where she does her fractions. **Motives & relationships:** Wants the adults to be okay, in the unexamined way kids do, and works at it — her dish ratings are partly a service: giving everyone something small to argue about. Adores Theo (he doesn't talk down to her) and is the only one who can sit with him after the broth. With Mara: kitchen apprentice, salt-thief, the closest thing Mara will let herself have to a daughter. **Voice:** - *"Eight out of ten. It'd be nine but Dad sang."* - *"The yellow forest has a smell now. It's like the cellar but nicer. Are you going to finish that?"* - *"Biscuit didn't run away. She just lives outside now. Like everybody else."* # Pelle **Background:** Mid-forties. Drove a propane delivery route; hunted every season since he was twelve. Junie's father — raising her alone since the evacuation, carrying a coal of guilt that he didn't put her and her mother on the same truck, or stop the truck, or something. Lost his brother to the walkers in the first winter — saw it — which is the furnace under his eagerness to eat them. Not a neighbor originally; he and Junie showed up at the fence in year one with a rifle and a feverish kid, and Gran let them in over everyone's objections. **Personality & idiosyncrasies:** Jokes as load-bearing structure — the worse things are, the worse the puns ("well, the mushroom course is also the meat course, that's efficiency"). The household's necessary bluntness: he says the things others are still circling. But he's not a brute; he sings to Junie badly and on purpose, keeps a tally of his bad jokes that made Gran laugh (current count: three), and is meticulous — almost prayerful — about clean kills. His revenge-eating is real and he doesn't hide it: he calls his second helping "one for Marcus." **Flaws:** Confuses momentum with courage — he'd rather do a wrong thing now than sit with an open question. He's the one who'd have force-fed Theo on night one; only Gran stops him, and the fact that *Mara* then does it quietly is something he never learns, and would have respected, which says something bad about both of them. Sentimental in ways he punishes himself for. Cannot apologize in words; apologizes in firewood. **Physicality:** Built like the propane tanks he used to haul — short, thick, going grey in the beard. Loud walker, loud sitter, a man you always hear coming. Hands always busy: whittling, cleaning the rifle, drumming. After the change starts, his bad knee goes quiet and he keeps absent-mindedly crouching and standing, crouching and standing, like he's testing a lie. **Motives & relationships:** Junie, Junie, Junie — everything routes through her; the day her eyes show amber flecks he doesn't sleep, and the day after, he decides it's fine, because the alternative is unsurvivable. Owes Gran a debt he services daily in chores. Treats Theo with a careful, baffled gentleness, like a man handling someone else's religion. With Mara: kitchen-and-rifle partnership, the two practical people, complicit twice over (the watchband; the smoking salt). **Voice:** - *"Ate my brother's whole bowling team, probably. Way I see it, the least they owe us is dinner."* - *"That's not a watchband. Hand me the saw."* - *"It's the smoking salt. Has to be something, so it's the smoking salt."* # Gran Odessa **Background:** Eighty-one. Lena and Theo's grandmother; the farmhouse and land are hers — sixty years of canning, slaughtering, surviving lean years before lean years were apocalyptic. Buried a husband and a son. Was the one who decided to take Pelle and Junie in: "house needs a child in it" was the entire stated reasoning. Ran the household stores like a quartermaster long before the fall made it necessary. **Personality & idiosyncrasies:** Authority without speeches — she rules by allocation: who gets seconds, when the sugar tin opens, settles arguments by ending them ("done now"). Pointedly unsentimental in word, sentimental in act: claims she keeps the sugar locked for discipline; actually she's been saving it for Junie's birthdays, two more measured out. Plays a vicious, cheating game of cribbage. Her one open vanity is her hair, which she has Junie braid every Sunday. NOT a fortune-cookie matriarch: when she's profound it's by accident and she'd be annoyed to hear it called that. **Flaws:** Hardest on the people she loves most — Theo gets the sharp side of her tongue precisely because he's blood; Pelle, the stray, gets indulgence. Hoards beyond reason; even in abundance she under-reports the stores slightly, to everyone, including the journal-keeper. Her decline frightens the others more than the walkers do, and she handles it by refusing all acknowledgment of it. **Physicality:** Small, bent at the hip that never healed right, but occupies a room like furniture that was there before the house. Hands knotted and still fast with a paring knife. As the change takes her, the loss is in language, not body — sentences trail mid-word, and she shifts to humming, touching, allocating with gestures. By chapter 8 she's nearly wordless and seems — this is the unbearable part — *content*. **Motives & relationships:** Keep the household until she can't, then hand it to Mara — which she does without ceremony, by simply starting to ask Mara what's for dinner instead of telling her. Lena was her favorite and she has never said Lena's name since the taking; Theo is therefore both her last blood and a daily reminder, and she's awful to him and would burn the world for him. Her "no" in the sickroom is the one time she overrules Pelle outright, and it costs her words she doesn't get back. **Voice:** - *"Seconds for the child. Working men get firsts. You —"* (to Theo) *"— get what your manners earn."* - *"I had a name for it. I had — well. Pass the salt."* - *"House needs a child in it. Done now."*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One — Nine Days of Potato Soup *Potato soup. One potato, quartered small. Snowmelt, two quarts. Wild onion if Pelle finds any, which he won't, because it's January. Simmer until the potato gives up. Salt gone since Tuesday.* Nine days now on the above. I write it down anyway because Lena said recipes die with the cook, and because writing it makes it look like a choice. Cellar inventory, taken this morning by lamplight: eleven potatoes, four of them soft. One jar of green beans I am not opening until somebody is dying. Half a sack of cornmeal with weevils in it, which is protein if you don't think about it, so we don't think about it. Gran's sugar tin, locked, contents unknown, ask Gran at your peril. That's the whole cellar. It used to take me twenty minutes to do inventory. It took four. Junie lost a tooth at breakfast. Top left, the one that's been loose since before the snow. It came out in the soup, which she thought was tremendous, and Pelle made a joke about extra flavor, and then he went out to the woodpile and didn't come back for an hour. The tooth came early. They do, when there isn't enough. I know that from a pamphlet at the church drive three years ago and I wish I didn't. I gave her the heel of the cornbread off my plate and she rated breakfast a five out of ten, generous, she said, on account of the tooth. Cold snap holding. The fence wires sing in it. There were two of them at the east line this morning, doing what they do, which is mostly nothing — standing in the snow like men who've forgotten why they came into a room. The tall one had its head tilted all morning. I have stopped wondering what they look at. Theo split kindling on the porch with his back to the fence the entire time, which is a skill he has, keeping his back to exactly one thing no matter how he turns. I set the salt cellar on the left of the stove this morning before I remembered it's been empty since Tuesday. Left it there anyway. It lives on the left. In the afternoon one of them came at the wire. Not the tall one — a thick one, shoulders like a wet haystack, and it didn't lumber the way they mostly do, it *leaned*, steady, like a man pushing a stalled truck, and the fencepost groaned. Pelle was out the door with the rifle before Gran finished saying his name. One shot. He doesn't waste them. It went down soft, the way they do, no thrash in them, just a kind of agreement with the ground. Then there's the rest of it, which is the part nobody tells you. You can't leave them against the fence. The growth comes up through them after, into the wire, into the post, and then you're digging out the post in March. So you haul them to the burn pile, and they're heavy, heavier than a man that size ought to be, dense like green wood. Pelle took the shoulders and I took the legs, which were not exactly legs at the ankle anymore, and we went a hundred yards in eight rests. At the fourth rest the carcass had split along the side, where the shot went through. I was bent over with my hands on my knees, breathing, and the wind turned. I'm going to write this part plainly because that's the only way I know how to write anything. It smelled like chestnuts. Roasting chestnuts, the cart that used to set up outside the hardware store in October, paper bag warm through your gloves. Under that something like searing pork fat, and under *that* something sweet I didn't have a name for and stood there trying to name. I stood in the snow next to that thing with my mouth running like a faucet and my stomach making a fist, and I thought — I won't write what I thought. Pelle had his head turned away, down the fence line. Checking the wire, maybe. Or maybe his nose works as well as mine, and he was doing the same arithmetic I was doing and not letting me see the carry. We burned it. It went up quick — they do, they're dry inside somehow even fresh — and the smoke smelled like nothing at all, which felt like a mercy and also like a door closing. Don't. I came in and washed my hands twice and made the soup. Ten potatoes now. Junie did her fractions on the counter while I cooked — Theo's got her converting the chore chart into eighths, and she informed me that I am responsible for three-eighths of the household work, which she said was "most," and Theo, mending Pelle's coat by the stove without looking up, said that three-eighths was not most, it was a plurality, and Junie said that wasn't a real word, and he said most things he knew weren't real words anymore. Gran told them both done now and dealt the cribbage hands. Pelle ate fast and went back out to check the post the thick one had leaned on. Took the lantern. Was gone longer than a post takes. Gran won at cribbage by cheating, which Junie tracks now and lets her do, because catching her is its own game and Junie's playing the long one. Junie went up at eight, took the tooth in her fist to put under the pillow. We still do the tooth thing. There's nothing to leave under there, so Gran leaves a bottle cap, and Junie pretends the fairy traffics in bottle caps, and the pretending goes both directions like everything in this house. Theo banked the fire. Going up, he stopped in the doorway — he fills a doorway like a coat hung on a too-short nail — and said the cold would break by Friday, he could feel it. He says things like that when he wants there to be a Friday. Nine potatoes after tomorrow. We had soup.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of writing that demonstrates mastery of voice, restraint, and tonal control. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Craft** The first-person kitchen journal voice is established immediately and maintained with remarkable consistency. Mara's voice is exactly as the character profile describes: practical to the point of evasion, processing everything through tasks and measurement. "It used to take me twenty minutes to do inventory. It took four." This is devastating in its compression. The voice never strains for effect; the dread accumulates through what is not said, through the flat register applied to terrible things. The epigraph recipe is genuinely thin and functional — "salt gone since Tuesday" is a perfect detail that does double work (establishes scarcity, establishes time). The plan called for bland practicality in the epigraphs, and this delivers. **Character Work** Every character introduced here is consistent with their profiles and rendered through behavior rather than description. Theo's back to the fence is a single image that communicates his entire relationship to Lena's absence without a word of explanation. Junie's tooth rating ("five out of ten, generous, she said, on account of the tooth") is exactly the kid-logical honesty the profile describes. Pelle going to the woodpile for an hour after the tooth joke — the profile's "apologizes in firewood" made literal — is beautifully handled. Gran's cribbage cheating and the bottle cap fairy are small, perfect domestic details. The Lena establishment is done with extraordinary economy: the salt cellar on the left, Theo's back to the fence. Two gestures, no explanation, complete emotional information. **The Central Scene** The chestnut smell scene is the chapter's core, and it's handled with exactly the restraint the plan called for. "I won't write what I thought" is the right move — the narrator's self-censorship is itself characterization. The "door closing" metaphor for the smoke smelling like nothing is one of the few metaphors deployed, and it earns its place. The "Don't." paragraph break is a masterstroke — a single word that functions as journal entry, self-command, and structural beat simultaneously. Pelle's possible parallel experience ("maybe his nose works as well as mine, and he was doing the same arithmetic I was doing and not letting me see the carry") is rendered with appropriate uncertainty. The "carry" in arithmetic as metaphor for moral weight is subtle and lands. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional. The Theo/Junie exchange about "plurality" is genuinely funny and characterizes both of them efficiently — Theo's pedantry as fear-tell, Junie's sharpness. "Most things he knew weren't real words anymore" is a line that could tip into performed profundity but is saved by its context (a man correcting a child's vocabulary while mending a coat, not making a speech). Gran's "done now" is exactly right. **Potential Weaknesses** The "wet haystack" simile for the walker's shoulders is slightly strained — it's reaching for texture and lands awkwardly. "Dense like green wood" is better. The "coat hung on a too-short nail" for Theo in the doorway is vivid but perhaps one metaphor too many in a chapter that otherwise deploys them sparingly. These are minor. The "mouth running like a faucet and my stomach making a fist" — the faucet is a cliché, though "stomach making a fist" partially redeems the passage. This is the chapter's weakest moment of prose. **Structural Execution** The chapter plan called for: establishing the household through inventory, Junie's tooth (malnutrition, stated flatly), the fence and walkers, Lena in two sentences sideways, Pelle shooting a walker, the chestnut smell, "We had soup." All of this is executed, and the "We had soup" ending is exactly right — flat, final, the whole chapter's emotional weight compressed into three words. The plan also called for Mara writing the smell down and then writing "don't" — the chapter delivers this as a structural beat rather than a literal journal entry, which is actually more sophisticated. **Overall Assessment** This is writing that trusts its reader completely. The horror is entirely ambient — nothing is announced, nothing is underlined. The cozy-horror balance is achieved through domestic specificity (bottle caps, cribbage, fractions on the chore chart) set against the matter-of-fact presence of the walkers and the starvation. The chapter does exactly what the plan intended and does it with genuine literary skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two — First Taste *Dressing one: open along the seam under the arm, where it splits natural. No blood. It parts like firm mushroom, pale gold all through, and the smell is the worst part, because it isn't bad. Discard the head end whole. Don't look long at the hands.* Two days now without the potatoes. I'm not going to write a paragraph about that. The last one went into Thursday's soup and Junie didn't come downstairs Friday until eleven, and when she did she sat by the stove with her arms inside her shirt sleeves and didn't do her fractions and didn't rate anything. So Friday night, after she was up, I told them about the smell. I'd been carrying it eight days like a stone in my pocket and it came out flat, the way confessions do when you've rehearsed them too long. I said: when the thick one split at the burn pile. I said: chestnuts. I said Pelle already knew, and Pelle looked at the table and didn't deny it, so then everyone knew that too. It wasn't a long argument. Nobody had the strength for a long one. Theo said no. Then he said it again with more words in it: "You don't know what's in them. You don't know *who's* in them." And he looked at me when he said who, and I looked back, and the whole rest of that sentence sat on the table between us, unsaid, where it has lived ever since. Pelle said, "Good. Let them be useful for once." Not loud. He had his hands flat on the table and his second-helping voice on, the one with no joke in it. "They ate Marcus's whole town. They can stand us a winter." Theo started in on spores, contamination, the word *bioaccumulation*, which is his fear-tell, the correcting — and Gran got up. She went to the shelf and took down the sugar tin and unlocked it and set it open in the middle of the table. There was maybe a third of a cup in it. Grey at the edges. She didn't say anything. She sat back down and picked up her cards. That settled it the way nothing else would have, because we'd all assumed the tin was deep, the way you assume the floor is under the rug. If Gran's tin was a third of a cup, there was no bottom under anything. Saturday morning Pelle took one at the north line. Small, narrow through the shoulders, nothing on it, nothing shaped wrong about it except everything. He carried it to the barn alone, wouldn't let me help with the hauling, which I understood later was its own kind of grace — he wanted my hands fresh. I have dressed deer, pigs twice, more chickens than there are stars. I want that on the record before the next part. My hands shook so bad on the first cut I went in crooked, hit something dense, had to back out and start again, and the second cut was worse because by then the smell was out, and the smell, I have said, is the worst part. Roasting chestnuts in a cold barn with your dead wife's brother not watching from the house. I had to stop and stand with my wrists on the table edge like a drunk. Then I told my hands what Lena used to tell me when the tickets stacked up — *it's just the next thing, do the next thing* — and the next thing was the seam under the arm, and after that my hands knew it better than I did. It parts easy. Easier than pig. No blood, no smell of insides, just that pale gold all the way through, faint rings in it like a tree. Four pounds dressed weight off it, which off a thing that size should have been forty. They're dense like green wood to carry and then there's almost nothing in them you'd keep. I don't know where the weight goes. I didn't look for it. I seared a piece the size of a playing card in the dry skillet, because if it was going to be terrible I wanted it terrible fast. It was not terrible. I want to be careful here, because I was hungry, and hunger is a liar, and everything tastes like trumpets when you're starving. But I have been cooking thirty years and I know what I tasted. It went brown like a scallop. It had that chestnut sweet on the nose and under it something dark and meaty, like the crust end of a pork shoulder, and under *that* the thing I couldn't name in the snow, which I still can't name, which is the part I'd pay money for if money were real. Junie ate her piece and held her plate out. Gran ate slow with her eyes shut, which she does for good food and bad memories both, and I don't know which it was. Pelle ate his and said "one for Marcus" and ate another. Theo drank pine-needle tea and watched us, and did not say anything, and his not saying anything took up its whole chair. Here is the part I'd skip if I were writing this for company. We were starved, and you cannot pour into a starved body the way we poured. By midnight Pelle was on the porch being sick over the rail, and Junie was crying with cramp, and I lay on the floor by the stove with my knees up, sweating, thinking *this is it, this is the punishment, it comes this fast.* It wasn't punishment. It was arithmetic. A gut that's had potato water for a month can't take richness, doesn't matter if the richness came off a pig or a fence. Gran, who ate three small pieces slow, was fine, and said so by going to bed. Sunday we ate again. Small. Slow. Junie kept hers down and by evening she was on the counter doing eighths. Biscuit came in while I was cooking, put her nose up at the skillet from across the room, and turned around in the doorway and went back out to the cold. Didn't growl. Just declined. Six out of ten, Junie said. The food was a nine but nobody talked.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that demonstrates genuine craft and restraint. Let me work through it carefully. The epigraph functions exactly as planned: bland, practical, with the horror living in the clinical instruction "Don't look long at the hands." It doesn't wink. It doesn't perform. The dread is in the matter-of-factness. The voice is consistent and earned throughout. Mara's first-person kitchen-journal register is maintained without slipping into self-consciousness. "I'm not going to write a paragraph about that" is exactly right — it tells us she's aware of the journal as a form, aware of what she's suppressing, without making a performance of the suppression. The line "I'd been carrying it eight days like a stone in my pocket and it came out flat, the way confessions do when you've rehearsed them too long" is genuinely good — it's a metaphor that earns its place because it's the kind of thing a practical person would think, not a lyric poet. The argument scene is handled with real skill. The plan called for it to be "short, tired, and ugly rather than philosophical," and it is. Theo's line — "You don't know what's in them. You don't know *who's* in them" — lands because of the italicized "who" and the immediate notation that he looked at Mara when he said it. The unsaid sentence sitting on the table "where it has lived ever since" is one of the chapter's best moments. Pelle's counter is revenge, not pragmatism, exactly as planned. Gran's sugar tin move is devastating in its simplicity — the plan called for it to settle the argument "without a speech," and it does, and the explanation ("we'd all assumed the tin was deep, the way you assume the floor is under the rug") is the kind of domestic metaphor that feels genuinely discovered rather than constructed. The butchery scene is where the chapter takes its biggest risk and mostly succeeds. "Roasting chestnuts in a cold barn with your dead wife's brother not watching from the house" is the chapter's single most compressed emotional moment — it does enormous work in one clause. The detail about Lena's voice ("it's just the next thing, do the next thing") is introduced naturally, not as a locket-beat, and the fact that it's a kitchen instruction repurposed for grief is exactly right for Mara's character. The physical description of the meat — "pale gold all the way through, faint rings in it like a tree" — is specific and strange without being overwrought. The tasting paragraph is careful and honest about its own epistemology: "I want to be careful here, because I was hungry, and hunger is a liar." This is Mara being a reliable narrator about her own unreliability, which is sophisticated. The flavor description is specific enough to be credible without being precious. The refeeding scene is handled with the right tone — "this is it, this is the punishment, it comes this fast" followed immediately by "It wasn't punishment. It was arithmetic" — which is both funny and true and keeps the horror from becoming melodrama. Gran eating three small pieces and being fine is a perfect character beat. Theo's absence from the eating is handled exactly right: "his not saying anything took up its whole chair." This is the chapter's best line. It's not purple, it's not forced — it's the kind of thing you'd think if you were watching someone perform principled silence at a dinner table. Biscuit's exit is clean and ominous without comment. The chapter ends on Junie's rating, which is perfect: "The food was a nine but nobody talked." This is the cozy-horror register the plan aimed for — the child's honest mouth delivering something that's both funny and quietly terrible. Weaknesses: The line "like the crust end of a pork shoulder" is slightly too specific in a way that feels like the author showing off food knowledge rather than Mara thinking. The phrase "everything tastes like trumpets when you're starving" is the one moment where the prose reaches for a slightly too-literary image — "trumpets" is a bit precious for Mara's register. The plan mentioned Pelle saying "one for Marcus" and eating another, which is here and works, but the plan also specified Pelle's counter as revenge-not-pragmatism, and the chapter delivers this well. The plan called for Gran to unlock the sugar tin "instead" of speaking — the chapter executes this but adds the explanation of what it meant, which is slightly over-explained; the image might have been stronger left to stand alone. The dialogue is sparse and functional throughout, which is correct for this chapter. No line feels staged or thesis-delivering. The characters are consistent with their profiles: Mara practical and evasive, Theo prickly and using technical vocabulary as fear-tell, Pelle blunt with his grief, Gran ruling by allocation, Junie rating. The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully, hitting every beat: the confession, the argument, Gran's tin, the bad butchery, the first taste, the midnight sickness, Sunday's smaller meal, Biscuit declining. The writing prompt is served — the horror of eating the mutants is present but the cozy register is maintained. Overall this is a high-quality chapter that demonstrates genuine control of voice, tone, and structure. The few weaknesses are minor and don't undermine the whole. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three — Fence Pork *Smoking: split into strips no thicker than two fingers, the long way of the grain, such as the grain is. Cold smoke, low and slow, apple wood if Pelle will part with it. A day and a night. It takes smoke better than pig does — drinks it. Keeps without salt, which is good, because there is no salt. Note: Pelle is to stop hanging his strips on the middle rack. The middle rack is the good rack. Everyone knows this.* Three weeks since the first one, and the cellar is doing something I had forgotten cellars could do, which is fill. Pelle takes them at the fence line mostly, sometimes a short walk past it with Gran watching the wire behind him. He's particular about it in a way I didn't expect — won't take a shot that isn't clean, waits out the ones that are moving, says you owe a thing a good end even if it isn't a thing anymore. He came back empty Tuesday rather than take a bad angle. Came back with two on Thursday and we worked the barn till dark. I have a system now. The seam, the rings, the four pounds — it's always near four pounds, big ones or small, which I've stopped remarking on. My hands don't shake. That happened somewhere in the second week without my noticing, the way a burn stops hurting. The naming thing came to a head Monday. Junie put her fork down and said she wasn't eating anything called *mutant* anymore, because mutant was a scary word and you can't eat a scary word, and frankly she'd been heroic about it this long. Theo, from the corner, said the accurate term would be fruiting body, and Junie said that was worse, and she was right, it was much worse. So we auditioned names over dinner. This is a real thing that happened and I'm writing it down because Lena would have wanted minutes kept. Pelle opened with *walker bacon*, which lost on the grounds that bacon raises expectations. *Shroom steak* — too restaurant, said Gran, who has never once been to a restaurant I'd cook at. Junie proposed *snow beef*, which had a run of support until Theo said beef was a cow and a cow was an animal and an animal had parents, and what we were eating was the weather, and the table went quiet, and then Junie said, "You can't eat weather, it'd be soup," and the table came back. It was Gran who said *fence pork*. Said it the way she says everything, like it had already been decided last year and we were slow getting the news. It stuck before the plates were cleared. It stuck because it's honest about where and a lie about what, and that's the right mix. That's most of what a name is for. Theo doesn't say it. Theo says *it*, or, when he wants the room to feel him, *them*. He eats his cornmeal and the jarred beans — I opened them; somebody was dying, it just turned out to be nobody — and the pine tea, and he's thinner than the rest of us now by a margin you can see across a room. Junie's birthday was Friday. Nine. Gran unlocked the tin and there was sugar in it again — I don't ask how much she'd been holding back or where, that tin runs on faith — and I made a cake. Cornmeal, rendered fat off the smoking strips, sugar, and the last cocoa tin scraped to silver. Candle stubs, four, because four burning is better than nine unlit, Gran ruled. And Pelle attempted dumplings as his gift, fence-pork dumplings, his own recipe, by which I mean no recipe, and they came to the table grey and seamless like river stones, and Junie bit one and rated it a two, and Pelle demanded a recount, and she revised it to a one, and he laughed till he had to put his head down on the table. The dumplings were genuinely bad. The broth they failed in was genuinely wonderful. The whole dish averaged out, Junie ruled, to a four, carried entirely by the broth, and dinner overall was an eight, docked from nine because her father sang. He did sing. He sang the propane jingle from his old route with her name swapped in, and Gran laughed, which Pelle says makes four lifetime, and he went to the wall by the door where he keeps the tally in pencil and marked it in front of everyone like a man hanging a medal. I stood at the stove with the cake and watched them and the thought arrived plainly, the way true things do: this is the first laughing since Lena. Twenty-six months. I didn't cry about it. I frosted the cake with nothing, which is a technique, and brought it over, and Junie blew out all four stubs in one go and wouldn't tell the wish, and Theo said wishes told are wishes spent, and ate a slice of cake, which had fence-pork fat in it, which I did not say, and which I have now written down instead of saying, which I suppose is worse. Two small things, for the record, because the record is what I keep. Biscuit has moved to the barn. She comes to the porch for her share of cornmeal, takes it off the boards delicately like she's being polite about something, and goes back out. She's warm enough out there in the straw. She's old. Dogs get particular. And at breakfast Saturday, Junie, mouth full, said she'd dreamed of a yellow forest, warm, with a hum in it like the stove makes, and she'd walked around in it and it was nice. Pelle was lifting the kettle and he set it back down on the stove without pouring. Then he poured. I didn't say anything, and I want to be honest in these pages about why, since these pages are where I'm honest: it's because Tuesday night I dreamed it too. Warm, and yellow, and humming, and nice. Nobody said anything. I wrote down the dumpling recipe so it never happens again, and not the dream.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of cozy-horror writing that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Craft** The first-person kitchen journal voice is sustained with impressive consistency. Mara's register is exactly as planned: practical, evasive, processing everything through food and task. The line "I frosted the cake with nothing, which is a technique" is genuinely excellent — funny, sad, and characterologically precise all at once. The voice never slips into self-pity or melodrama. The observation about the burn stopping hurting as an analogy for her hands steadying is earned and unforced. The epigraph works beautifully. "Pelle is to stop hanging his strips on the middle rack. The middle rack is the good rack. Everyone knows this." This is exactly the right tone — domestic, petty, funny, and quietly horrifying in context. It establishes the cozy-horror register without winking at the reader. **Character Execution** The characters are rendered with fidelity to their profiles. Theo's line about "the weather" is perfectly in character — pedantic, cutting, and genuinely philosophically interesting without being a thesis statement. Junie's "You can't eat weather, it'd be soup" is the ideal kid-logical counter, sharp without being precocious-cute. Pelle's tally of Gran's laughs on the wall is a beautiful character detail — sentimental in the way he punishes himself for, but here allowed to be tender. Gran's "fence pork" delivery — "like it had already been decided last year and we were slow getting the news" — is perfect characterization. The Lena moment lands without sentimentality: "this is the first laughing since Lena. Twenty-six months. I didn't cry about it. I frosted the cake with nothing." The restraint here is the craft. The revelation that the cake contained fence-pork fat and Theo ate it, followed by "which I have now written down instead of saying, which I suppose is worse" — this is the chapter's best moment. It foreshadows the Theo betrayal, establishes Mara's complicity-by-omission, and is genuinely chilling without announcing itself. **The Two Wrong Notes** The chapter plan called for two unremarked wrong notes: Biscuit moving to the barn, and the shared yellow-forest dream. Both are executed well. Biscuit's section is handled with exactly the right amount of rationalization — "She's old. Dogs get particular" — which reads as Mara lying to herself in real time. The dream sequence is handled with appropriate restraint: Pelle setting the kettle down and then pouring, Mara's admission that she dreamed it too, and then the deliberate non-recording. "I wrote down the dumpling recipe so it never happens again, and not the dream" is a strong closing line that earns its weight. **Dialogue** The dialogue is largely excellent. The naming-audition scene is genuinely funny and serves multiple purposes simultaneously — character revelation, world-building, tonal establishment. Theo's "fruiting body" correction and Junie's response are both in character and actually funny. The dialogue avoids the trap of being too polished or too on-the-nose. **Minor Weaknesses** The phrase "the way true things do" in "the thought arrived plainly, the way true things do" is slightly soft — it's the one moment where the prose reaches for a small aphorism. It's not egregious but it's the weakest line in the chapter. The observation about Gran never going to a restaurant Mara would cook at is a nice character beat but slightly over-explained — "which has never once been to a restaurant I'd cook at" does the work but could be tighter. The chapter is perhaps slightly less surprising than it could be — the naming scene, while well-executed, is a fairly expected beat in this kind of story. But the execution elevates it above the predictability of the concept. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and well. The cozy peak is achieved. The naming scene happens. Junie's birthday with the dumplings and the real laughter is rendered. The two wrong notes (Biscuit, the dream) are present and unremarked. The plan's instruction to write down the dumpling recipe and not the dream is executed as the closing line. The chapter does not overreach or underdeliver. **Overall Assessment** This is skilled, disciplined writing that achieves its tonal goals with consistency. The horror is genuinely in the gaps — the fat in the cake, the shared dream, the dog's polite distance. The lightness is genuinely earned through character and situation rather than imposed. The restraint is the achievement. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four — Dale *February 9th. No recipe today. Buried one instead of dressing it. Plot by the east wall, past the burn pile, where the ground was soft from under the compost. Junie picked the spot.* I'll set this down in order, because order is what I have. Pelle took it Thursday at the south corner, clean as always, and dragged it to the barn on the sled and went back out for wood, and I started the dressing alone, which is the routine now. The seam under the arm. The rings. My hands going on ahead of me, the way hands do once a thing has become work. The knife caught at the chest, on something hard that wasn't the dense part. I worked the flesh back, figuring stone — they take stones up into themselves sometimes, fence wire once, a whole hinge — and it was flat and white and machine-made, and I cleaned it with my thumb. A name tag. Plastic, the pin-back kind. The supermarket logo across the top, the green one off Route 9, and under that, in the punched-letter tape they used: HI I'M DALE. I knew a Dale. That's the thing I keep arriving at and walking away from. Not knew — Lena did the shopping mostly, but I knew him the way you know a town: lane four, big laugh, asked everyone about the weather like the parking lot wasn't right there in the windows. Bagged slow. Lena liked him because he bagged slow, said it meant he was thinking about your eggs. I can't say it was him. That's the honest sentence. The tag was grown into it the way the hinge was grown into that other one, the flesh gone around it in rings, and a tag can blow forty miles, a tag can be picked up, carried, who knows what they carry or why. It was a tag in a thing, that's all I can swear to. I put the knife down and went up to the house and sat at the table until they noticed me sitting. The conference was short and quiet. I put the tag on the table, wiped clean, and that was most of what I had to say. Junie was sent up for her bath, went without arguing, and listened from the stairs, which everyone knew and nobody stopped, because what would be the point — she eats it too. Pelle turned the tag over twice. Theo didn't touch it. He stood at the window with his back to the room and let us sit in it, and I'll give him this, which costs me: he didn't say what he could have said. He had two months of *I told you* banked and he left it in the bank. That was its own kind of mercy and its own kind of weight, because the unsaid ones don't disperse, they move in. Gran ruled, eventually, the way she rules: by allocation. We don't take the ones wearing anything. We don't take the ones that hesitate at the fence — the ones that stop, that stand, that tilt. Clothing, hesitating, anything shaped too right about the hands: those we leave, or we put down and bury, but we don't dress them. Done now. I wrote the rule on the inside cover of this journal, where the rules go. And I'm going to write under it here the thing I'd never say at the table, because the table is for living together and these pages are for me: the rule is so we can sleep. The thick one that fed us through January wasn't wearing anything, and I don't know what it was before either, and the rule doesn't reach backward, and we won't be checking. A rule like this is a door you close in a house with no walls. We closed it. I slept. We buried Dale Friday morning. I say Dale. The household says Dale now too, which started as Junie being literal and became the name because what else. The ground by the east wall took him easy, soft from the compost heat, and Junie picked the spot because she said he'd want to be where things were already growing, which stopped everyone's breath a second, and she meant nothing by it, and that was that. Pelle filled the grave. Theo came out for it. Stood at the back with his arms crossed and his glasses fogging, and when it was filled he said, "He bagged slow," and went inside. So Theo knew him too. Lane four was everybody's lane. Theo didn't come down for dinner that night, or the next. He's not sulking — I want to be fair — he takes a plate of cornmeal up and he mends by lamplight and he comes down for breakfast civil enough. But his chair at dinner sits there doing the thing his silence does, taking up its space, and Junie has started setting his place anyway, every night, fork and all, and nobody told her to stop. Then there's the rest of the month, which I have been circling for a page. Lean stretch, the back half of February. The walkers thinned out — they do this, they ebb, nobody knows the tides of them — and the strips in the cellar were going down faster than they should, which is a sentence about appetite I'll look at another time. Eleven days without a take. Cornmeal arithmetic again. Junie quiet again. The twelfth day, dusk, Pelle got one at the wire. We hauled it in near dark and I had the lamp up close for the dressing and there was something at the wrist. A band. Or a ridge of growth, banded — they grow in rings, I've said, and sometimes the rings raise up, and at the wrist where it's narrow a raised ring could look like anything. Could look like a watchband. Pelle looked at it a long moment. Then he said, "That's not a watchband. Hand me the saw." I handed him the saw. The entry for that day, in the margin, says four pounds. That's all it says. I'm leaving it that way.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of writing that executes its plan with precision and restraint. Let me work through it carefully. The epigraph functions exactly as intended: a dated entry, two lines, about burying instead of butchering. It's stripped of the recipe format, and that absence is itself meaningful — the chapter plan called for this, and the execution delivers. The tonal shift is immediate and earned. The Dale discovery is handled with exceptional craft. The prose stays in Mara's practical register throughout: "I worked the flesh back, figuring stone — they take stones up into themselves sometimes, fence wire once, a whole hinge." This is exactly the voice established in the plan — a cook who processes through inventory, through the physical facts of work. The detail about the hinge is a perfect piece of world-building that doesn't announce itself. The paragraph about knowing Dale is the chapter's emotional center, and it's beautifully calibrated: "Not knew — Lena did the shopping mostly, but I knew him the way you know a town: lane four, big laugh, asked everyone about the weather like the parking lot wasn't right there in the windows." The parenthetical about the parking lot is a small, precise observation that feels genuinely human rather than constructed. "Lena liked him because he bagged slow, said it meant he was thinking about your eggs" — this is the Lena characterization done sideways, exactly as the plan specified, and it works because it reveals Lena through a stranger's small virtue. The conference scene is excellent. The plan called for it to be "quiet and procedural," and it is. Theo's restraint — "he had two months of I told you banked and he left it in the bank" — is rendered with the right ambivalence. The narrator gives him credit while acknowledging the weight of the unsaid. This is sophisticated moral accounting. Gran's rule-making is handled with characteristic economy: "by allocation." The rule itself is rendered with the right mixture of practicality and self-awareness: "A rule like this is a door you close in a house with no walls." This is the chapter's one genuinely poetic line, and it earns its place because it's immediately followed by the flat "We closed it. I slept." — the narrator doesn't linger on her own metaphor, which is exactly right. Theo's "He bagged slow" at the graveside is devastating in its brevity. The plan called for Theo's absence from dinner to be "worse" than any cutting remark, and the execution delivers: "his chair at dinner sits there doing the thing his silence does, taking up its space." Junie setting his place every night, fork and all, is the kind of detail that accumulates meaning without announcing it. The watchband ending is the chapter's masterstroke. The plan called for Pelle and Mara to look at each other and butcher it anyway, with the journal entry being only the weight of the meat. The execution is precise: Pelle's "That's not a watchband. Hand me the saw" followed by Mara handing him the saw, followed by the four-pound entry. The complicity is enacted, not described. The reader does the moral work. There are minor quibbles. "The unsaid ones don't disperse, they move in" is slightly more self-consciously literary than the rest of the prose, though it doesn't break the register badly. The phrase "stopped everyone's breath a second" is a small cliché in an otherwise cliché-free chapter. The dialogue is sparse and functional — exactly right for this chapter. Every line of speech carries weight: Pelle's "That's not a watchband," Theo's "He bagged slow," Gran's allocation. The plan's instruction to make the conference "quiet and procedural" is honored. The chapter follows its plan with exceptional fidelity: the name tag discovery, the household conference, the rule-making, Theo's absence from dinner, the lean stretch, the watchband, the four-pound entry. Nothing is missed; nothing is added that doesn't belong. The darkness is entirely well-earned. The horror of the watchband scene works because the chapter has done the work of establishing the rule, establishing the lean stretch, establishing the complicity between Pelle and Mara. When Pelle says "That's not a watchband," the reader understands exactly what kind of lie this is and why both of them need it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five — A Good Winter, Considering *March 2nd. Flatbread: cornmeal, water, a spoon of the rendered fat, griddle hot as it goes. The fat is the whole trick. Junie eats four.* — **March 3rd.** Pelle hauled water bare-armed this morning, hard frost, sleeves shoved up like it was June. I watched him from the window do six trips and never once tuck his hands. When he came in I said something about it and he looked down at his arms like they belonged to a neighbor and said, "Huh." Then he did his knee thing — crouch, stand, crouch — which he does at the woodpile now, at the stove, in doorways, like a man testing ice that keeps holding. **March 5th.** Was scrubbing the skillet and noticed my arms. The burn scars — the rack stripe on the left forearm, sixteen years old, my apprenticeship written on me — gone. Not faded. Gone, smooth, the skin there newer than the skin around it. I stood at the basin a long time. Here's the thing I'll only say here: I missed it. That stripe was the night Lena waited out back of the kitchen with ice in a bar towel, the first time she ever touched my arm. Things you'd never trade have a way of getting traded without you. **March 7th.** Wood ran low overnight and nobody noticed because nobody was cold. I'm noting that the way I'd note a leak. **March 9th.** The dream again, which is every night now, and I'm done pretending these pages don't know. The yellow forest. Warm, the hum like a stove two rooms over, the light coming from no particular up. And new: Pelle was in it. Not a figure like him — him, and he saw me, and raised a hand the way you do across a parking lot. At breakfast neither of us said anything and both of us knew, and that's becoming its own kind of conversation, the held kind. **March 11th.** Junie's eyes. I was checking her scalp for nits, old habit, and she looked up and the lamp caught her and there are flecks in the grey now, amber, like something coming up through water. I held her chin and looked too long and she said, "What," and I said nits, all clear, and she went back to her bottle caps. Wrote it here between the bread and the woodpile because I don't know where else it goes. **March 12th.** Pelle saw them too. He didn't say so. What he did was not sleep — I heard him on the porch past two — and the next morning he was loud and funny at breakfast, too loud, the tally-mark voice, and by dinner he'd decided it was fine. I watched him decide. It's what I'd have decided. **March 14th.** Gran, at cards: "I had a name for this hand. I had —" and then she looked at the cards a while and laid down a pair and won anyway. She laughs when the words go. Says, "Well, it'll come back or it won't, like everything." Junie has started finishing her sentences for her, guessing, and Gran nods at the good guesses like a woman at an auction. **March 16th.** None at the fence in nine days. Used to be nine days of ebb meant hunger arithmetic; the cellar's deep enough now that it just means quiet. But it's not ebb. They're there — the tall one stands at the tree line most mornings, the same one, I know its tilt by now — they've just stopped coming *to* us. They stand off at a distance like men outside a church. Pelle walked the whole wire Tuesday and found no lean-marks, no fence-songs at night. Junie waved at the tall one Thursday from the gate. I was right there. I'd like to write that I stopped her. It raised — I'm not going to finish that sentence. It didn't do anything. It stood there. **March 19th.** Biscuit went through the gap behind the barn where the wire's been down-bent since the thaw. Junie saw her go and reported it like weather: didn't run, she said, just walked out, looked back once, kept on. Nobody went after her. Nobody fixed the gap either, all week, and I kept putting it on the chore chart and it kept not getting done, three-eighths of it mine. She was old. She was warm in the barn and fed and old, and dogs get particular, and she just lives outside now, like everybody else, Junie says. I said that's right. The gap got fixed Sunday. I'm reading back what I wrote about her being old and I know what kind of sentence it is. It's a sentence like the rule inside the front cover. I'm leaving it. **March 21st.** Spring smell on the wind, early. Green coming at the fence margins, earlier than green comes. **March 23rd.** Now the one I've been not-writing all week. Theo caught his hand on the smoker latch Monday — a nothing cut, across two knuckles. He won't let anyone near his food but he'll let me dress a wound; we've never discussed the line but we both keep it. So I had his hand in mine by the lamp, and I peeled the old wrapping, and the cut was set in and weeping a little, ordinary, a body doing its slow ordinary work — slow because he's thin, because pine tea and cornmeal don't knit anybody fast. And my mouth ran. Like a faucet, like the burn pile in January. Over a wound. Over *Theo*. I turned my head and made it a cough and wrapped the hand clean and said keep it dry, and he said he knew how wounds worked, he wasn't one of my dumplings, and I laughed the right amount and left the room with my heart going like a kettle. That night by the stove, late, Pelle and I were both not sleeping. I got as far as: "When I dressed Theo's hand—" And he said, "It's the smoking salt." There is no smoking salt. There's been no salt since January and we both stood there knowing it, in the kitchen, in the warm. "Right," I said. "It's the salt." **March 24th.** Flatbread again. Junie ate five.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely impressive piece of fragmented journal writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through it carefully. **Structure and Voice** The dated-entry format works exceptionally well here. The voice is consistent throughout — practical, evasive, self-aware about its own evasions. The epigraph (flatbread recipe, Junie eating four) and the closing mirror (flatbread again, Junie eating five) is a clean structural choice that doesn't announce itself. The voice never slips into self-pity or melodrama, which is the hardest thing to sustain across this kind of material. The prose is largely plain and precise in the way the plan demanded. "Things you'd never trade have a way of getting traded without you" — this is the one line that edges toward aphorism, but it earns it because it's embedded in a specific, physical memory (the burn scar, the bar towel, Lena's first touch). It doesn't float free as wisdom; it's anchored. **Character Work** Mara's voice is exactly as profiled: practical to the point of evasion, lying by omission, aware she's doing it. "I'm reading back what I wrote about her being old and I know what kind of sentence it is. It's a sentence like the rule inside the front cover. I'm leaving it." This is excellent — the narrator catching herself in the act of self-deception and choosing to leave the lie in place. That's sophisticated characterization. Pelle's "It's the smoking salt" is the chapter's best moment. The plan called for this beat and the execution is clean: the lie is transparent to both parties, they both know it, and they agree to it anyway. The line "There is no smoking salt. There's been no salt since January and we both stood there knowing it, in the kitchen, in the warm" — this is the chapter's emotional peak and it lands because it's been earned by everything preceding it. Gran's language deterioration is handled with restraint: "I had a name for this hand. I had —" and then she wins the hand anyway. The detail of Junie finishing her sentences and Gran nodding "like a woman at an auction" is precise and strange in the right way. Junie's amber flecks, reported between bread and woodpile — the placement is doing real work. The narrator's discomfort is shown through where she puts things in the journal, not through explicit statement. **The Biscuit Section** This is the chapter's most complex passage. "She was old. She was warm in the barn and fed and old, and dogs get particular, and she just lives outside now, like everybody else, Junie says." The self-deception is visible and the narrator knows it and says so. "I'm reading back what I wrote about her being old and I know what kind of sentence it is." This is the plan's "plainly a lie she's telling herself" executed with real craft — the narrator is both lying and confessing to lying in the same breath. **The Salivation Beat** The plan called for "one frightening symptom they rationalize together — Mara salivating at Theo's wound." The execution is good: "And my mouth ran. / Like a faucet, like the burn pile in January. Over a wound. Over *Theo*." The italicized "Theo" does real work — it's not just a wound, it's *him*, the last piece of Lena. The horror is specific. The comparison to the burn pile (the first time she smelled the walkers) is a callback that rewards attention. **Minor Weaknesses** The line "like men outside a church" for the walkers standing at the tree line is slightly generic — it's the one simile that feels chosen for atmosphere rather than precision. The plan warned against prose that performs, and this one edges that way. "The held kind" (referring to the unspoken conversation with Pelle) is a small flourish that's slightly self-congratulatory — the sentence before it already does the work. The March 7th entry ("Wood ran low overnight and nobody noticed because nobody was cold. I'm noting that the way I'd note a leak.") is efficient and good, but "I'm noting that the way I'd note a leak" is a slight tell — the narrator explaining her own tone, which she doesn't usually need to do. **Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely and well. The gifts (scars gone, Pelle's cold-resistance, shared dream, Gran's language, Junie's eyes), Biscuit's departure, the salivation beat, the smoking salt exchange — all present and executed. The plan's instruction to write the dumpling recipe and not the dream is honored (the dream is written, but the *content* of the dream is kept spare, and the chapter ends on flatbread). The walker raising something at the gate — the plan says "It raised — I'm not going to finish that sentence" — is handled with exactly the right restraint. **Overall Assessment** This is a high-quality chapter that executes a difficult plan with consistent craft. The voice is sustained, the horror is embedded in the domestic, the characters are recognizable and specific, and the emotional beats land without announcement. The weaknesses are minor: one or two moments where the prose slightly performs, one generic simile. The "smoking salt" exchange is genuinely excellent writing — two people agreeing to a lie they both know is a lie, in a warm kitchen, and the warmth being part of what's wrong. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six — Pine-Needle Tea *Pine-needle tea, for Theo. New growth if there is any, the tips. Don't boil the needles, you scald the green out — pour the water just off the boil and let it sit seven minutes, covered. He times it. Of course he times it.* The woodpile took him Thursday. He was splitting rounds with his back to the fence the way he does, and the maul glanced — off a knot, he said, though I'd watched him work all month like a man arguing with the wood, swinging harder than the job wanted — and the edge caught him above the knee. Deep. Junie was the one who heard him and her shout brought me out with my hands still floured. We got him in, got it cleaned and closed as well as boiled thread closes anything, and I want it written that for a day we thought we'd been lucky. The medicine box has been a box of dead air since October. Two aspirin, a rubber tube, iodine bottle dry as a complaint. I keep it on the shelf anyway because a shelf with a medicine box on it is a different shelf. By Saturday the leg was hot. By Sunday the heat had a smell, and red was tracking up from the wound the way frost tracks up a window, and Theo's fever came on in the afternoon like weather you can watch arrive across a field. Here is the thing about a sickroom in this house now. We have all of us spent two winters learning what we can survive, and the lesson lately has been: nearly anything. Pelle's knee, my scars, cuts that close overnight clean and pink. And there was Theo in the bed, the only one of us running on cornmeal and pine tea and principle, with his body doing what bodies always did before, which is lose slowly, and all five of us in the house knew the arithmetic and nobody said it. It sat at his bedside with us anyway. It took up its chair the way his silences do at dinner. The broth was downstairs in the cellar in jars and every person who came through that room walked through the knowing of it. Pelle said it out loud Sunday night, in the kitchen, because Pelle is the one who says things.
