Not cruel — practical, low, his rifle-cleaning voice: strain it fine, it could pass for anything, the man's out of his head half the time, he'd never — "No." Gran was in the doorway. And then she came apart on it, the words, worse than cards: "He gets to — he's the only one still — he gets to be the —" and her hand was shaking on the doorframe, and she could not land the sentence, and the rage of not landing it was terrible to watch. Pelle stood there and took it. Finally she just said, "No. Done now," and the no held, because her no's hold, and she went up the stairs slow on the bad hip and we heard her stop halfway and stand. I took the night sitting. Junie had been in before bed — she's the only one Theo's body relaxes around, I've watched it, the line of his shoulders comes down half an inch — and she'd read to him from one of his four paperbacks, the one about the mountain, doing the voices wrong on purpose until he told her which wrongness was closest. Around two he surfaced. The fever backed off the way it does at night's bottom, cruelly, just enough to give him himself. He knew where he was. He knew what was in the cellar. He'd done all the arithmetic days ago — he's the science teacher, he was always going to be ahead of us on it. He took my wrist. His grip had nothing in it, which was the worst part. "Not while I can't choose," he said. "Mara. Whatever I am when this is over — even if it's nothing — I was the one who didn't. You hear me. Promise it." I said I promise. I want that written plain. I said the words I promise, in the dark, with my hand in his, to Lena's brother. Monday night was the worst night. The fever climbed past where it had been and he stopped surfacing. He talked — to his classroom, to the maul, once to Lena, and I will not write what he said to Lena, that belongs to him even unconscious. His breathing went shallow and quick like Junie's used to in the bad winter. Gran sat with him till midnight and then her hip made her go down, and Pelle had the early watch, and from midnight to four it was me and the lamp and that breathing. I went down to the cellar at around three. I'm not going to dress this up and I'm not going to flog myself across four pages either. This journal gets the plain version. I strained the broth twice through cloth until it ran thin and pale as barley water. It could pass for anything. It passed for anything. I sat on the edge of the bed and I fed it to him in spoonfuls, slow, the way you feed the very old, and he took it the way the fevered take anything, which is without knowing, which was the entire point and the entire crime. He kept it down. I washed the cup and the pot and the cloth and the spoon before Pelle came up at four, and the entry for Monday in this journal said: *bad night. he kept some broth down.* Both sentences true. I'm a good liar in writing. It's the same skill as recipes — what you leave out is the dish. He turned the corner Wednesday. By Friday the fever was gone, and the wound — the wound was a week ahead of any wound he had a right to. Closed, dry, the skin coming in smooth and pink and too clean, a scar like mine aren't anymore. Saturday he was propped up, lucid, thin as a fence slat. He looked at the leg a long time. He touched the scar with two fingers, the way you'd touch wet paint. Then he looked at me in the doorway with the tray. Neither of us said it. There aren't words assigned to it; I made sure of that when I wrote *broth* in this book like a coward. He eats nothing I cook now. He boils his own water, seven minutes, and his pot has moved to its own corner of the stove, and on the chore chart, in his handwriting, the cooking column next to his name says *self*, and Junie hasn't asked why, which means she knows. The tea recipe at the top of this page is for whoever keeps this book after me. Make it right. It's all he takes from the kitchen now, and only if Junie carries it up.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Craft** The first-person kitchen journal voice is sustained with exceptional discipline. Mara's voice is exactly as the profile describes: practical to the point of evasion, processing everything through tasks, lying by omission. The line "Both sentences true. I'm a good liar in writing. It's the same skill as recipes — what you leave out is the dish" is genuinely excellent — it's self-aware without being self-congratulatory, and it earns its metaphor because Mara is a cook and the comparison is organic, not decorative. This is the kind of poetic moment that lands because it's rooted in character rather than authorial performance. The prose is largely clean and controlled. "The fever climbed past where it had been and he stopped surfacing" is good. "His grip had nothing in it, which was the worst part" is devastating in its plainness. The chapter avoids the trap of over-writing the emotional peaks — the betrayal scene is rendered in flat, procedural language that makes it more disturbing, not less. **The Betrayal** The plan called for the betrayal to "actually happen and leave a mark," and it does. The execution is careful: Mara doesn't dress it up, doesn't flagellate herself excessively, and the journal entry "bad night. he kept some broth down" is exactly the kind of omission-as-lie the character profile promised. The aftermath — Theo's pot moved to its own corner, "self" written on the chore chart, Junie not asking why — is handled with the restraint the plan demanded. The silence between Mara and Theo after is more damning than any confrontation would be. **Gran's Scene** Gran's breakdown in the doorway is one of the chapter's strongest moments. "He gets to — he's the only one still — he gets to be the —" captures her language deterioration (established in the plan as her transformation symptom) while also being emotionally true. She can't finish the sentence because the sentence is about Theo being the last human one, and she can't say that. The rage of not landing it is well-observed. This is consistent with her profile and the chapter plan. **Theo's Promise Scene** "Not while I can't choose" is a strong line. The grip "had nothing in it, which was the worst part" — this is good. The scene is brief and doesn't overstay. The plan called for Theo to make Mara promise, and she does, and the chapter is honest about what that promise means and what she does with it. **Minor Weaknesses** The opening epigraph is well-executed — the parenthetical "He times it. Of course he times it" is a nice touch that characterizes Theo without belaboring it. "It sat at his bedside with us anyway. It took up its chair the way his silences do at dinner." — This is the chapter's most overtly literary moment. It works, but it's slightly more performed than the rest of the prose. The personification of the unspoken thought is a small risk that mostly pays off. "The lesson lately has been: nearly anything" — this is a good sentence. The chapter follows its plan closely: the sickroom over four days, Gran's "no" (angry, mid-sentence, losing words), Theo's lucid promise, the betrayal with strained broth, the too-fast healing, the aftermath of Theo boiling his own water. The plan is executed with fidelity and craft. One small issue: "thin as a fence slat" is a slightly too-neat metaphor given the "fence pork" euphemism — it feels like it might be an accidental echo rather than an intentional one, and it's the kind of thing that could read as cute rather than earned. The chapter does not include the Lena thread that the plan mentioned for chapter 7 (appropriately — that's next chapter), and it correctly leaves the moral weight unresolved rather than adjudicated. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. Pelle's practical suggestion, Gran's fragmented refusal, Theo's "Not while I can't choose" — all are in character and none are over-written. The chapter avoids the trap of having characters articulate the theme. **Overall** This is a high-quality chapter that executes a difficult tonal balance — cozy-horror, domestic apocalypse, moral betrayal rendered in kitchen language — with genuine skill. The prose is controlled, the characters are consistent with their profiles, the plan is followed faithfully, and the emotional beats are earned rather than manufactured. The darkness is well-earned precisely because it's delivered in the same register as a recipe. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven — The One at the Gate *For company: dumplings stretch further than strips, count your chairs before you mix. A short measure of meat in each goes further than honesty would. Broth does the rest. Broth always does the rest.* False spring, and I mean false. It's February by the calendar Theo keeps — he keeps it scratched on the barn wall and corrects us when we drift, and we drift, the days run together like a sauce now — but there's green at the fence margins three weeks early. New grass in the lee of the porch. The yard willow budding. And here is the part I walked twice to be sure of: it's only us. Take the lane a quarter mile in any direction and the world is brown and locked and right for February. The green comes up in a circle, and the circle is the house, and you can stand on the rise by the burn pile and see the shape of it like spilled paint. Junie says the yard is early because we're warm. She says it the way she says everything, like it's been in the almanac for years. I wrote it down because nobody offered a better theory, and nobody tried. Wednesday morning one came up the lane. I want to get the order of it right. Junie saw it first, from the window, and didn't shout. That's the detail I keep returning to. Two winters of drill — *you see one inside the fence line, you shout* — and instead she said, in the voice you'd use for snow starting, "There's one at the gate." It was the tall one. The tilt. It came up the middle of the lane, not fast, not slow, the way the mailman used to come, and it stopped at the gate — outside it, mind, a thing that has leaned through wire all winter long stopped at a *gate*, at the suggestion of a gate — and it stood. Pelle was out the door with the rifle, and I went after him, and we stood on the porch maybe thirty feet from it. It didn't press. It didn't tilt at us. It stood at the gate the way a man stands at a door he's knocked on. Pelle put the rifle up. I watched him do everything that comes before a shot, the breath, the settle, all of it in order, and then the order ran out and nothing came after. A full minute. The bead on it and his finger laid along the guard and the morning going on around us, the willow, the dripping eaves. He lowered it. He said, "Can't say why." I can't either. That's the truthful sentence for this page. It wasn't mercy and it wasn't fear. It was like raising a rifle at the tall neighbor leaning on your gate, the wrongness of the gesture, your arm answering before your reasons do. There's a hum I've stopped pretending I don't hear — in the cellar among the jars, in my own chest some evenings, in the yellow forest nightly — and standing on that porch the hum and the morning were the same thing, and you can't shoot a morning. It stayed three hours. We did the chores. I want that written because it's the maddest part: we did the chores, with it standing there, Junie at the window narrating like a wireless — *it's still there. It moved its arm. It's looking at the willow now* — and the house just went on around it the way a pond goes on around a heron. Gran came down for it, stood at the window with her braid over her shoulder, looked a long time and said, "Well." Just that. Theo came to the window too. He's up and around now, the limp settling into him like it means to stay. He stood the longest of anyone, and his face did something I haven't seen it do in two years, and then he said, very quietly, not to me, not to anyone: "It walked like—" And stopped. And left the room, the limp going down the hall, the door of his room clicking shut not loud. I'm not going to write the rest of his sentence. I've turned this page's worth of paper into ash twice tonight getting that decided, and it's decided. The tall one's tilt is a tilt. Lots of people walked lots of ways. Lane four was everybody's lane and the lane to this gate isn't anybody's, and that's where I'm leaving it, in this book and in my chest. By afternoon it was gone. None of us saw it go — that itself, five of us in a house with windows on three sides and nobody saw it go. It left something on the gatepost. A cluster, golden, the size of two fists together — fruit-bodies, Theo's word, the only time he's offered a word for any of this since the leg. They sit up off a shared stem like pears that grew wrong, and the smell off them is the smell, the chestnut smell, but over-ripened, gone toward honey and toward something under the honey. Pelle wouldn't touch it with bare hands. Junie wanted to and was told no by four voices at once, the fastest the house has agreed on anything all winter. Nobody ate them. I want that on the record. Nobody so much as broke one open. But here is what we also didn't do: we didn't burn them, and we didn't bury them, and the burn pile was right there. Gran went out at dusk with the blue mixing bowl, the good one, and gathered the cluster off the post like a woman taking in eggs, and set the bowl on the kitchen sill where the light comes in the morning, and when Pelle asked her what in God's name, she said, "They're —" and waited for the word, and it didn't come, and she shrugged and straightened the bowl an inch and that was the explanation. They're. They sit there now. Like cut flowers. The kitchen smells faintly of honey and the other thing. Junie counted chairs at dinner and counted six, with Theo's place set like always, and I told her six was right, and she said she knows, that's why she said it. Dumpling count for the pot: fourteen. I'm writing the count because it's what I can write.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Execution of the Plan** The chapter follows its blueprint with impressive fidelity. The false spring with the green circle around the house is rendered precisely as planned. The walker at the gate, Pelle's aborted shot, Theo's unfinished sentence, Gran's bowl of fruit-bodies on the sill — all present and executed with craft. The dumpling count as the final line, substituting for what can't be written, lands exactly as the plan intended. The chapter plan called for Junie to narrate the walker "like weather" and she does, with the wireless metaphor being one of the chapter's best moments. **Voice and Prose Quality** Mara's voice is consistent and strong throughout. The journal register — practical, evasive, self-aware about its evasions — holds. "I've turned this page's worth of paper into ash twice tonight getting that decided, and it's decided" is excellent: it shows the narrator's active suppression without telling us what she's suppressing, and the physical act of burning drafts is both literal and metaphorical without straining. The line "you can't shoot a morning" is the chapter's most poetic moment and it earns its lyricism because it's grounded in the hum that's been established across prior chapters. The epigraph works well — "A short measure of meat in each goes further than honesty would" is quietly devastating in context, and "Broth always does the rest" echoes the Theo betrayal from Chapter 6 without announcing itself. **Character Work** Theo's unfinished sentence is handled with exactly the restraint the plan demanded. The plan said "Theo, at the window, very quietly — 'It walked like —' and stops, and leaves the room" and the execution adds the detail of the limp going down the hall, the door clicking "not loud" — that "not loud" is a small, perfect touch. Mara's decision not to write the rest of his sentence, and her reasoning ("Lane four was everybody's lane"), is the chapter's emotional core and it's handled with appropriate obliqueness. Junie's "wireless" narration is pitch-perfect for her character — kid-logical, matter-of-fact, slightly eerie. Her counting six chairs and saying "I know, that's why I said it" is one of the chapter's best moments: it's Junie being Junie, but it also carries weight about who the sixth place might be for. Gran's "They're —" with the word not coming is consistent with her established decline and is genuinely moving without being sentimental. Pelle's "Can't say why" is exactly right for him — the man who confuses momentum with courage, stopped by something he can't articulate. **Weaknesses** The hum passage — "There's a hum I've stopped pretending I don't hear — in the cellar among the jars, in my own chest some evenings, in the yellow forest nightly — and standing on that porch the hum and the morning were the same thing, and you can't shoot a morning" — is the chapter's most vulnerable moment. "The hum and the morning were the same thing" is doing a lot of work and it's slightly abstract in a way that risks feeling like performed profundity. It mostly earns it, but it's the one place where the prose reaches past what the narrator's register would naturally produce. The "pond goes on around a heron" simile is good but slightly more literary than Mara's established voice — she's a line cook, not a nature writer. It's a minor note. The chapter is perhaps slightly less surprising than the best chapters in this kind of work — the walker-at-the-gate scenario is well-executed but the beats (Pelle can't shoot, Gran takes the fruit-bodies inside) are somewhat predictable given the story's trajectory. The plan called for this and the execution is faithful, but the chapter doesn't quite transcend its blueprint the way the best chapters do. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. "Can't say why" is perfect. "Well." for Gran is perfect. "They're —" is perfect. Junie's wireless narration is excellent. Theo's truncated sentence is the chapter's emotional climax and it's handled with appropriate restraint. The dialogue never overstays its welcome. **Overall** This is high-quality literary fiction executing a sophisticated plan with consistent craft. The voice holds, the characters are true to their profiles, the horror is earned through restraint, and the ending (dumpling count) is exactly right. Minor quibbles about one slightly overreaching metaphor and a simile slightly outside the narrator's register don't significantly diminish what is a very strong chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eight — Setting the Table *To whoever finds this kitchen: the salt is in the cellar now, second shelf, behind the jars — there's salt again, ask me nothing about how the trade works, things arrive at the gatepost and things are taken, that's the whole ledger. Sear the strips hot and fast, fat side down first. If you've been hungry a while, eat slow. We learned that the hard way, and you don't have to.* Spring proper now. Real spring, the calendar's own, though ours got here first and the seam where our green met the world's green has grown over like everything grows over. We moved the long table outside the first warm week and it never came back in. Pelle and I carried it out — it took four of us to carry it out of the church hall three years ago, and this spring it took two, and neither of us remarked on that, and I'm remarking on it here, once, and done. So: the household, set down plainly, the way I set down inventory, because this book has always been inventory at its heart. Junie is nine and a half and tall out of all her hems, which Theo lets down with his mouth full of pins and his put-upon face on. Her eyes went all the way amber in April. They're beautiful. I'm allowed to say that here. She goes barefoot in frost and her feet come back warm, and she's out the gate most mornings to the tree line and back before breakfast, and we let her, and I remember when that sentence would have been a horror, and I can remember it the way you remember being afraid of the dark in a house you've since lived in for years. Pelle's tally on the wall by the door says seven now. Gran doesn't laugh in words much anymore but a laugh's a laugh and he counts them, and pencils them, and the day she goes fully quiet I believe he'll keep counting by the eyes. Gran hums. That's most of her talk now — she allocates by hand on a shoulder, by the tap of the spoon, by pushing the pot toward you, and the house runs on it fine, better than fine, because the truth I can write here is that most of what anyone ever said out loud was just pointing with extra steps. She started asking me what's for dinner sometime in April — asking, not telling — and I understood what was being handed over, and there was no ceremony to it, and that was the ceremony. Sunday Junie braids her hair and Gran hums the propane jingle, which means she's teasing Pelle, which means she's still entirely in there, just past where the words live. The ones at the tree line stand most days now, two or three, sometimes more of a gathering. They don't come past the gate. They're like relatives at the edge of a wedding photo, the great-uncles nobody's sure of, present and apart. The tall one is often there. I look at its tilt and I let the looking be just looking. Things arrive on the gatepost — the golden clusters, once a dead pheasant, once, I swear this on the book itself, a jar, an actual mason jar, river-washed. We leave things too. Junie handles that side of the ledger. She says they like the dumplings best and I have stopped asking how she knows what they like, because the answer is the hum, and the hum is not a thing this book can hold. The yellow forest every night. We're all there now, all five — I'll get to that — and Gran talks in it. I'll leave that there. Gran talks in it, and the first morning after I heard her, I cried into the washing-up where no one could see, and it wasn't grief, or it wasn't only. Now the part I've sat with longest. Tuesday morning I came down first, like always, and there was a bowl in the basin. Stew bowl, used, wiped the way Theo wipes things, which is to say badly, the man has many skills and dishwashing was never among them — there's a way he leaves the rim that I'd know in the dark. The stew Monday night was fence pork. There wasn't anything else in the pot to have. I stood at the basin a while with the morning coming up through the window and the bowl in my two hands. Then I washed it properly, the way it deserves, dried it, and put it away in the stack with the others, fifth bowl down, no bowl different from any bowl. He came down at his usual hour. Took his pot to his corner of the stove. Seven minutes. We talked about the hems and the weather, and I am not going to write anything else about Tuesday, here or anywhere, ever. Some things you season by leaving out. He was the one who didn't, for as long as didn't was a thing a person could carry, and that's how it'll stand in this book, and this book is the record. His place is set tonight like every night. Junie counts six chairs and gets six. It's evening now. The table's laid out under the willow, and the smell off the skillet is going out over the yard, chestnut and the dark thing under it that I never did name and have stopped trying to, because some tastes are the name. Junie's lighting the stub-ends of the candles. Pelle's threatening to sing. I'm setting an extra place. I do it most nights now. Pelle teases me for it and I let him, because the truth doesn't need the table to know it: it's for whoever comes up the lane. Travelers do, lately. Hungry ones, walking how the hungry walk — I know that walk from inside it. Some stay a meal. One stayed a week. The lane brings who it brings, and you can't know who's out there, and some of them have been walking such a long time, and if it's ever — if whoever it is comes to the gate and stands there the way you stand at a door you've knocked on, there will be a chair, and a bowl, and the salt on the left of the stove where it lives. So this last page is for you, whoever's reading it, standing in my kitchen with your stomach making a fist. I know. I remember. The pot on the stove is banked warm and it's meant for you — that's not a figure of speech, I bank it every night on purpose, feeding people is the whole of what I know how to mean. Eat slow. There's plenty. There's seconds. The soup keeps fine till morning.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with the epigraph-as-recipe, which functions exactly as planned: bland, practical, guileless, with the horror living in the gaps ("ask me nothing about how the trade works, things arrive at the gatepost and things are taken"). This is the voice working at its best — the narrator's incuriosity about her own situation is the dread, not any winking acknowledgment of it. The household inventory section is well-handled. Junie's amber eyes described as "beautiful" with "I'm allowed to say that here" is a quietly devastating moment — the narrator's self-permission to find the transformation lovely is more chilling than any horror-register description would be. The detail of Junie going to the tree line and back before breakfast, and Mara's reflection that "I remember when that sentence would have been a horror," is exactly the right register: the narrator is aware of the change but has metabolized it completely, and her equanimity is the horror. Gran's decline into near-wordlessness, rendered through the hum and the gesture, is handled with real restraint. "Most of what anyone ever said out loud was just pointing with extra steps" is a line that earns its place — it's not purple, it's Mara's practical voice finding a genuine insight. The detail of Gran talking in the yellow forest dream, and Mara crying into the washing-up, is the chapter's most emotionally honest moment. The Theo bowl scene is the chapter's centerpiece and it largely delivers. "The man has many skills and dishwashing was never among them — there's a way he leaves the rim that I'd know in the dark" is excellent characterization through domestic detail. The decision to wash it properly and put it "fifth bowl down, no bowl different from any bowl" is the right gesture — it's Mara's love language (care, erasure of difference, feeding as acceptance) rendered in action. "Some things you season by leaving out" is a line that could tip into self-congratulatory writerly cleverness, but it's in Mara's voice and she's a cook, so it earns its place. The plan called for Theo's bowl to be found and for Mara to "write nothing about it" — the chapter does something slightly different and arguably better: she writes about it but declares she won't write more, which is more honest to the character's self-aware evasion. The line "He was the one who didn't, for as long as didn't was a thing a person could carry" is the chapter's best sentence — it honors Theo's resistance without diminishing it, and the phrase "a thing a person could carry" is genuinely earned. The Lena thread: the plan called for an extra place set "maybe, never said, for Lena" and the chapter delivers this with appropriate obliqueness. "If it's ever — if whoever it is comes to the gate" — the interrupted syntax is doing real work here, the narrator catching herself and redirecting. The salt on the left of the stove, established in chapter 1, pays off quietly. This is good structural memory. The final paragraphs are where the chapter is most vulnerable to criticism. "Feeding people is the whole of what I know how to mean" is slightly too neat, too quotable — it risks becoming the aphorism the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The plan said "no aphorisms" and this line is close to one. Similarly, "The soup keeps fine till morning" as a last line is effective but perhaps slightly too composed, too aware of itself as a last line. The plan called for the narrator to be "entirely guileless" and there's a faint sense here of the prose knowing it's ending. The "great-uncles nobody's sure of, present and apart" metaphor for the walkers at the tree line is good — domestic, slightly absurd, exactly right for Mara's register. The mason jar detail ("I swear this on the book itself") is a nice touch of voice. What the chapter does exceptionally well: it maintains the narrator's voice consistently throughout, it executes the plan's key beats (the bowl, the extra place, Gran's transformation, Junie's eyes), it earns its emotional moments through accumulation rather than announcement, and it resists the temptation to make the horror explicit. The complicity is complete and the narrator is genuinely unaware of her own chill. Minor weaknesses: the "yellow forest" section is slightly rushed — the dream has been built up across chapters and its payoff here (Gran talks in it) is handled in two sentences that feel slightly thin. The walkers-as-relatives metaphor, while good, is the chapter's most "literary" moment and sits slightly proud of the surrounding prose. The final address to the reader ("So this last page is for you") risks breaking the journal frame in a way that feels slightly calculated. On dialogue: there's almost none, which is appropriate for a final chapter that's largely reflection. What exists (Pelle threatening to sing, the Tuesday conversation about hems and weather) is handled with appropriate lightness. Overall this is a high-quality piece that executes a difficult tonal challenge — cozy horror, guileless narrator, earned transformation — with real skill. The plan is followed closely and the character voices are consistent. The prose is controlled without being cold. The few weaknesses are minor and don't undermine the whole. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of post-apocalyptic horror-adjacent fiction that earns its "cozy-horror" designation through sustained tonal control. The narrator's voice — a cook, a survivor, a keeper of records — is established immediately and maintained with remarkable consistency across all eight chapters. The journal/recipe format is not merely decorative; it does genuine structural work, with the recipe headers functioning as emotional counterpoint to the chapter content (the absence of a recipe in Chapter Four, replaced by a burial note, is a genuinely affecting formal choice). **Strengths:** The prose voice is distinctive and controlled. Sentences like "It's a sentence like the rule inside the front cover. I'm leaving it." demonstrate a writer who understands that restraint is its own form of expression. The narrator's habit of writing around things — the watchband, the broth fed to Theo, the tall one's tilt — creates genuine dread and emotional weight without melodrama. Character differentiation is strong. Pelle (practical, grieving, the one who says things), Theo (principled, scientific, ultimately human), Gran (ruling by allocation, losing words but not authority), Junie (the child who processes horror through rating systems and fractions), and the narrator (the cook, the keeper, the one who lies in writing) are all distinct and consistent. The detail of Junie rating meals — "six out of ten, generous, she said, on account of the tooth" — is a brilliant recurring device that grounds the horror in domestic normalcy. The Dale chapter is the moral and emotional center of the piece, and it's handled with genuine sophistication. The rule Gran establishes ("we don't take the ones wearing anything") is presented honestly as a psychological mechanism rather than a genuine ethical solution, and the narrator's self-awareness about this ("A rule like this is a door you close in a house with no walls") is exactly right. The Theo arc — his principled refusal, the fever, the narrator's betrayal, his eventual silent capitulation — is the most emotionally complex thread and is handled with admirable restraint. The detail of the stew bowl wiped badly, the narrator washing it and saying nothing, is genuinely moving. The transformation elements (amber eyes, healed scars, the yellow forest dreams, the cold tolerance) are introduced gradually and with appropriate ambiguity. The piece wisely never resolves whether this is contamination, symbiosis, or something else entirely. **Weaknesses:** Some metaphors strain: "his second-helping voice on, the one with no joke in it" is slightly unclear. "Dense like green wood" is used twice (once for carrying, once for the weight), which feels like a repetition that wasn't intentional. "His silence does, taking up its chair" is used multiple times as a construction, which starts to feel like a tic. The final chapter's turn toward hospitality and the "extra place set for travelers" risks sentimentality — the cozy resolution feels slightly too neat given the moral weight accumulated. The piece earns most of its darkness but the lightness of the ending, while thematically appropriate to "cozy-horror," is perhaps a half-step too comfortable. The "poetic riffing" is largely controlled and earned, but occasionally tips into self-consciousness: "you can't shoot a morning" is the kind of line that calls attention to itself in a way the rest of the prose carefully avoids. The world-building around the spore mutants is deliberately minimal, which mostly works, but the mechanics of the gatepost exchange (things arriving, things being left) in the final chapter feel slightly rushed compared to the careful pacing of earlier revelations. The dialogue is sparse and functional — appropriately so for this narrator's voice — but when it appears it's generally strong. Gran's fragmented speech is particularly well-rendered. **Overall:** This is a genuinely impressive piece of sustained creative writing. The formal choices are purposeful, the voice is consistent, the emotional beats are earned, and the horror-coziness balance is maintained throughout. It's not quite a masterpiece — the ending softens slightly too much, and there are occasional moments where the self-conscious literariness shows — but it's well above average work. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Pacing: 17 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 85.8/100
+ Victorian Romance — The Upper Window (Chapter Avg: 86.5 | Final: 90.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **The prompt:** "One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows." This first-person, wry voice suggests someone who habitually enters manors clandestinely—a thief, a spy, a secret lover, a governess sneaking back in after curfew, a lady climbing into her own home to avoid scandal. **Who climbs through upper windows in a Victorian Romance?** 1. **A cat burglar / jewel thief (woman)** who climbs into a lord's manor and is caught—or nearly caught—by the lord himself, who turns out to be intrigued rather than outraged. Class tension, danger, eventual romance. He could be in debt, complicit, or hiding his own secrets. 2. **A secret lover:** A young man of low birth in love with the daughter of the house, climbing the ivy night after night. Risk of discovery, dueling suitors, an arranged engagement looming. Classic but maybe too predictable unless twisted. 3. **A lady's companion or governess** sneaking in and out to conduct some secret business—writing scandalous novels, attending political meetings (suffrage precursors), nursing a sick relative she's hidden, or moonlighting as something improper. The master of the house notices the patterns. Romance builds through suspicion turning to fascination. 4. **A woman locked out of her own life:** A widow whose brother-in-law has seized her home; she climbs in to retrieve letters/documents proving her inheritance. A solicitor or investigator helps/hinders her. Romance with the man hired to catch her. 5. **A thief and a thief:** Two burglars hit the same manor on the same night, collide in the dark, must cooperate. Banter, rivalry, romance. Fun, capery tone. 6. **The gender-flip caper:** A gentlewoman fallen on hard times, stealing back items pawned/swindled from ruined families—a Robin Hood figure. The hero is a baronet whose house she enters, only to find he's already sold the item she came for... or that he recognizes her from a ballroom. Double life: by day she moves in society; by night she climbs. **Tone options:** Gothic and brooding; light caper with wit; melancholy and yearning; sensation-novel (Wilkie Collins) flavor with secrets and inheritance plots. **Voice:** First person fits the prompt's aphoristic line perfectly—ideal opening sentence. A dry, competent narrator with secrets. **Possible 8-chapter shapes (using idea 6 as strongest seed):** - Ch 1: Establish narrator (call her Eleanor / "the Magpie"?), her trade, her reasons (father ruined by a swindler; she retrieves what was stolen from victims). A climb gone slightly wrong—she's seen by the manor's owner, Julian, Lord Ashworth, who lets her go—or she escapes barely, but drops something identifying. - Ch 2: Daylight life—she's a music teacher / paid companion moving on society's edges. She meets Ashworth socially. Tension: does he recognize her? He seems to. He's odd: unfashionable, observant, rumored to be cold. - Ch 3: He confronts her privately—but instead of exposing her, he asks for her help: something was stolen *from him* (or his late mother's letters are held by the same villain who ruined her father). Alliance formed. - Ch 4: Planning and a shared job; growing intimacy; backstory exchanged on a rooftop. Banter, competence kink, hands and ropes and moonlight. - Ch 5: The job—entering the villain's manor through, naturally, the upper windows. Complication: they find evidence implicating someone unexpected, or are nearly caught; forced proximity (hiding in a wardrobe—classic). - Ch 6: Fallout—villain suspects, threatens exposure; or Julian's reputation/engagement complicates things; a rift—she believes he used her, or he learns she lied about something. - Ch 7: Crisis—she's caught or must do one final climb alone; he comes for her—through the upper window, role reversal (the lord learns *her* art). The villain confronted/defeated via the documents. - Ch 8: Resolution—restitution to the swindled families, scandal weathered or sidestepped, proposal that honors her independence; final image of a window left unlatched for her—she may be a lady now but she'll always prefer the window. **Names/details:** Villain: Sir Marcus Blackwood / Mr. Pemberton, a financier. Heroine: Rosalind "Rose" Vane? Eleanor Quill? Setting: 1870s London + a country house. Motifs: ivy, latches, gloves, the moon, her father's clock or her mother's brooch as the original stolen item. **Other beats to consider:** a loyal accomplice (fence, housekeeper sister), a rival thief, a Bow Street/Scotland Yard inspector circling. Maybe keep cast tight for 8k words. **Alternative: idea 5 (two thieves)** is charming and fast, but romance between equals may lack the Victorian class-tension engine. **Idea 2** is the most traditionally "Victorian Romance" but the climber-POV would be the suitor; could gender-flip: a woman climbing to her beloved? Risky to sustain 8 chapters without a plot engine beyond secrecy. # Reflection What fits the prompt best: the line implies *repeated practice*—"one quickly learns"—so the narrator should be a habitual climber, which favors the thief/Robin Hood concept over the one-time lover. It also implies a confident, ironic first-person voice, which should be the narrative voice throughout: dry, precise, a little rueful. That voice will carry the whole piece and make even planning chapters entertaining. Idea 6 works best because it fuses genre requirements: Victorian setting and texture, a genuine romance arc with class friction, and a plot engine (the swindler, the retrieval jobs) that gives each ~1000-word chapter a clear job. The lover-up-the-ivy idea is too thin for eight chapters; the dual-thieves caper is fun but tonally slighter and less "Victorian Romance"; the dispossessed widow is solid but more sensation-novel than romance. Concerns to manage: - **Pacing:** 8 × 1000 words is tight. Each chapter needs one scene, maybe two. The plan above risks overcrowding ch 5–7. Solution: keep the cast minimal (heroine, hero, villain, one confidante), and let the heist and discovery share a chapter, with fallout compressed. - **The hero's agency:** He shouldn't merely be charmed; giving him his own stake (the villain holds something over him—his late brother's debts, his mother's letters) makes the alliance plausible and gives the romance reciprocity. His learning to climb in ch 7 is a satisfying symbolic reversal of the prompt. - **The rift in ch 6:** must not be a contrived misunderstanding. Better: a genuine moral collision—he wants to settle quietly to protect his family name; she wants public ruin for the villain who destroyed hers. That's a real values conflict resolvable through character growth, not a cleared-up mishearing. - **Romance beats:** need steady escalation—ch 1 glimpse, ch 2 charged drawing-room meeting, ch 3 confrontation/alliance, ch 4 intimacy of shared craft, ch 5 forced proximity, ch 6 rupture, ch 7 rescue/reversal and declaration, ch 8 union on her terms. That maps cleanly onto eight chapters. - **Voice consistency:** open the story with the prompt line itself; bookend with a window image in ch 8 (a husband who leaves the casement unlatched). The aphoristic style can head each chapter lightly without becoming a gimmick. - **Victorian authenticity:** period texture (ivy, sash weights, calling cards, the Season, entailment, scandal mechanics) should flavor rather than clog; the heroine's plausible cover identity (music teacher to good families—gives her access to floor plans, a nice practical detail) ties her two lives together neatly. I'm confident the Robin Hood thief + wounded lord alliance, told in her dry first-person voice, is the strongest direction: it honors the prompt literally and thematically, sustains eight distinct chapters, and delivers a genuine Victorian romance rather than just a caper with kissing.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I will write a Victorian romance narrated in first person by Rosalind Vane, a gentlewoman turned cat burglar who retrieves heirlooms swindled from ruined families—her own father among the victims of the financier Sir Marcus Pemberton. The prompt line opens the story verbatim and establishes her voice: dry, precise, ironic, competent. That voice is the piece's spine; every chapter should sound like a woman who has measured drainpipes for a living and suffers no illusions. **Plot:** Rosalind is glimpsed mid-burglary by Julian, Lord Ashworth, who—rather than exposing her—seeks her out and proposes an alliance: Pemberton holds his late brother's forged notes of hand, instruments of quiet blackmail. Their joint burglary of Pemberton's manor yields more than expected, intimacy included. The rupture is a genuine moral collision, not a misunderstanding: Julian wants the matter buried to protect his family's name; Rosalind wants Pemberton publicly ruined. She acts alone, is caught, and Julian comes for her—through the upper window, the symbolic reversal that resolves both plot and romance. The ending gives restitution, Pemberton's downfall managed on terms both can live with, and a marriage that preserves her independence: a husband who leaves the casement unlatched. **Romance arc:** glimpse → charged drawing-room recognition → confrontation/alliance → intimacy of shared craft → forced proximity → values rupture → rescue and declaration → union on her terms. Escalation must be steady and earned; the love story is between two competent, wounded people who respect each other's skill. **Cast kept minimal:** Rosalind; Julian; Pemberton (villain, seen sparingly but menacing); Mrs. Bell, Rosalind's landlady-fence-confidante (light comic warmth, sounding board). No inspectors, rival thieves, or subplots. **Style choices:** Each chapter opens with a brief aphorism in the spirit of the prompt line ("One learns..."), used lightly, never laboured. Period texture—sash weights, ivy, calling cards, the Season, music-teaching as cover that supplies floor plans—deployed for flavour, not clutter. One scene per chapter, two at most, given the 1000-word constraint. Sensory specificity in the climbing scenes; restraint and subtext in the romantic ones. **To avoid:** contrived misunderstandings; melodramatic villainy speeches; overcrowded chapters; modern idiom; explicit content (Victorian restraint—charged glances, gloved hands, a single kiss landed late and well); making Julian either a rescuer-saviour or a passive admirer—he must have his own stake, his own wound, and in the end he must learn *her* art rather than lift her out of it. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Art of Entry.** Opens with the prompt line. Rosalind narrates her craft and, briskly, her history: father ruined by Pemberton's investment scheme, dead within the year; she now steals back swindled heirlooms for other victims, working as a music teacher by day (lessons supply floor plans). Tonight's job: retrieving a widow's miniature from a Mayfair manor—Ashworth House, whose new owner bought it at Pemberton's auctions. She secures the miniature but is interrupted; Julian, sleepless in the dark study, sees her clearly at the window. A long, strange beat—he does not call out. She drops a glove in the ivy and is gone, shaken less by the risk than by his silence. **Chapter 2 — The Drawing Room.** "One learns that daylight is the more dangerous element." A week later, Rosalind teaches the Fairfax daughters; Lady Fairfax's musical afternoon brings unexpected guests—including Lord Ashworth, rumoured cold, recently bereaved of his elder brother. He watches Rosalind play; conversation laced with double meanings (climbing roses, the trouble with ivy, things that go missing). As he departs he returns her glove—"I believe you dropped this, Miss Vane"—and requests a private interview. End on her calculating exits. **Chapter 3 — Terms of Alliance.** She comes to the interview armed with three lies and a hatpin; he disarms her by confessing instead. His brother Edward, before his death, fell into Pemberton's hands—notes of hand, some forged, used to bleed the estate quietly; exposure would brand Edward a forger and disgrace their mother. Julian has watched Pemberton's victims, traced the pattern of small recoveries, deduced the burglar. He doesn't want her arrested; he wants her skill. Rosalind, hearing Pemberton's name, reveals more of herself than she intends. Alliance struck—she names her terms; he, surprisingly, accepts all of them. First real warmth: mutual recognition of grief turned to work. **Chapter 4 — Lessons.** "One learns more of a man by how he holds a rope than by how he holds a fork." Preparation: Mrs. Bell's lodgings, maps of Pemberton's country house (Rosalind once taught there—she knows the nursery floor). She drills Julian in the basics—he insists on going; the strongbox is behind doors only he can identify by Edward's description. Rooftop practice scene at night: banter, his unexpected aptitude and her grudging approval, exchanged histories under the chimneys. The intimacy of shared craft; a near-fall, her hand on his wrist, the moment held and deliberately released. Mrs. Bell's knowing commentary brackets the chapter. **Chapter 5 — The Upper Windows of Holloway Court.** The job, during Pemberton's autumn house party (cover: noise, guests, confusion). Entry through the nursery window; tension and texture. They find Edward's notes—and more: a ledger documenting dozens of swindles, forgeries, names of ruined families including Vane. Footsteps; the classic wardrobe concealment—forced proximity, breath and heartbeat, Pemberton himself in the room speaking with his man of business about "tidying up" a creditor. They escape with notes and ledger. On the dark lawn, adrenaline and triumph—the almost-kiss, interrupted by an alarm raised behind them. **Chapter 6 — The Rupture.** "One learns that the longest drop is from another person's good opinion." Safe in London, the collision: Julian wants to burn Edward's notes and lock the ledger away—use it as a silent leash on Pemberton, protecting every named family from public shame, his mother above all. Rosalind wants the ledger given to the press and the courts—her father died unvindicated; quiet mercy for the guilty looks, from below, exactly like the conspiracies of gentlemen. Real argument, both partly right, neither cruel. She leaves with nothing; he keeps the ledger. Mrs. Bell counsels patience; Rosalind instead plans one last climb—into Ashworth House, to steal the ledger from the man she has begun to love. **Chapter 7 — Reversal.** She enters Ashworth House by the upper window—and finds the study empty, the ledger gone, and Pemberton's men waiting: Pemberton, tipped by his man of business who traced the burglary, has moved first, snatching her as leverage to force the ledger's return. Held at Pemberton's town house, top-floor room, ironically secure. Julian, finding her gone and Pemberton's note arrived, refuses the trade as offered—and comes for her by the roof, clumsy, determined, through the upper window: "You said one learns quickly." Escape together; Pemberton confronted not with a trade but with the truth that Julian has already lodged copies with solicitors and one incorruptible editor—Rosalind's way, chosen freely. Declaration made plainly on a rooftop, where they are both most honest. **Chapter 8 — The Open Casement.** "One learns, eventually, the art of entering by the door." Aftermath, briskly: Pemberton ruined—flight abroad, the ledger's victims quietly made whole where possible; Edward's notes burned, his name shielded by the scandal's larger noise; Lady Ashworth's grief eased. Restitution scenes in miniature—the widow's miniature returned with interest. Julian's proposal, made without rescue or condescension: she keeps her work (now lawful—recoveries through solicitors, mostly), her name on the settlements, her terms accepted as before. Final image: Ashworth House at night, a married woman climbing the ivy purely for the pleasure of it, her husband holding the casement open from within. Closing line returns to the art of entry: doors are well enough, but some of us were made for windows.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This A human writer given "One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows" would probably fall in love with the *voice* first and the plot second. That line has a personality — wry, slightly weary, professionally proud — and a human would chase the texture of that personality: what she complains about (ivy is overrated; lead guttering is treacherous; the real enemy is dogs), what she notices that nobody else does, what she lies to herself about. The plot would emerge from the voice rather than the voice being draped over a pre-built plot. A human would also tolerate mess. They'd let some scenes run long and others get dispatched in a sentence. They'd let the romance be awkward and unflattering in places — a fight where the heroine is actually wrong, a hero who is irritating before he's appealing. They'd resist symmetry. They'd cut things that a structural outline says are necessary because the page says otherwise. And crucially: a human writing in a Victorian first-person voice would commit to its *constraints* — what a woman of that era cannot say directly, what the narrator withholds from the reader out of pride or shame. The gaps and evasions would do the emotional work. # Telltale LLM Habits, Generally 1. **Safety.** Conflict gets sanded down. Characters are reasonable; arguments are balanced debate-club exchanges where "both are partly right"; nobody is petty, cruel, wrong, or unlikable for long. 2. **The bow.** Every thread resolved, every wound healed, justice distributed, everyone improved. Endings that read like an audit. 3. **Symmetry addiction.** Setups paid off with mechanical neatness; motifs returned exactly once at exactly the expected moment; chapter structures that rhyme too perfectly. 4. **Over-engineered cleverness.** Aphorisms, callbacks, and "satisfying reversals" prioritized over the feeling of lived experience. Writing that performs intelligence rather than embodying a person. 5. **Even pacing.** Every chapter the same shape and weight: scene, beat, button. No chapter that lingers or sprints. Word budgets met like invoices. 6. **Explained subtext.** The narrator tells you what the charged silence meant. Emotions named ("I felt a strange warmth") instead of dramatized or — better — denied. 7. **Frictionless competence.** Plans work. Skills transfer. The amateur learns to climb in one chapter. Failure, when it occurs, is plot-mandated rather than humiliating. 8. **Generic period furniture.** "Calling cards, the Season, ivy" — Victorian wallpaper rather than specific, researched-feeling oddity. Real period texture is weird and particular (the smell of gas jets, the etiquette of mourning crepe, the price of a cab). 9. **Stock characters in the margins.** The warm, knowing landlady. The cold-but-secretly-tender lord. The villain who exists to be defeated. 10. **Prose habits:** triplet constructions, "not X but Y" formations, paragraphs ending on a poised little epigram, em-dash flourishes, every metaphor drawn from the story's own theme (everything compared to climbing, locks, windows). # Deep Dive: Where the Plan Falls Into These Traps **1. The aphorism-per-chapter device is the biggest red flag.** "One learns that daylight is the more dangerous element," "One learns more of a man by how he holds a rope..." — this is exactly the kind of patterned cleverness an LLM loves: a gimmick that's charming once (in the prompt) and becomes a tic by chapter four. A human might use it twice, irregularly, or let the narrator visibly *fail* to produce one ("There is no aphorism for this"). Eight epigraph-flavored openers will make the story feel like a structure wearing a story. **2. The ending is a full gift-wrap.** Pemberton ruined *and* fled abroad, victims made whole, Edward's name shielded, the mother's grief eased, restitution "in miniature," a proposal on her terms, lawful employment secured, and a closing image of recreational ivy-climbing with a husband holding the casement. That's six bows on one box. Real Victorian endings — even happy ones — leave residue: money that can't be recovered, a mother who never learns the truth and resents the wrong person, a heroine whose marriage costs her something real (her anonymity, her work's edge, her old self). The "climbing the ivy for pleasure" final image is cute in the way LLM endings are cute: thematically tidy, emotionally weightless. A wife climbing her own ivy is a domesticated burglar — arguably the story's *tragedy* dressed as its triumph, and the plan doesn't notice. **3. The moral rupture is suspiciously well-balanced.** "Real argument, both partly right, neither cruel" — that's the LLM safety instinct codified into the outline. Humans fight ugly. Rosalind, in that argument, should say something genuinely below the belt about Edward (a dead man, a forger, beloved); Julian should pull rank in a way that reminds her exactly what class he is — "you forget yourself," or worse, an offer of *money*. The rupture lands only if each says something they can't fully take back. Also: the plan resolves the conflict by Julian simply... adopting her position offstage ("lodged copies with one incorruptible editor — Rosalind's way, chosen freely"). That's capitulation, not synthesis. He converts to her worldview as a romantic gesture, which is the bow again. What does *she* concede? Where's her growth? As planned, she's right about everything from chapter one, which makes her static. **4. Julian learning to climb is too frictionless and too symbolic.** One rooftop lesson in chapter 4, and by chapter 7 he's coming through Pemberton's upper window. The plan even flags this as "the symbolic reversal" — which is the problem: it's a *symbol* first and an event second. A human version: he climbs badly. He gets stuck. He arrives bleeding, having torn his coat and nearly died, and the rescue half-fails and they have to improvise. Or subvert it entirely — he comes through the *door*, bluffing as a gentleman caller, because that's *his* native art, and the reversal is that each uses their own skill rather than him borrowing hers. The planned version exists because it's neat, not because it's true. **5. The wardrobe scene.** The plan literally calls it "the classic wardrobe concealment." LLMs reach for the inherited beat. Either own the cliché with self-awareness in the narration, or find a less furnished hiding place — under a child's bed in the nursery they entered through, which would be more humiliating, more tense, and connect to texture already established. **6. Pemberton is a cardboard financier.** "Seen sparingly but menacing" usually means "seen sparingly and generic." He swindles, blackmails, keeps a convenient self-incriminating ledger (a very LLM plot device — villains who document their crimes for the heroine to find), and takes a hostage. A human would give him one scene of real charm or real grievance — the self-made man's contempt for the gentry who sneered at him, which would uncomfortably echo Rosalind's own class resentment. The ledger needs to be less of a McGuffin: maybe what they find is partial, deniable, *not enough* — which would put real pressure on the chapter 6 argument (publishing insufficient evidence is reckless; sitting on it is cowardly). **7. Mrs. Bell is the stock warm landlady.** "Knowing commentary brackets the chapter," "counsels patience." She's a function, not a person. Give her a stake (she fences the goods — she profits from Rosalind's trade and might resist the heroine going respectable), or an edge (she thinks the Robin Hood framing is a vanity and says so), or cut her down to a few lines and spend the words elsewhere. **8. Rosalind's nobility is too clean.** She steals only to restore the swindled. This is the LLM laundering its protagonist. A human would let her keep something, sometimes — or admit she enjoys the work for itself, that the righteous mission is partly an alibi for the fact that she's good at this and it's the only time she feels alive. The prompt's voice is a professional's voice, not a saint's. Her self-deception (telling the reader she does it for the victims while the prose betrays her pleasure) is far richer than the plan's tidy Robin Hood. **9. Chapter shapes are uniform.** Eight chapters, each "one scene, a beat, a button," each ~1000 words, each opening with its epigram. The plan even prescribes "Mrs. Bell's knowing commentary brackets the chapter" — bracketing! Symmetry within symmetry. At least one chapter should break form: chapter 6's argument could be nearly all dialogue, raw; chapter 1 could spend "too long" on craft detail because that's who she is; chapter 8 could be very short. 10. **Thematic metaphor monoculture.** The plan's imagery is all windows, climbing, latches, ropes, drops ("the longest drop is from another person's good opinion"). An LLM will generate every figure of speech from the story's own theme. A human narrator's mind wanders — her metaphors should come from music (her cover trade, currently unused as anything but a plot key!), from her father's world, from housekeeping, from scripture half-remembered. The music-teacher identity is pure plot machinery in the plan; it should color her perception (she hears houses; a creaking stair is a wrong note; Julian speaks in a key she can't place). 11. **Explained romance.** "Mutual recognition of grief turned to work," "the moment held and deliberately released" — these are stage directions for feelings. The risk in execution is the narrator announcing her emotional state at each beat. Better: she's an unreliable narrator about exactly one thing — him. Precise about gradients of slate roofs, evasive and clipped whenever Julian enters the paragraph. The reader sees what she won't say. # Brainstorming: Making It More Human - **Let her be wrong.** The chapter 6 fight: Rosalind's insistence on public ruin should have a cost the story honors — publishing names the victims too, including families who'd rather starve than be pitied. Maybe a victim *begs* her not to publish. Her conviction is partly vengeance wearing justice's clothes, and Julian sees it before she does. Then the resolution can be a real synthesis (selective exposure? Pemberton destroyed financially but the victims unnamed?) that costs both of them. - **Kill a bow or two.** Some money is never recovered. Her father stays unvindicated in any public sense — and she has to live with that. Lady Ashworth never knows what Julian did and dies thinking him cold. Pick one or two permanent losses. - **Reconsider the final image.** Options: the open casement, no climb shown; or she climbs once, finds it diminished now that it's permitted, and the last line admits it; or end *before* the wedding entirely — the proposal made through the open window, answer implied not given. - **Roughen the rescue.** Julian's climb should nearly kill him or fail outright — she ends up rescuing him mid-rescue, which is both funnier and truer. Or he enters by the front door with brass-faced gentlemanly fraud while she goes out the window — two arts, one house. - **Use the aphorism device against itself.** Two or three only; let chapter 6 or 7 open with the narrator refusing one. The reader feels the form break exactly when she does. - **Give the voice flaws.** Pride that curdles into snobbery-in-reverse; pleasure in the work she won't own; a tendency to inventory rooms even at emotional moments (deflection as character). - **Specificity over wallpaper.** One or two researched-feeling details per chapter — the weight of a sash window's counterbalance, the particular smell of a gaslit landing, the awkwardness of climbing in 1870s skirts (does she wear breeches? whose? that's a story detail with class and gender voltage). - **Make the evidence imperfect.** The ledger is in a private shorthand, or incomplete, or implicates a victim. Forces harder choices everywhere downstream. - **Sharpen the minor cast.** Mrs. Bell as fence with a profit motive and an opinion that Rosalind's crusade is bad business. Pemberton given one scene of plausible self-justification that rhymes uncomfortably with Rosalind's own grievances. - **Vary the music.** One dialogue-heavy chapter, one almost silent one (the burglary), one that compresses weeks in a paragraph. Let the word counts breathe within the constraint rather than each chapter doing identical work. The plan's bones are sound — alliance, heist, rupture, reversal is a good skeleton. The danger is entirely in the connective tissue: too balanced, too symbolic, too resolved, too in love with its own motif. The fixes are mostly subtractions and roughenings, plus one structural rethink (what the ending costs, and who concedes what).
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention A Victorian romance narrated by Rosalind Vane: music teacher by day, burglar by night, retrieving heirlooms swindled from ruined families by the financier Sir Marcus Pemberton — her own dead father among his victims. The skeleton stands from the earlier plan (glimpse → alliance → heist → rupture → reversal → union), but the connective tissue changes throughout. **Voice first.** The story opens with the prompt line and lives or dies on her voice: dry, precise, professionally proud — and unreliable about exactly one subject, which is Julian. She inventories rooms when she's frightened, deflects feeling into craft detail, and never names an emotion she can dodge. Her metaphors come from music as often as climbing (she *hears* houses; a wrong stair is a wrong note), so the imagery doesn't collapse into windows-and-ropes monoculture. The "one learns..." aphorism device appears exactly three times: chapter 1 (the prompt), once mid-story, and once *broken* — at the lowest point she admits there is no aphorism for this. The form fractures when she does. **Dirtier protagonist.** Rosalind is not a saint. She tells the reader she steals for the victims; the prose betrays that she steals because she's good at it and it's the only time she feels alive. She keeps a percentage — Mrs. Bell, her landlady and fence, insists on it, and Rosalind doesn't argue hard. Mrs. Bell has a profit motive and an opinion: the Robin Hood crusade is vanity and bad business. **An ugly rupture, a real concession.** The chapter 6 fight is not balanced debate. The evidence they steal is *imperfect* — a ledger in private shorthand that names victims as well as crimes. Julian wants it buried to protect his forger brother's name and his mother; Rosalind wants it published. Both say unforgivable things: she calls Edward what he was; he offers her money. And Rosalind is partly *wrong* — publication would name and shame the victims too, and one of them begs her not to. The resolution is a costly synthesis (Pemberton ruined financially and privately, the victims unnamed, her father never publicly vindicated), with concessions on both sides — not Julian converting to her worldview offstage. **No symbolic climbing rescue.** Julian does not learn her art in one lesson. In the climax each uses their *own* craft: he enters Pemberton's house by the front door, bluffing with gentlemanly fraud, while she gets herself half out the window — the rescue half-fails, is improvised, slightly ridiculous, and she finishes saving them both. The reversal of the prompt is earned differently: in the final chapter *he* is the one at the window. **Bows withheld.** Not all money is recovered. Lady Ashworth never learns what her sons did or cost. Pemberton is broken, not punished in court. The ending is a proposal made through an open upper window, answer implied rather than narrated — no wedding, no recreational ivy. **Practical constraints:** ~1000 words per chapter means one scene each, two at most; chapters vary in texture (ch. 5 nearly silent, ch. 6 nearly all dialogue, ch. 8 short and quick). Period detail is specific and sparse — breeches under a cloak, sash-weights, gas smell, mourning crepe — never wallpaper. Restraint in the romance: one kiss, late, earned. Avoid: triplet rhythms, paragraph-ending epigrams every page, explained subtext, villain monologues, neat symmetry. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — The Art of Entry (~1000 words).** Opens with the prompt line and a working burglary: Ashworth House, Mayfair, retrieving a widow's miniature bought at Pemberton's auctions. Heavy on craft texture — the borrowed breeches, the treachery of new lead guttering, her habit of listening to a house like a score. Backstory dispatched in one tight paragraph: father ruined by Pemberton, dead within the year; the work began as restitution and she lets the reader believe that's all it is, though her evident pleasure says otherwise. She's interrupted: Julian, sleepless in the dark study, sees her plainly — and says nothing. She leaves a glove in the ivy. The silence unsettles her more than capture would have; she catalogues the room's furniture in her memory all the way home, which is what she does instead of being afraid. **Chapter 2 — Daylight (~1000 words).** Her cover life, briskly drawn: teaching the Fairfax daughters scales, the small humiliations of genteel employment, Mrs. Bell at home taking her cut of the widow's fee and pronouncing the crusade "sentiment, dear, and sentiment don't pay rent." Then Lady Fairfax's musical afternoon: Lord Ashworth among the guests — recently bereaved of his brother Edward, rumoured cold, watching Rosalind play with disconcerting attention. Conversation in front of others, all double meaning; the narration goes notably clipped whenever he's in the paragraph (the unreliability begins). At his departure he returns her glove without a word of accusation and requests an interview. She spends the last page deciding between flight and curiosity, and lying to herself about which one wins. **Chapter 3 — Terms (~1000 words).** The interview. She arrives with three prepared lies and a hatpin; he wrong-foots her by confessing first: Edward forged notes of hand that Pemberton now uses to bleed the estate — exposure would brand a dead man a forger and break their mother, who lives in deep mourning two floors above. He has traced the pattern of small recoveries among Pemberton's victims and deduced her months ago. He wants her skill, not her arrest. At Pemberton's name she gives away more than she means to — the only crack in her composure all chapter, and she resents it. Terms negotiated like a contract (she insists on payment; nobility is for people who can afford it). The chapter's warmth is oblique: two people who recognise grief converted into work, neither saying so. **Chapter 4 — Reconnaissance (~1000 words).** Compressed preparation — weeks in paragraphs. Rosalind once taught at Holloway Court, Pemberton's country house; she knows the nursery floor and the governess's stair. Julian insists on coming: only he can identify Edward's hand among the papers. One night of practical instruction on Mrs. Bell's roof, where he proves game, careful, and *not* a natural — he gets stuck at the parapet and she has to talk him back, which he takes with unexpected grace, and that grace does more damage to her composure than charm would. Exchanged history under the chimneys, both editing themselves. Mrs. Bell's verdict on the partnership: "Gentlemen are a luxury, and you've never had luxuries." The almost-moment happens and Rosalind narrates the brickwork instead. **Chapter 5 — Holloway Court (~1000 words).** The burglary, during Pemberton's house party — the quietest chapter, short sentences, present-tense attention. In through the nursery window; the house "played" from memory. They find Edward's notes — and a ledger, but it's in Pemberton's private shorthand, partial, and where legible it names victims alongside crimes: the Vane entry included, with a notation that suggests her father was not entirely a dupe. Footsteps: they hide *under the nursery bed*, the indignity noted, Pemberton's voice above discussing the "tidying up" of a stubborn creditor — menace conveyed in commercial euphemism, no monologue. Out across the leads. On the dark lawn the adrenaline nearly becomes a kiss; the dog finds them first. They run. She is laughing, and hates that he heard it. **Chapter 6 — What It's Worth (~1000 words).** Nearly all dialogue, the gloves off. Julian: burn Edward's notes, lock the ledger away, use it as a silent leash — every family named in it is protected by silence, his mother above all. Rosalind: publish and ruin him; quiet mercy among gentlemen is exactly how her father died unvindicated. It turns ugly: she calls Edward a forger who deserves his disgrace; Julian, cornered, offers her *money* to be sensible — the one insult she cannot forgive, and he knows it as he says it. She walks out. Then the chapter's true blow: she visits the widow of chapter 1 to recruit an ally for publication, and the widow begs her not to — print the ledger and you print our shame with it. Rosalind, for the first time, is not sure she's the just one. No aphorism opens this chapter; she notes there isn't one. **Chapter 7 — The Front Door (~1000 words).** Rosalind decides to settle it alone — steal the ledger back from Julian, force the issue — and finds Ashworth's study already ransacked: Pemberton's man traced the Holloway burglary, and Pemberton has taken the ledger *and* left word he knows the woman's name. She goes to Pemberton's town house to take it back and is caught — held in a top-floor room, ironically secure. Julian arrives not by the roof but by the front door, every inch the bored aristocrat, bluffing a negotiation while she works the window from inside; the rescue half-fails (the sash is screwed shut; she breaks it; he must catch her on the leads and nearly takes them both off the roof; she saves *him*). The real weapon: Julian has already deposited attested copies of the legible pages with the family solicitors — exposure of Pemberton's *crimes* without the victims' names, the synthesis neither would have chosen alone, and he tells her plainly it was her argument that forced him there, and the widow's that tempered it. On the leads, breathless and ridiculous, the kiss — narrated, for once, without inventory. **Chapter 8 — The Open Window (~700–800 words, deliberately short).** Aftermath without bows: Pemberton broken by his creditors and quietly gone abroad — no trial, no headline with the name Vane in it. Some money recovered, much not; the widow's miniature stays returned; her father stays publicly unvindicated, and Rosalind tells the reader, flatly, that she has decided to live with it, which is not the same as peace. Edward's notes burned in Julian's grate; Lady Ashworth never knows, and never warms. Mrs. Bell takes her final percentage and her leave of the crusade. Last scene: Rosalind in her lodgings at night, a noise at the casement — Julian, having climbed badly and torn his coat, at her upper window, because some questions should be asked on equal terms and this is the only door she's ever respected. The proposal is made through the open window. Her answer is not narrated. The closing lines return to the prompt: one quickly learns the art of entering through the upper windows — and more slowly, the art of leaving one unlatched.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Rosalind Vane **Background.** Born 1845, the only child of Charles Vane, a Norwich solicitor of modest practice and immoderate optimism. Her mother died when she was nine; her father raised her on Beethoven, double-entry bookkeeping, and the conviction that the world rewards honest men — a theory he tested fatally in 1868 by placing his savings, his clients' trust funds, and his good name into Sir Marcus Pemberton's Anglo-Peruvian Guano Consolidated.
