Dialogue-led, and she makes him work: no speech from him lands; what lands is him admitting the interview was partly vanity and he can't fully tell which part. She reads one margin note aloud; her voice does something she didn't authorize; she stops reading. The reconciliation is conditional and practical, on her terms — filming wraps in three weeks; he can come back when there's no story in it and prove the quiet is what he wanted; meanwhile "there's a delivery Thursday and you owe me for the hardback you never read." He laughs — the first unperformed laugh of the book. Last image small: the sign still says Closed, the dog asleep on his feet again, rain getting its second and final good sentence.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Carys Pritchard **Background.** Forty next March, though she tells people thirty-eight because the lie amuses her more than the truth. Born in Llanmorwen, above the shop — Pritchard's Books has been there since her grandfather opened it in 1962, selling chapel histories and Westerns to a town that read more then. She got out, briefly: a Welsh and English degree at Aberystwyth, then six years in Cardiff doing editorial work for an educational publisher, where she was good at her job and slowly suffocating. The official story is that she came home in 2014 when her father's memory started going. The truer story, which she's never fully said aloud, is that she'd already begun dismantling her Cardiff life before the diagnosis — turned down a promotion, let a good relationship starve through neglect — and her father's illness arrived almost as a relief, a respectable reason to do what she was doing anyway. Gareth, the fiancé, didn't leave her for London; he asked her to come and she said the shop needed her, which they both knew was only half of it. He's married now. She found out from Facebook before she deleted Facebook. Her father, Emrys, has been in Hafan y Môr care home outside Aberystwyth for three years. He doesn't know her most Tuesdays. She goes every Tuesday anyway and is ashamed of how much she resents the drive, the dayroom smell, the cheerful staff, the whole performance of a visit he won't keep. She's never told anyone that some Tuesdays she sits in the car park for ten minutes working up to it. The shop loses money gently, like a slow leak. She's three months behind with the supplier, juggles which bill screams loudest, and has twice refused — out of pure stubbornness — an offer from a Bristol couple who want the building for a "coastal lifestyle boutique." She knows the maths. She refuses to do the maths. **Personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws.** Dry, watchful, economical with warmth — not because she lacks it but because she rations it like everything else. Her wit is real but unreliable; under genuine pressure she goes blunt and graceless rather than sharp, and composes the devastating line afterwards, lying awake, furious at her brain's timing. She is rude when flustered — this is the key mechanism of the whole story — and her rudeness escalates in direct proportion to how much she's affected. Polite Carys is indifferent Carys. Specifics: she alphabetizes by author but shelves R.S. Thomas face-out, always, a private editorial position. She licks her thumb to count banknotes, a habit from her grandfather she'd deny having. She talks to the dog in Welsh and to customers in English, and the dog gets the better conversation. She keeps the shop's accounts in a school exercise book because the laptop's spreadsheet feels like admitting it's a business and not a vocation. She reads three books at once and finishes none of them some months. She has a single expensive habit — good whisky, one bottle at a time, Penderyn, kept in the back room behind the Oxford Companion to English Literature, which nobody ever asks for. Flaws, the real ones: she has converted self-protection into a moral position — tells herself her wariness is discernment when it's mostly fear with a better coat on. She over-diagnoses herself and gets it wrong: she believes her problem is that she stayed in Llanmorwen, when her actual problem is that she's never forgiven herself for *wanting* to stay. She mistakes pride for independence, particularly about money. And she punishes people slightly for getting through to her — the warmer she feels, the colder the next thing she says, a reflex she notices about one time in three. **Physicality.** Tallish, angular, dark hair going visibly grey at the temple and left to it. Dresses for the shop's temperature, not for anyone's eye: cardigans with sagging pockets full of pencil stubs and rubber bands, jeans, boots. Reading glasses pushed up into her hair and lost there daily. She moves around the shop with the spatial certainty of someone who's lived in a building forty years — never looks where she's reaching. Stillness is her tell: when she's genuinely rattled she stops moving entirely, goes flat-voiced and over-occupied with whatever's in her hands. Her laugh, when it escapes, is loud and unguarded and embarrasses her. **Motives, allegiances, relationships.** She wants the shop to survive, and underneath that, less admitted: she wants her staying to have *meant* something — to not have been a waste dressed up as duty. Loyal to Ffion with a gruffness that fools nobody, to her father's former self, to the town in the way you're loyal to family you complain about. Friendly-not-close with Delyth at the post office (the town's broadband, gossip-wise). She is not lonely, she'd tell you, and she'd hold eye contact while saying it. **Voice samples.** *"No, you can stand there as long as you like. I'm not paying you any attention, mind, I've a delivery to book in. There's a chair by the poetry if your legs get tired of being famous."* *Internal:* The line came to her at ten past two in the morning, complete, perfectly weighted, the line that would have ended the conversation with him flat on the canvas. She said it aloud to the ceiling. The ceiling was impressed. Twelve hours too late, as per. Her brain kept its best work for an audience of nobody, which, now she thought about it, made two of them. *"I forgave you the moment you told me, Ffion, so you can stop crying into the Penguin Classics, they're foxed enough as it is."* # Dylan Vaughan (Gethin Lewis) **Background.** Forty-four. Born Gethin Lewis in Pontypridd, son of a school secretary and an electrician at the Treforest estate. Drama was an accident — a teacher needing boys for *Under Milk Wood* — that turned into the only place he'd ever felt the specific warmth of a room full of people deciding to like him. RADA on a scholarship, where a director told him, kindly, over a pint, that "Gethin Lewis" sounded like a rugby fixture and the accent would cap him at coal-miner parts. He became Dylan Vaughan over a single summer — chose "Dylan" thinking it kept a thread to home, and has since endured twenty years of Americans telling him how much they love that he's named for the poet, a compliment he accepts with a smile that costs him nothing because nothing about the name is true. The career is real and large: two prestige TV runs, a franchise villain that bought the houses, a film with a director people call *a* director. The breakup currently feeding the tabloids — Marina Kessler, his co-star of three years — was genuinely his fault and not in the way the papers say. He didn't cheat. He did something he finds harder to confess: he was never fully *in* the room with her, always slightly performing the relationship, and she told him so on the way out, in a sentence he has not stopped hearing. The tabloids cast him as a heartbreaker. He half-prefers the libel to the truth. His mother still lives in Ponty. He calls every Sunday, sends money she banks and doesn't spend, and hasn't been home in person for two years because being Gethin for a weekend leaves him disoriented for a fortnight after — like a diver coming up too fast. **Personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws.** Charming as a *practice*, not a pose — it's his native language now, the reflexive offering of whatever the room wants. He genuinely likes people; that's what makes it complicated. The flaw isn't cynicism, it's need: a room that hasn't warmed to him registers as a physical problem, like a draught, and he cannot rest until he's fixed it. He knows the first name of every runner on every set he's worked. He also checks his phone with the compulsive rhythm of a smoker — not vanity exactly, more like checking a wound. Specifics: he reads obituaries of actors and measures the column inches. He over-tips catastrophically, which he tells himself is generosity and is partly insurance. When a joke dies he repeats the last word of it quietly, a verbal limp he's unaware of. He's a genuinely good reader — books, not just scripts — but has learned to hide the seriousness of it because "surprisingly well-read" plays better than "actually clever." Under stress, or three drinks in, the Pontypridd vowels surface and he corrects them mid-word, a tiny audible flinch. He cannot bear to leave a phone call on a bad note; his publicist exploits this weekly. The deep flaw: he cannot distinguish, even internally, between performing a feeling and having one — the instrument and the player fused decades ago. His self-awareness is real but always *partial and slightly misaimed*: he'll cop to vanity (manageable, even charming to admit) to avoid copping to fear. When he's hurt his first instinct is to make the person laugh, which is also his first instinct when *they're* hurt, which has ended at least one relationship. **Physicality.** Handsome in the durable, slightly weathered way that survives forty — good bones, expensive teeth, grey allowed at the temples because his people decided it tested well. Moves like someone permanently aware of his angles, until he's rattled, when the grace drops and he becomes briefly, endearingly clumsy — knocks things off counters, sits down wrong. His resting expression is ten percent performance even alone. The genuine smile, the rare one, arrives lopsided and makes him look younger and less finished, like a costume slipping. **Motives, allegiances, relationships.** Surface motive: ride out the Marina story, finish the shoot, protect the brand. Real motive, unadmitted until the story forces it: he is exhausted in a way sleep doesn't touch, and Carys's shop is the first room in years that didn't need anything from him — her refusal to applaud is the first silence he's been able to sit in. Allegiances: his mother, guiltily; Marina, complicatedly — he wants her forgiveness too, of course he does, he wants everyone's; his publicist Saskia, whom he obeys and resents like weather; the crew-level people on every job, who all love him, which proves nothing and he's starting to know it. **Voice samples.** *"Okay, in my defence — and I want it noted I'm aware of how this sounds — most people, when I do the thing with the, the recognising-me thing, most people are at least civil about it. You're looking at me like I've trodden something into your carpet."* *Internal:* She hadn't laughed. Fine. People didn't laugh sometimes — no. People laughed. People always laughed, that was the whole arrangement, that was the deal he'd signed in a school hall in 1994, and this woman was sat there licking her thumb and counting tens like he was the draught from the door. He should leave. He was absolutely going to leave. He picked up another book he didn't want. *"Gethin Lewis. No — say it like it's two words, not a sneeze. There we are. Him I haven't played in a while. The reviews were mixed."* # Ffion Hughes **Background.** Seventeen, lower sixth at the comprehensive in the next town, works in the shop Saturdays and two afternoons. Took the job because her mam knows Carys; stayed because she genuinely loves it, though she'd phrase it as "it's fine, it's quiet, I can do my coursework." Wants to do English at Cardiff and hasn't told Carys that Carys is the reason. Documents her entire life in a private-ish Instagram and a public TikTok with forty-three followers, where she reviews books in fifteen-second bursts. **Personality, flaws, function.** Sunny, unguarded, constitutionally incapable of playing it cool — the precise inverse of her employer, which is why they work. Talks in unfinished sentences when excited. Knows every detail of the Dylan Vaughan–Marina Kessler coverage and recites it with the moral seriousness of a scholar, no malice in her anywhere. Her flaw is innocence of consequence: the tagged selfie — *shop name, location pin, "NO ONE will believe who came in today"* — goes up in chapter 2 with forty-three followers and detonates in chapter 6 when the tabloids trawl. Her guilt when it lands is enormous and genuine, and Carys's rough instant forgiveness of her is the story quietly rehearsing its ending. **Physicality.** Small, quick, school lanyard still on half the time, paints tiny flowers on her nails during slow shifts. Physically incapable of not mouthing along to whatever's in her earbuds. **Voice samples.** *"Carys. Carys. Do you know who that — okay you clearly do know and you're being weird about it, but that is — he was in the thing, the one with the — I've literally seen everything he's ever — can I have my break early."* *"I didn't think! Forty-three followers, Carys, one of them's my nan!"* # Saskia Brand (voice on the phone) **Background & function.** Dylan's publicist of eleven years, London, late forties, the best in the business at a job that consists of deciding which truths are affordable. She is not a villain; she is a professional doing exactly what Dylan has paid her to do for a decade — protect the asset — and the chapter 6 statement that erases Carys is competent, kind by industry standards, and approved by him on a phone call he barely attended to. That's the point: the machine is just his flaw with a staff. **Personality.** Brisk, fond of Dylan in the way of someone who's seen all of it, immune to the charm and therefore one of the few people he's honest-ish with. Speaks in options and optics. Her chapter 7 function: she offers the staged Marina détente without apology, takes his refusal with one beat of silence that's almost respect, and tells him the truth on her way out of the call. **Voice samples.** *"Two options, my love. One: you and Marina, photographed having coffee, civilised, everyone exhales, dead by Friday. Two: you keep sounding like this and I find out what's actually going on, which we both know I will."* *"You want to give an interview as Gethin Lewis. Right. And is this honesty, or is it the most interesting part you've been offered all year? — No, don't answer fast. That's the tell, Dylan. Answering fast."* # Emrys Pritchard & Dylan-the-dog (brief) **Emrys.** Carys's father, eighty-one, Hafan y Môr care home. A chapel-raised reader who once knew Welsh poetry by the yard; the dementia has left courtesy and rhythm but not names. In chapter 4 he takes to Dylan instantly — laughs, engages, briefly *shines* — because Dylan gives him twenty minutes of full performance, and Emrys, who forgets faces, still recognizes warmth. He calls Carys "the girl from the shop" that day, which she's used to, and it never stops costing her. His function: the scene where Carys can't tell whether what Dylan does is kindness or performance — and begins to suspect the question is badly framed. **Dylan-the-dog.** Elderly border collie cross, came with a house clearance seven years ago, named Dylan because the dead owner's books were all Thomas. Sleeps in the poetry section, dislikes most men, and settles on the actor's feet in chapter 3 — the shop's only review Dylan Vaughan trusts completely, because the dog has no idea who he is.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One — We're Not Really Open The bell went and a man came in fast, shoulder first, like the weather had thrown him. Carys didn't look up. She was on the phone to Howell's, and Howell's were being difficult about the invoice, and the invoice was a conversation she could not afford to lose to a customer who would buy one postcard or nothing. "No — no, I understand that, Gary, but you'll have it by the twentieth. The twentieth. Because that's when the rates come out and I'm not — fine. Fine. Twentieth." She put the phone down. The man was standing in the middle of the shop with his back to the window, very still, in the manner of someone trying to be furniture. Expensive coat, wet through at the shoulders. He was looking at her with an expression she'd describe later, to the ceiling at two in the morning, as *pre-emptively delighted*. "Sorry," he said. "Don't mind me. I'm browsing. Enthusiastically browsing." "We're not really open." "The door was." "The door sticks." She picked up the delivery slip and started checking it against the box on the counter. "Latch wants seeing to." He stayed where he was. Outside, on the seafront, a man with a long lens went past at a half-jog, looked left, looked right, kept going. The man in her shop watched him the whole way along the window, head turning slowly, like a cat with a gull. "Friend of yours?" Carys said. "Hm? No. No, that's — " He turned back and the smile arrived, full beam, a smile that had clearly opened a great many doors and expected to open this one. "You know what, I love this place. Genuinely. The smell alone. You can't buy this smell." "It's damp." "It's *atmosphere*." "It's damp," she said. "There's a dehumidifier on order. Was there something you wanted?" He laughed, a warm easy laugh, and then seemed to notice it hadn't been joined and wound it down. He drifted to the nearest shelf and took out a book without looking at it. He was, she registered without wanting to, very good-looking, in a way that suggested a team. The face was familiar in the manner of a road sign — seen a thousand times, never considered. "So," he said. "Quiet today." "Tuesdays." "Right. Tuesdays." He put the book back upside down. "You, ah — you might have seen my face about, actually. This is going to sound — I'm not saying it to be — but it's possible you recognise me, and I'd rather get that bit out of the way, because otherwise it sits there, doesn't it, like a — " "Forty pounds of cookery books," she said to the delivery slip. "I ordered thirty." He stopped. She felt him stop. She went on counting cookery books. "Sorry — do you not — " He came a step closer to the counter. "Most people, when I do the thing with the, the recognising-me thing — most people are at least civil about it. You're looking at me like I've trodden something into your carpet." "I'm not looking at you at all. I've a delivery to book in. There's a chair by the poetry if your legs get tired." He didn't sit. He hovered, and picked things up, and put them down in the wrong places, and she felt the hovering the way you feel weather coming. The photographer went back along the seafront the other way, slower. Her visitor angled himself behind the rotating postcard stand with a casualness that was almost professional. "I could sign something," he offered, from behind the postcards. "For the shop. People do come in for that sort of thing. A signed photo by the till — I'm only saying, it shifts product. We did it for a bookshop in Notting Hill and the woman said — " "Did she." " — said it doubled her footfall. Doubled." "This is Llanmorwen," Carys said. "If you doubled my footfall I'd have to dust." He laughed again, properly this time, surprised into it, and she was annoyed to find the laugh was better when it was real. She licked her thumb and counted the tens in the float, which she didn't need to do, and was aware of doing it, and did it anyway. "You genuinely don't know who I am," he said. He sounded fascinated. He sounded the way men sound at a locked door. "Dylan Vaughan," she said, flatly, to the float. "You were the villain in the one with the submarines. You did a Hamlet at the Almeida about ten years back. My assistant has a poster of you in a tuxedo holding a gun sideways, which is no way to hold a gun." The silence had a texture to it. When she looked up his face was doing several things, none of which she imagined got much use. "Right," he said. "Right. So when I came in — " "You were standing between me and Gary from Howell's, who matters." "And the whole — " he gestured at himself, the coat, the smile, the general production of him — "all that, you just — " "I'd a delivery slip wanted checking." "Wow." He said it quietly, to the bookshelf, like it was the last word of a joke that had died. "*Slip*." He shook himself, visibly changed gear, came up to the counter with his hands open, peace terms. "Okay. Starting over. There is a gentleman outside with a camera who is being paid an obscene amount to make my week worse, and I would consider it an enormous personal favour if I could stand in your damp shop for fifteen more minutes. And in exchange I will buy books. Actual books. Lots of them." "You don't have to buy anything." "No, I want to. What's good? What do you — sell me something. Sell me the best thing in the shop." She should have given him a Penguin Classic and the door. Instead — and she examined this later, at length, without mercy — she went to the poetry shelf, took down the R.S. Thomas, face-out, the good collected, and put it in his hands. "There," she said. "He'd have hated you. Fiver." He turned it over. "Hated me why?" "Read it and find out." He bought it, and a hardback on coastal birds he plainly didn't want, and he paid with a twenty and waved off the change with a carelessness that went through her like a draught, because she took it. She took it, and rang it in, and hated that he'd been standing there when she'd said *the twentieth* to Gary in that voice. At the door he stopped, rain readying itself behind him. "You never said your name." "No," she agreed. When he'd gone she stood for a while doing nothing, which wasn't like her, and then booked in the rest of the cookery books wrong and had to do them twice.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of opening chapter writing. Let me work through it carefully. **Craft and Execution** The chapter opens with a precise, economical image: "a man came in fast, shoulder first, like the weather had thrown him." This is a good metaphor that earns its place — it establishes both the physical reality (he's fleeing something) and his slightly chaotic energy. It doesn't overreach. The dialogue is the chapter's great strength. The voices are genuinely distinct. Carys's lines are clipped, practical, and withholding in exactly the way the profile demands: "It's damp." "Tuesdays." "I'd a delivery slip wanted checking." These aren't witty comebacks — they're the responses of someone who is genuinely occupied and mildly irritated, which is far more deflating to a charmer than wit would be. Dylan's lines carry the self-aware performance the profile requires: the pre-emptive charm, the "recognising-me thing" speech, the pivot to "starting over." His verbal limp — repeating the last word of a dying joke ("*Slip*") — is deployed exactly as planned and lands perfectly. The chapter follows its plan with impressive fidelity. The invoice call, the photographer outside, the charm offensive landing on stone, the R.S. Thomas purchase, the overpayment, the withheld name — all present and executed. The ending (booking in the cookery books wrong) is the right kind of small, unshowy tell. **Character Consistency** Both characters are entirely consistent with their profiles. Dylan's micro-panic when the charm doesn't land is rendered through behavior rather than statement: "He said it quietly, to the bookshelf, like it was the last word of a joke that had died." His careless wave of the change — and her taking it, and hating that he saw — is the class dynamic the plan specified, handled with restraint. Carys's tell (going still, going flat-voiced, over-occupying herself with whatever's in her hands) is shown through the float-counting. The ending — doing the books wrong twice — is exactly the "replaying the conversation" beat the plan called for, compressed and shown rather than narrated. **What Doesn't Quite Work** The line "The silence had a texture to it" is the chapter's one moment of reaching slightly too hard — it's not terrible, but it's the kind of phrase that announces itself as literary in a way the rest of the prose carefully avoids. It's a minor blemish. The "face was familiar in the manner of a road sign" is a good simile — functional, slightly deflating, right for Carys's POV. The plan called for her to "replay the conversation all evening, lands on the perfect line she should have said, and is disgusted with herself." The chapter delivers this in compressed form at the end rather than as a full evening scene, which is actually the right call for pacing — the plan was a guide, not a script, and the compression works. **Dialogue Quality** The dialogue is genuinely strong. It avoids the "zinger rally" the plan warned against. Neither character wins every exchange. Dylan's charm visibly misfires — the Notting Hill bookshop anecdote, the signed photo offer — and we watch the micro-adjustments. The reveal that she knows who he is ("Dylan Vaughan... you were the villain in the one with the submarines") is delivered flatly, as planned, and the aftermath ("Right. Right. So when I came in —") captures his disorientation without overplaying it. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally engaging precisely because it doesn't push for emotion. The chemistry is present in the negative space — what she doesn't say, what he keeps trying. The detail of her taking the change and hating it is the chapter's most emotionally resonant moment, and it's handled in a single sentence. **Overall Assessment** This is a very high-quality piece of opening chapter writing. It executes a difficult brief — dialogue-dominant, chemistry through resistance, class dynamics, character establishment — with consistent skill and without the common failure modes (purple prose, thesis-statement dialogue, symmetrical callbacks). The prose is controlled, the voices are distinct, and the plan is followed faithfully while the execution feels organic rather than mechanical. Minor quibbles (one slightly reaching phrase) don't significantly diminish what is otherwise near-exemplary work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two — Returns Two days later the bell went at ten past four and Carys knew before she looked. Something about the speed of it. Nobody local came through a door like that, like an entrance. "So," said Dylan Vaughan, holding up the R.S. Thomas. "We need to talk about this." "Was it faulty?" "Was it — no. No, it was not faulty." He came to the counter and put the book down between them with a kind of ceremony. The spine was creased now, three places. Corners turned down, she noticed, which under other circumstances would have been a hanging offence. "I have a complaint. The complaint is that I've read it twice and I haven't slept properly since, and nobody warned me. *The Welsh Hill Country*. The one about the — the empty chapel one. He's writing like he hates everything he loves. The whole way through." "Yes," said Carys. "That's it? Yes?" "You wanted a refund?" "I wanted — " He stopped, and laughed, and ran a hand through his hair, and she watched the laugh decide to be honest. "All right. Fine. Full disclosure. It's a pretext. The book. A genuinely felt pretext, everything I said was true, but I had the conversation planned out walking up the hill and I can hear how planned it sounds, so." He spread his hands. "There's no filming till six. It was the shop or the hotel bar, and the hotel bar has a man in it who keeps sending drinks over and telling me I was robbed at the BAFTAs." "Were you?" "Robbed? Comprehensively. That's not the point." "You'd rather be somewhere you're not liked." "I'd rather — " he started, and the back door went, and Ffion came through with a box of Ladybird books and stopped as if she'd walked into glass. What happened then took some time. There was a noise Carys had previously heard only from kettles, and then Ffion said "Carys" twice, and "the thing with the" three times, and "I've literally seen everything you've ever" once, unfinished, and Dylan Vaughan turned to her with the full beam, the door-opening one, and Carys watched it work on someone, which was instructive. It worked very well. He asked Ffion's name and used it three times in a minute. He admired her nails — tiny daisies — like they were the Sistine ceiling. By the time the selfie happened (two takes; Ffion's hand was shaking) he had an audience and Carys had, apparently, a circus. "Oh my god, and you're — sorry, but you're so — they're all saying you're a monster, you know," Ffion said happily. "Online. Because of Marina Kessler. There's a whole — the *Mail* did 'Heartbreaker Vaughan' across two pages, and there's a TikTok edit of her crying at the airport with sad music that's got like four million — it's so unfair, you can tell it's not even from the same day, the *coats* are different — " "Ffion," said Carys. "The Ladybirds." "It's fine," said Dylan. It wasn't. Carys saw it happen — a small subsidence around the eyes, the smile staying up while something behind it stepped out of the room. It lasted half a second and then he was asking Ffion whether the edit at least used good music, and Ffion was away again, and the half-second stayed with Carys like grit in a shoe. When Ffion had been despatched to the children's section, glowing, he turned back to the counter. "So you do know the coverage," he said. "You let me stand here on Tuesday thinking you didn't know me from the postman." "I knew the films. I don't read that sort of thing." "Everyone reads that sort of thing." "I haven't the time. I've a business to run into the ground, it's a full day." She started pricing the Ladybirds, soft pencil, inside cover. "Anyway. It's none of mine, your — Marina Kessler." "No," he said. "It's not." And then, because apparently he couldn't leave it on a flat note, any note, ever: "For the record, the airport footage is from March. Different coat. Your assistant has a good eye." He browsed. He didn't browse like a customer; he browsed like a man being filmed browsing, until — and she watched this too — somewhere along the Welsh history shelf he forgot to. His shoulders came down an inch. He took out Jan Morris and stood there reading on his feet for ten solid minutes, lips moving very slightly, and the shop went quiet around him the way it went quiet around proper readers, and Carys found she'd priced the same Ladybird twice. "You're from here," she said. Not quite a question. He looked up too fast. "I'm sorry?" "Wales. Somewhere down the Valleys, I'd say, under all the — " she made a small gesture that took in the vowels, the teeth, the whole transatlantic lacquer of him. "I was born in Pontypridd," he said, and the word *Pontypridd* came out of him in pure unreconstructed Ponty, two decades of elocution falling off it like rain off a roof, and he heard it happen — she saw him hear it — and the next sentence arrived re-lacquered and laughing. "Long time ago. We moved around a lot." "Did you," said Carys, and let him think she hadn't noticed, and filed it. He bought the Jan Morris and two others she chose for him, and didn't argue with either, which told her he wasn't reading the titles. At the door he stopped. She'd learned this about him now: he couldn't leave a room without checking how he was leaving it. "Genuine question," he said. "And you can decline it. Why don't you like me? Because it's not the fame thing, I know the fame thing, the fame thing has a *flavour* and this isn't it. Everyone in that hotel likes me. The whole crew likes me. The man with the BAFTA opinions *loves* me. You look at me like a delivery you didn't order." She could have said any number of things. Later, considerably later, horizontal in the dark, she composed several, beautifully. What she actually said was: "I haven't thought about you enough to dislike you." He took it standing, nodded slowly, said "right, fair," in a voice that meant it wasn't, and went out into the grey with his collar up. Through the window she watched him pause on the front and look at the sea like it might applaud. "Carys," said Ffion, from the children's section, in tones of religious awe. "He used my *name*." "He does that," said Carys, and went back to the Ladybirds, and found she'd lost count, and the lie she'd told at the door sat where the grit had been, considerably larger, and she carried it around for the rest of the day.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of creative writing that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Dylan and Carys have genuinely distinct voices. His opening gambit with the R.S. Thomas — "a genuinely felt pretext, everything I said was true, but I had the conversation planned out walking up the hill and I can hear how planned it sounds" — is exactly right: self-aware enough to be disarming, but the self-awareness is itself a charm move, and the text knows it. His inability to leave a room without checking how he's leaving it is shown rather than stated, and the BAFTA man detail is economical and characterful. Carys's voice is well-maintained: flat, deflecting, the "business to run into the ground" line landing with the right dry weight. The "I haven't thought about you enough to dislike you" payoff works precisely because the chapter has established it as a lie, and the lie is then named without being over-explained. **Ffion** The Ffion scene is handled with real lightness. "A noise Carys had previously heard only from kettles" is a good line — not overworked, earns its small comedy. The detail about the coat being from March, the different-day footage, is clever: it gives Dylan a moment of vanity-as-accuracy that's in character, and Ffion's "good eye" observation is a nice grace note. **The Subsidence Moment** "A small subsidence around the eyes, the smile staying up while something behind it stepped out of the room" — this is the chapter's best piece of psychological observation. It's precise, it's visual, it doesn't over-explain, and "stayed with Carys like grit in a shoe" is a metaphor that earns its place because the grit image is then returned to at the end ("the lie she'd told at the door sat where the grit had been, considerably larger"). That's a controlled callback, not a mechanical one. **The Pontypridd Moment** The accent slip is executed well. "The word Pontypridd came out of him in pure unreconstructed Ponty, two decades of elocution falling off it like rain off a roof" — this is the chapter's most overtly "poetic" sentence, and it mostly works, though "falling off it like rain off a roof" is slightly generic as a simile. The key beat — "he heard it happen — she saw him hear it — and the next sentence arrived re-lacquered" — is sharp and shows the plan's intention (she lets him think she didn't notice) executed cleanly. **The Jan Morris Browsing Scene** "He browsed like a man being filmed browsing, until — and she watched this too — somewhere along the Welsh history shelf he forgot to." This is excellent. The "lips moving very slightly" detail is good. "Carys found she'd priced the same Ladybird twice" — showing her distraction without announcing it. This is the chapter doing exactly what it should. **Weaknesses and Criticisms** The ending image — "he pause on the front and look at the sea like it might applaud" — is the chapter's most forced moment. It's a thesis-statement image, almost too neat: the sea as absent audience. The plan explicitly warns against "the single legible 'tell' stamped on scene endings," and this is precisely that. It's the one place where the writing reaches for a poetic button to press rather than trusting the scene. "The whole transatlantic lacquer of him" — "lacquer" is used twice in the chapter (here and "re-lacquered"), which is either deliberate motif-building or a slight verbal tic. It works the first time; the repetition is borderline. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the pretext return, the Ffion meltdown and selfie, the tabloid coverage recitation, the subsidence moment, the Pontypridd vowel escape, the "why don't you like me" question, and the lie at the end. The plan's instruction that "her answer is rude rather than clever" is executed — "I haven't thought about you enough to dislike you" is rude, and the chapter correctly identifies it as a lie rather than a triumph. **Character Consistency** Both characters are consistent with their profiles. Dylan's compulsive room-checking, his name-use with Ffion, his inability to leave on a flat note — all present. Carys's rudeness-under-pressure, her private composition of better lines, her filing of the Pontypridd slip — all present. The plan's note that "her best lines arrive too late, in private, narrated with self-disgust" is honored. **Overall Assessment** This is high-quality literary fiction writing. The plan is followed with intelligence rather than mechanical compliance. The dialogue is genuinely good — distinct, functional, character-revealing. The psychological observation is precise. The one significant weakness is the sea-applause closing image, which is too legible and slightly self-congratulatory. There are minor quibbles (the "lacquer" repetition, the rain-off-a-roof simile being slightly generic), but these are small against the chapter's considerable achievements. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Model Output:
# Chapter Three — Weather The photographer came back on the Friday and made a camp of it. Folding stool, flask, long lens across his knees, set up by the war memorial with the patience of a heron. Carys clocked him at four and said nothing. At half five, when Dylan Vaughan had been pretending to read the same shelf of theology for twenty minutes with his back to the window, she said: "He's still there." "Who?" "Don't insult the both of us. There's tea in the back if you want to wait him out. They don't sit past seven, I shouldn't think, not in this." The back room was the shop's actual heart and she was aware of what she was admitting him to: the armchair with the horsehair coming through, her grandfather's desk, the accounts in their exercise book — which she turned face-down, not quickly enough — and the dog, who lifted his head from the rug and assessed the visitor with the flat yellow look he reserved for men. "Hello," said Dylan, to the dog, with no performance in it at all, and crouched and offered his knuckles, and waited. The dog took his time. Then he got up, turned a circle, and lay back down across the man's feet like a delivery. "Traitor," said Carys. "What's his name?" "Dylan." The grin came up like a sunrise. "You're joking. That's — " "Named seven years before I'd heard of you," she said, filling the kettle. "He came with a house clearance. Old boy out at Penbryn died and the whole library was Thomas — both Thomases, the saint and the sinner — so. Don't flatter yourself. He's named after a dead man's bookshelf." "That," said Dylan, settling into the armchair like it had been measured for him, "is somehow better." They drank tea. Rain got going against the window in earnest, the proper kind, and the shop creaked around them the way old buildings do when they're deciding to let you stay. She did the accounts, or held the pencil near them. He talked. He was good at talking — it was, she supposed, the job — but in the small room, with the dog anchoring his feet, the talk kept developing holes. "It's been a strange month," is how one of them opened. "You'd have read about it, if you read that sort of thing, which you don't, so. Marina." He turned the mug around. "We were together three years. The papers have a version. The version's wrong, but it's wrong in a useful direction, if that makes sense, so nobody's correcting it." A pause exactly long enough to matter. Then the gloss came down like a shutter: "Anyway, she's well out of it, the catering on her new job is apparently spectacular, she's sent me photos of the bread station out of spite — " "What's the unuseful direction?" said Carys. He looked at her. The shutter stayed half-down. "You don't read tabloids but you ask tabloid questions." "You brought her in. I was doing my sums." "Yeah," he said, and didn't answer, and she let it lie, and oddly that seemed to land on him harder than pressing would have. He was quiet for almost a minute, which she sensed was a personal record. Later it was his mother. "She's still in Ponty. Same house. I bought her a bungalow in Radyr once — beautiful thing, level access, the works — and she let it sit empty for a year out of politeness before she told me." He did the voice, pitch-perfect, a small fierce Valleys woman: "*Gethin, love, what am I going to do in Radyr.*" It got its laugh — Carys gave it up before she could stop herself — and then she watched him hear what he'd said. *Gethin.* He picked the mug up and put it down. "It's a name she uses," he said, lightly, lacquer going back on. "Old family thing." "Mm," said Carys, and turned a page of the accounts she wasn't doing. The rain thickened. The dog snored. And because the silence was the kind that asked to be filled, and because filling silences was his cardio, he reached over to the desk where the R.S. Thomas lay — he'd brought it with him, she noted, carried it round like a passport — and opened it at one of the turned-down corners. "Can I?" he said, already settling, already finding the voice, and read. It was, she had to admit, beautiful. Of course it was. The voice came out of him rich and grave and perfectly weighted, every line landing like a stone laid in a wall, the hill country and the empty pews and the stubborn God, all of it lit and warmed and *served*, and it was the best reading of the poem she would ever hear and it was unbearable. "Stop," she said. "Stop acting it." He stopped. The room rang a little. He looked at her over the top of the book with an expression she hadn't had from him yet — not the charm, not the wound under the charm, but something bare and briefly furious, the look of a man whose one good currency has been refused at the till. "That's the only way I know how to read," he said. "No it isn't." He held her eye. Then he looked back down at the page, and started again, and it was terrible — halting, flat, all the music stripped out, the accent loosening line by line until the hills in the poem were the hills he was actually from, and he stumbled twice and didn't fix it, and by the last lines his voice had gone somewhere effortful and narrow, and the poem stood in the room without any help from him at all. Nobody said anything. The dog sighed extravagantly. "My father could recite him by the yard," Carys said, into the quiet, surprising herself. "Chapel-raised, see. He's in a home now, Aberystwyth way. I go Tuesdays." She straightened the exercise book. "Most weeks he doesn't know me. I'm the girl from the shop." She had not said that last part aloud to anyone, ever, and she heard it leave her with a kind of distant alarm, like watching your hat go over a cliff. Dylan didn't do any of the things she braced for. No warm anecdote, no swerve into a story of his own, no joke to make it lighter. He sat with the book shut on his finger and asked: "Tuesdays. Is it the drive that's worst, or the bit in the car park before you go in?" She looked at him for a long moment. "The car park," she said. "How do you know about the car park?" "Everybody's got a car park," he said. The rain eased about seven, as predicted. The photographer had folded his stool and gone. At the door, Dylan turned his collar up, thanked her with unusual brevity, and left without checking how he was leaving the room — and that, she thought, locking up, ten minutes flat-voiced and over-still behind a closed sign, that was the most alarming thing he'd done yet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me examine it critically across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. It operates on multiple registers simultaneously — surface conversation, subtext, and the characters' internal negotiations — without ever becoming thesis-heavy or self-congratulatory. The exchange about Marina ("wrong in a useful direction") is economical and revealing. The "Gethin" slip and its immediate lacquering-over is handled with surgical precision: "He picked the mug up and put it down. 'It's a name she uses,' he said, lightly, lacquer going back on." This is showing, not telling, and it's done with a light touch. The R.S. Thomas reading sequence is the chapter's centerpiece and it earns its weight. The instruction "Stop acting it" followed by his bare, halting second reading is dramatically effective and thematically resonant — it literalizes the story's central tension (performance vs. authenticity) without announcing itself as a symbol. The plan called for this to be "worse and better" and the execution delivers: "the poem stood in the room without any help from him at all" is a fine line. The car park exchange is the chapter's emotional peak and it's handled with admirable restraint. "Everybody's got a car park" risks being a zinger but is saved by its context — it arrives after genuine vulnerability from both characters, and his not-performing in response to her disclosure is noted precisely: "No warm anecdote, no swerve into a story of his own, no joke to make it lighter." The plan specified he "just asks a second question" and the execution improves on this slightly — the question itself ("Is it the drive that's worst, or the bit in the car park before you go in?") is more specific and more penetrating than a generic second question would be. The dog as authenticator works well and isn't overplayed. "The shop's only review Dylan Vaughan trusts completely" from the plan is wisely not stated in the chapter itself — it's shown through the dog's behavior and Carys's "Traitor." The chapter's ending is excellent. "Left without checking how he was leaving the room" is a precise, character-specific observation that pays off the established characterization of Dylan's constant room-management. It's the right note to end on. WEAKNESSES AND CRITICISMS: The prose occasionally reaches for poetry in ways that don't fully earn their keep. "The shop creaked around them the way old buildings do when they're deciding to let you stay" is the most egregious example — it's the kind of atmospheric sentence that sounds lovely but is doing atmospheric work the chapter doesn't need, and the personification is slightly strained. Similarly, "like a stone laid in a wall" in the Thomas reading description is competent but not distinguished. "The grin came up like a sunrise" is a cliché that the author seems to deploy knowingly (it's the kind of thing Carys might think sardonically), but it's not clearly marked as ironic, so it reads as straight purple prose. The chapter is almost too well-executed — it follows the plan so faithfully that there are few genuine surprises beyond what the plan promised. The "everybody's got a car park" line, while effective, does approach the zinger territory the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. It's a good line, but it's a *good line* in a way that's slightly too polished for a man who's supposedly having his performance stripped away. The mother anecdote and the "Gethin" slip feel slightly compressed — the plan called for him to "visibly regret the punchline" and while the text shows him covering, the regret is more implied than dramatized. The accounts exercise book being turned "face-down, not quickly enough" is a nice detail from the plan that lands well in execution. The chapter follows its plan almost point-for-point, which is both a strength (the plan was good) and a slight limitation (there's little that surprises beyond the plan's parameters). The writing is consistently at a high level without quite reaching the transcendent moments that would justify a near-perfect score. OVERALL: This is skilled, disciplined literary fiction with strong dialogue, well-managed subtext, and characters who behave consistently with their profiles. Its weaknesses are minor — occasional over-reach in the prose, one or two lines that are slightly too polished for the moment — but they don't significantly undermine a chapter that largely delivers on ambitious intentions. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Model Output:
# Chapter Four — Tuesday He turned up the following Tuesday at half one, as she was bringing the sign in. "You're closing? It's Tuesday." "That's why I'm closing." "Aberystwyth," he said, remembering, and then stood there on the pavement while a thought visibly arrived and surprised him. "I'll drive you." "You will not." "I've nothing till tomorrow. They're shooting interiors at the manor all day, none of mine. And you'll have noticed I'm not insured to be anywhere a man with a stool can see me." He nodded at the car parked along the front, a discreet German thing the production had given him. "It's wasted on the hotel car park. Come on. I drive well. I did three months of pursuit training for *Undertow* and retained almost none of it." She meant to say no. The bus took an hour and forty with the change at Aberaeron, and it was raining again, and these were the reasons she gave herself in the passenger seat, arms folded, while Llanmorwen fell away behind them. He did drive well. He drove like he did most things, with a watchful ease that was nine-tenths habit, and for the first twenty minutes he filled the car the way he filled rooms — stories, mostly, good ones, an anecdote about a horse on a Croatian set that made her laugh twice, properly, the loud laugh, and she caught him cataloguing the laugh like a review. "Don't do that," she said. "Do what?" "Bank it." A silence. The wipers went. Then he said, in a different register, "You know you're quite frightening," and turned the radio on, and turned it off again four seconds later because apparently silence with her in it had become preferable to Ken Bruce, which she declined to examine. Somewhere past Llanrhystud she said, because the car made it easier, both of them facing the road: "Why does it bother you. One bookshop owner in a town of eight hundred. You've got the whole hotel. You've a *fan* by the war memorial with a flask." He didn't answer at once, which from him was an event. "Honest answer? I don't know. It's like — " his hands adjusted on the wheel — "everywhere I go, there's a sound. Has been for twenty years. Like a — hum. The room liking you. You stop hearing it as a sound, it's just the temperature." He glanced at her. "Your shop's the first place I've stood in since I don't know when where the hum's off. And I came out the first day rattled, genuinely, asking myself what's wrong with her — and about the third visit I clocked it. It's quiet in there. That's all it is. I can hear myself think and I don't much like the company, but it's quiet." "So I'm a soundproofed room." "You're — " he started, and the Ponty came up through it, and this time he didn't sand it back down. "I sat in that armchair last week and read a poem badly for the first time since 1994 and I've thought about it every day since. Make of that what you want. I haven't managed to." She looked out the window at the wet sheep and made nothing of it, loudly, all the way to the turning. — Hafan y Môr smelled the way it always smelled, of lunch and lilies and the cheerfulness of staff. Her father was in the dayroom window seat, a rug on his knees, looking at the rain like it owed him money. "Now then," said Dylan, before she'd worked out what to do with him, and pulled a chair round, and was in. It was extraordinary and she hated it slightly. Twenty minutes of full beam. He didn't talk down — that was the thing, he pitched it perfectly, rugby and chapel and "you'd know the Graig, then," and her father, who hadn't strung four sentences together for her since the spring, *lit*. Laughed. Corrected Dylan's geography of Pontypridd with relish. Got him muddled with a curate from 1971 and was charmingly allowed to. At one point Emrys recited four lines of Williams Parry to him, unprompted, in Welsh, word-perfect, and Dylan sat with his head bowed slightly like a man at a service, and Carys had to go and look hard at the noticeboard, which advertised a harpist for Friday. When they left, her father caught her hand at the door — a thing he did some weeks — and said, kindly, conspiratorially, "Thank you for coming, bach. Tell the girl from the shop the order's in." "I will," said Carys. "Bye now, Dad." In the car park she stopped by the passenger door and didn't get in. Rain hung about not committing itself. "What was that," she said, over the roof. "In there. Because I've been coming three years and I get fifteen minutes of weather and silence, and you walk in and he's — " her voice did something and she took it back under control. "So I want to know what I watched. Was that kind, or was that a performance? Because I can't tell, and it matters to me, and I think you're the one person who genuinely can't answer the question." He stood with his hand on the car roof and the rain starting in his hair, and for once didn't reach for anything. "It's not either/or," he said. "That's the bit nobody — " and stopped. She waited. He looked at the hills behind the home as if the end of the sentence were up there somewhere. "He had a good hour. I don't know what it cost him or me or whether it was true all the way through. I never know. You're asking me the only question I — ask me anything else. Ask me about the BAFTAs." "Gethin," she said. First time she'd used it. It went through him visibly, like the draught from a door. He came round the car — afterwards she couldn't have said which of them moved first, the geometry of it stayed unrecoverable — and they were close, suddenly, in the wet car park of her father's care home, his hand somewhere near her elbow not quite landing, her face turned up at an angle she had not authorised, and the whole thing hung there for one entire second — — and her nerve went, or his did, or both, and she said "right, visiting's over," in a voice like a fire door, and got in the car. They drove the first ten minutes in a silence with a fortnight's conversation packed into it. Near Llanon his phone went through the speakers — SASKIA — and he killed it with a thumb without looking, and she watched him not look, and put it away to think about later. On the hill above the harbour, a parked hatchback neither of them noticed had its window down two inches, in the rain, which should have told them something.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Characterization and Voice** Both characters are rendered with precision and consistency. Dylan's voice carries the planned "transatlantic with Welsh leaking through" quality — the Ponty vowels surfacing at the emotional moment ("the Ponty came up through it, and this time he didn't sand it back down") is handled with restraint rather than fanfare. His speech about the "hum" of rooms liking him is the closest he gets to self-knowledge all chapter, and crucially it's incomplete and slightly self-serving even in its honesty — he frames Carys as a soundproofed room before catching himself. This is exactly the character profile's instruction that his self-awareness is "real but always partial and slightly misaimed." Carys's interiority is rendered through action and restraint rather than exposition. "She looked out the window at the wet sheep and made nothing of it, loudly" is a superb line — the adverb "loudly" applied to silence is genuinely inventive and earns its place. Her voice at the care home ("right, visiting's over," in a voice like a fire door) is perfectly calibrated: the fire door simile is apt, not overwrought, and the line itself is exactly the graceless deflection the character profile promises. **The Care Home Scene** This is the chapter's centerpiece and it's handled with real skill. The plan called for Carys being unable to tell whether Dylan's performance with her father was kindness or performance, and the scene delivers this ambiguity without resolving it. Dylan's twenty minutes of "full beam" is shown through specific, earned details: "rugby and chapel and 'you'd know the Graig, then,'" the Williams Parry recitation with Dylan sitting "with his head bowed slightly like a man at a service." These are concrete, not generic. The father calling Carys "the girl from the shop" — a detail planted in the character profiles — lands with quiet devastation precisely because it's not underlined. Dylan's answer to her question — "It's not either/or... That's the bit nobody —" — is the planned incomplete self-knowledge, and it works because the incompleteness is structural, not just a trailing ellipsis. He genuinely cannot finish the sentence. **The Almost-Kiss** The plan called for a "scuffed" almost-kiss, "mistimed, interrupted by nothing but their own nerve failing," and the execution is faithful and effective. "The geometry of it stayed unrecoverable" is a strong line. The fire door line as deflection is exactly right. What's particularly good is that neither character is made to look foolish or pathetic — the moment is genuinely charged and the retreat is mutual and human. **Dialogue Quality** The dialogue is largely excellent. The opening exchange ("You're closing? It's Tuesday." / "That's why I'm closing.") is economical and character-revealing. The "Don't do that" / "Do what?" / "Bank it" exchange is sharp and shows rather than tells. The car conversation about the "hum" is the chapter's most ambitious dialogue and mostly succeeds — it risks being too articulate for a man who supposedly can't access his own feelings, but the plan does allow him moments of genuine insight, and the speech is hedged enough ("I don't know," "I never know") to stay credible. **Weaknesses** A few moments approach the over-written. "A silence with a fortnight's conversation packed into it" is slightly too neat — it tells us the silence is meaningful rather than trusting the reader to feel it. "Rain hung about not committing itself" is charming but slightly whimsical in a chapter that otherwise earns its effects. The final paragraph — "a parked hatchback neither of them noticed had its window down two inches, in the rain, which should have told them something" — is the chapter's weakest moment. The "which should have told them something" is a narratorial wink that slightly condescends to the reader and breaks the close-third intimacy maintained throughout. It's the kind of foreshadowing that announces itself. The horse anecdote that makes her laugh "twice, properly, the loud laugh" is told rather than shown — we're told it was good rather than experiencing it. This is a minor but real Tell-Don't-Show failure. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully: the drive, the care home scene with Emrys lighting up, the "too good" quality Carys can't parse, the incomplete self-knowledge speech, the almost-kiss scuffed by nerve failure, the phone call killed without looking. The unremarked photo is planted exactly as planned. The plan's instruction that "somewhere on the journey, unremarked, a phone takes a picture" is executed with the hatchback — though the "which should have told them something" undermines the "unremarked" quality. **Overall Assessment** This is high-quality literary fiction with a strong command of character, dialogue, and scene construction. The emotional beats are earned rather than asserted. The chapter's flaws are minor: one weak closing note, one or two moments of telling rather than showing, and occasional over-neat formulations. But the care home scene in particular is genuinely moving, the dialogue is distinctive and character-specific, and the almost-kiss is handled with the right combination of charge and awkwardness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# Chapter Five — Stock The Penbryn cousins had finally cleared the rest of the old boy's house, and the boxes arrived Thursday — fourteen of them, books on every subject including, mysteriously, three on bee-keeping and a Welsh-Italian dictionary. Carys was on her knees in the middle aisle at half six, shop locked, when the knocking started. She let him in mostly to stop the knocking. He had a bottle under his arm. "I saw the light on. I come bearing — " he checked the label — "whatever was best in the Spar. It's this or my per diem dies in the hotel bar with the BAFTA man." "That's a six-pound Merlot." "It's a *seven*-pound Merlot, there was a promotion." He looked at the boxes, the dust, the chaos. "What's all this?" "Stock. You can hold things or you can leave." He took his coat off, which she had not strictly authorised, and rolled his sleeves, which seemed excessive, and for the next hour he was the worst shop assistant in the history of the trade. He couldn't pass a box without reading half of it. He shelved a Dorothy Sayers under S-for-Sayers in among the Welsh history because he'd stopped listening, and put the bee-keeping books face-out "because they deserve it," and got distracted for ten full minutes by a 1958 guide to courtship etiquette, reading the worst bits aloud in the voice of a Pathé announcer until she was laughing too hard to price anything, sat back on her heels in the dust with the loud laugh going entirely unpoliced. She got the Penderyn out at some point — the Merlot having been tried and sentenced — and they drank it out of the two mugs that didn't match, and the shop went warm around them in the way it did for almost nobody. "Right," he said, from the floor, back against the biography shelf, dog across his shins. "Game. Books you'd be buried with. Three. Go." "Buried with. What good are they to me buried?" "It's not a logistics question, Carys." "The Mabinogion. The R.S., obviously. And — " she considered, mug at her chin — "the Sayers you just misfiled, *Gaudy Night*, and if you tell anyone that I'll deny it to the grave we're apparently furnishing." "Why deny it?" "It's a romance." "It's a — I've read it, it's a detective — " "It's a romance," she said, "wearing a detective novel as a coat. Like you wearing that accent. Your go. Three. And if one of them's a script I'm taking the whisky back." He laughed — there was a particular laugh she got out of him now, lower than the public one, like a door into a back room — and then didn't answer for a while, turning the mug. "There was a teacher," he said. "Mr Probert. Pontypridd. Did *Under Milk Wood* with us because he needed boys who'd stand still, and I couldn't, particularly, but I could be loud. And there's a bit — first voice, the beginning, the *starless and bible-black* bit — and I had it by heart inside a week. Couldn't tell you my times tables to this day. And I remember standing in that hall at the dress, fourteen years old, doing it, and the whole room went — " he made a small gesture, a settling. "Quiet. The good quiet. The kind that's pointed at you. And I can honestly say I have spent thirty years and a career and two relationships and a great deal of other people's money trying to get back into that hall." He drank. "Anyway. That's book one. The other two are whatever's nearest, it won't matter, I'll be dead." He said it all flatly, without shine, no eye contact, no checking — that was the thing she noticed, sitting in the dust with her mug: he told it to the middle distance and let it lie there, and didn't once look over to see how it had landed. From him that was practically sworn testimony. "Gareth," she said, by way of exchange, because apparently that was the currency tonight. "The fiancé. Everyone in town will tell you he left me for London, and I let them say it because it's tidier. He asked me to come. Three times. Last time was a Thursday, he had the train tickets in his actual hand, and I stood in this exact aisle and told him the shop couldn't run itself." She looked at the shelves. "It could've, mind. Dad was still managing then. I just — wanted this. And wanting this felt like losing, the way I was brought up, so I made it a duty instead and let Gareth be the villain. He sends a Christmas card. His wife does the writing in it." "Why are you telling me that?" "Because you told me about the hall," she said, "and don't make me regret — " He kissed her. Or she kissed him — the geometry went unrecoverable again, they were both reaching across the same box of bee-keeping books, and it was clumsy, off-centre, her glasses came down out of her hair and into the way, his elbow caught the etiquette guide and sent it flapping into the dark like a shot bird. They got it wrong and then less wrong. The dog, displaced, made a noise like a dowager. They broke apart. Neither of them said anything clever. That was what she kept afterwards, turning it over: the great Dylan Vaughan, a man with three writers on retainer somewhere, looked at her with his mouth open and said: "Aye," she said, no steadier. And then — and this was the part that undid her, more than the kiss, more than the hall — they went back to shelving. Just carried on, the two of them, passing books, hands not quite careful about touching, the shop lamplit and dusty and different, everything in the same place and nothing where it had been. He stayed till eleven. He read out more of the etiquette guide. *A gentleman never lingers at the door of a lady's establishment*, it said, and he lingered at the door of her establishment for some time. His phone lay face-down on the counter the whole evening, and from nine o'clock it lit up at intervals, buzzing against the wood like something trapped — SASKIA, SASKIA, SASKIA — and he never once turned it over. She noticed, though. Locking up, glasses crooked, smiling at the till like a fool: she noticed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of romantic fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me work through its qualities and failings systematically. **Characterization and Voice** Both characters are rendered with precision and consistency. Dylan's voice carries the promised Welsh vowels leaking through ("Aye"), the self-deprecating charm, and crucially the moment of genuine disclosure about Mr. Probert and the hall — told "to the middle distance," without checking for the review, which the narrative correctly identifies as "practically sworn testimony." This is the character plan executed well: the tell is behavioral, not announced. Carys's reciprocal disclosure about Gareth is equally well-handled — she frames it as currency exchange ("because you told me about the hall"), which is exactly her character: transactional even in vulnerability, self-aware about the transaction, and still doing it anyway. The detail that she "let Gareth be the villain" and made wanting-to-stay into duty is consistent with her profile's core flaw (converting self-protection into a moral position). Ffion is absent here, which is appropriate to the chapter's intimacy. The dog's cameo ("made a noise like a dowager") is perfectly calibrated — comic without being precious. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The banter about the six/seven-pound Merlot, the burial books game, the "it's a romance wearing a detective novel as a coat — like you wearing that accent" exchange — these feel genuinely earned rather than zinger-rally. The plan specifically warned against "every line wins" dialogue, and the chapter largely avoids this. The Gaudy Night exchange is particularly good: it reveals character (her defensiveness about sentiment, his genuine readerly engagement) while moving naturally. The etiquette guide bit — "A gentleman never lingers at the door of a lady's establishment" — is a light touch that earns its place without becoming a thesis statement. The kiss scene's aftermath is handled with real discipline: "the great Dylan Vaughan, a man with three writers on retainer somewhere, looked at her with his mouth open and said: [blank]." The blank is a genuine creative choice, not a cop-out — it enacts the silence rather than describing it. "Aye" as her response is perfect: Welsh, unguarded, unpolished. **The Plan's Execution** The chapter plan called for: warmth, dialogue games, him shelving badly, her laughing unpoliced, a true disclosure from him with no shine, the kiss mid-sentence and clumsy, going back to shelving with "the lights different," and his phone lighting up ignored by him but noticed by her. Every single one of these beats is present and executed. The plan's specific instruction — "neither of them says anything clever after it" — is honored. The phone detail (SASKIA, SASKIA, SASKIA) lands the chapter's final note of foreboding without overplaying it. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. "The shop went warm around them in the way it did for almost nobody" is quietly excellent — it earns its sentiment through understatement. "Like a door into a back room" for the private laugh is a good metaphor, grounded and specific. "The etiquette guide and sent it flapping into the dark like a shot bird" is vivid without being overwrought. "Everything in the same place and nothing where it had been" is the chapter's most overtly "literary" line and it earns it — it's not decorative, it's the emotional truth of the scene stated plainly. **Weaknesses** The chapter is not without flaws. "The shop went warm around them in the way it did for almost nobody" is good, but "the way it did for almost nobody" is slightly self-congratulatory — it tells us the shop's warmth is rare rather than fully trusting the scene to show it. The "shot bird" simile for the etiquette guide is vivid but slightly overworked for what is essentially a comic beat. The phrase "practically sworn testimony" in the narration is a touch too clever — it's Carys's voice bleeding into the narration in a way that's mostly earned but slightly strained. The Gareth disclosure, while well-executed, is perhaps slightly too neat in its symmetry with Dylan's hall story — the "currency" framing helps, but there's a risk of the chapter feeling too tidily structured in its emotional exchanges. The plan warned against "symmetrical callbacks clicking shut," and this doesn't quite violate that, but it approaches it. The ending — her noticing the phone while smiling at the till "like a fool" — is the chapter's one moment of slight over-signaling. The reader has already registered the phone's significance; the final beat of her noticing while smiling is good, but "smiling at the till like a fool" is a small concession to sentimentality that the rest of the chapter earns its way past. **Overall** This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with genuine craft. The warmth is earned, the lightness is well-deployed, the characters are consistent, the dialogue is strong, and the emotional beats land without being announced. It's not a masterpiece — there are small moments of over-writing and the structural symmetry of the disclosure exchange is slightly too clean — but it's accomplished, disciplined work that honors a sophisticated plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six — Statement Delyth from the post office rang at ten past eight, which is how Carys learned, because Delyth's voice had the particular kindness people save for telling you the cat's been hit. "You haven't seen, then. Don't look on an empty stomach, love." She looked on an empty stomach. The big one was the car park at Hafan y Môr — the two of them by the passenger door, his hand near her elbow, her face at that unauthorised angle, the whole second of it frozen and captioned. There was a second shot of them on the seafront from a distance, and a third of the shop front, sign and all, PRITCHARD'S BOOKS legible above the door her grandfather had painted. DYLAN'S VALLEY GIRL, said one. Another had found her age — they'd got it right, which was somehow worse — and a third had gone with *the dowdy divorcee who's caught the heartbreaker's eye*, which was wrong on two counts and didn't care. There was a paragraph on the shop. *Failing*, it said. *Cluttered*. One of them had a quote from "a local" about the rates arrears, and that one she had to put the phone down after, and stand at the sink for a bit. The comments she did not read, except that she read some of the comments. The kind ones were about how brave he was. The others were about her cardigan, her hair, what men like him actually wanted from women like her, and the phrase *bookshop hag* which a number of strangers had enjoyed independently arriving at by nine a.m. The phone started at half eight and didn't stop. Two papers, a radio producer, a woman from a daytime programme who said the word "celebrate" four times. By ten there were two photographers on the front, openly, lenses on the door like it owed them money. Ffion arrived at eleven through the back, white as the till roll, holding her phone out in front of her like evidence at an inquest. "It was me," she said, and her face came apart. "The selfie. From the first week — I tagged the shop, I had the location on, I didn't — they trawl, apparently, there's people whose whole job — forty-three followers, Carys, one of them's my *nan* — " "Ffion. Stop." "I'm so — " "I forgave you the moment you told me," Carys said, "so you can stop crying into the Penguin Classics, they're foxed enough as it is. You took a photo of a famous man in a shop. The rest of it isn't yours. Put the kettle on and stay off the front." It came out rough and final and Ffion, after a wet moment, obeyed, and Carys stood in her own shop listening to the phone go and thought: well. There it was. Forgiveness was apparently a thing she could do in one sentence when the person stood in front of her was seventeen and sorry. Worth remembering. She didn't expect to need it for anyone else. The statement went up at noon. Ffion found it; Ffion found everything. It was from his people, but it was him — that was what stopped her breath a little, reading it on Ffion's cracked screen at the counter. The cadence was his exactly. The warmth, the self-deprecating turn in the second line, the joke placed where he placed jokes. *Dylan is grateful for the warmth shown to him while filming in west Wales,* it said, *and dismayed that a private visit to a local business has been intruded upon. He'd ask the press to remember that not everyone in a photograph has signed up for the attention that follows him around.* Then a graceful sentence about Marina Kessler, *for whom he has nothing but affection and respect*, and a line about the film, and out. She read it three times. A local business. Not everyone in a photograph. She was scenery. Three weeks, the back room, the car park, the kiss over the bee-keeping books — and the official record, drafted in his voice and signed off by his thumb, made her a business he'd visited. Marina got a name. Wales got warmth. Carys got *intruded upon*, like a hedge. He came in through the back at half twelve, while the kettle was still hot, photographers covered at the front, and he came in already talking, already managing, his face arranged into the version of concern that came with a plan attached. "Okay — I know, I've seen it all, I'm so sorry, it's vile, the cardigan stuff alone, I've got people on it. Listen — it's handled. Genuinely. Saskia's good, she's the best, we kill the location angle, nobody confirms anything, and there's a thing tomorrow we can feed them that moves the whole circus to London. You won't have to do anything. You won't have to be part of any of this. I can keep you out of it — completely — by the weekend it's like you never — " "Like I never," said Carys. "— happened, in their — not in *mine*, obviously, I mean the coverage — " He heard it going wrong and reached for more charm, which was the reflex, which was the whole of him. "Carys. I'm protecting you. This is me protecting you." "I read the statement." "The statement's *good*. The statement's the best version of — Saskia drafted it, I signed it off from the unit, I had about four minutes between setups — " "It's in your voice."
